Auth material is embedded in EVERY generated URL as query items (SubsonicAuthorization.queryItems): token mode sends t=MD5(password+tokenSalt)&s=salt with a per-service-instance random salt; plain mode sends p=<raw password>. authMode starts as .token and is permanently flipped to .plain when a decode failure looks auth-related (Subsonic codes 40/41, HTTP 401/403, or message keyword heuristics in shouldFallbackToPlainAuth) — the fallback retry bypasses the coalescer.

Sensitive-data crossing: artworkTemplateURL() bakes the current auth query params (token or plain password) into artwork URL strings stored inside Catalog models, and PlaybackInfo.playbackURL likewise embeds auth; these strings flow into app layers (PlaybackController, PlaylistStore, download records, Kingfisher caches) and can be persisted there, while tokenSalt is regenerated per service instance — persisted URLs go stale across launches and, in plain mode, contain the raw password.

Request coalescing: concurrent identical cacheable requests share one detached Task via the RequestCoalescer actor keyed by the same cache key; the auth-fallback retry and non-cacheable requests skip coalescing.

Search enrichment: for song-type searches (prefetchSongMetadata, default on), enrichSongSearchResponse fans out a getSong detail fetch per result in a withTaskGroup, rebuilding the list in original order; any per-song failure SILENTLY keeps the thinner search row (no logging) — a quiet-degradation point for an integrity audit.
Pagination is synthesized, not server-provided: mapSearchResponse fabricates `next: "offset:N"` whenever the returned count >= limit; ResourceList href/next are otherwise nil for Subsonic data.

Mapping lossiness worth auditing: mapSong hardcodes hasLyrics=true / hasTimeSyncedLyrics=false, converts duration seconds -> durationInMillis (*1000), uses year as releaseDate string, and falls back to empty strings for missing artist/album ids in PlaybackInfo (artistID/albumID default ""); SubsonicLyricsPayload silently degrades to empty-string lyrics on unrecognized shapes; getAlbum/getSong with a missing payload return empty-data Responses rather than throwing (only playback(id:) throws invalidResponse).

Identity semantics: CatalogSong/CatalogAlbum/CatalogArtist equality and hashing use id(+type) only — attribute changes (e.g. enriched metadata, refreshed artwork URLs with new auth tokens) are invisible to Equatable/Hashable consumers such as diffable data source snapshots in the app.
perform() short-circuits with a localized transportFailed error when baseURL.host == "example.com" — a sentinel meaning no server configured, owned by the app-side configuration.
result.summary: SubsonicClientKit is a self-contained, dependency-free SwiftPM package (swift-tools 6.2, iOS 15/macOS 12) that implements the Subsonic REST API (v1.16.1, client name "museamp") behind one central type: `SubsonicMusicService`, a Sendable final class offering async `ping()`, `search()`, `album(id:)`, `song(id:)`, `playback(id:)`, and `lyrics(id:)`. Every call funnels through a single generic `perform(_:cache:decode:)` pipeline that builds URLs via the `SubsonicEndpoint` enum (which owns action names, query items, and per-endpoint `cacheIdentifier` strings), unwraps the `subsonic-response` JSON envelope, and maps raw `Subsonic*` DTOs (Models/Subsonic/, with lossy string/int decoding for inconsistent servers) into Apple-Music-shaped `Catalog*` public models (Models/, with attributes/relationships split per file). State ownership: the service owns five per-type in-memory `ResponseCache` actors (TTL hardcoded to infinity, 7-day stale window for failure fallback), a `RequestCoalescer` actor that dedupes concurrent identical requests, an NSLock-guarded `authMode` that auto-falls back from MD5-token auth to plain-password auth on auth-shaped failures, and an optional injected `CacheStorageProvider` for versioned raw-Data disk caching (currently nil in the app — only package tests exercise it). Authentication credentials are embedded as query parameters in every URL the package emits, including artwork template URL strings stored inside Catalog models and `PlaybackInfo.playbackURL`, which then cross into the app's Backend layers. Song-type searches optionally fan out per-song getSong enrichment via a task group, silently falling back to the thinner row on error. The only app-side consumer is `MuseAmp/Backend/API/APIClient.swift` (plus tvOS bootstrap), which constructs and rebuilds the service on configuration change; the package itself does no logging and no UIKit, communicating failures purely through the localized `APIError` enum.

result.scope: tvOS shell target (lifecycle, root state machine, thin adapters, symlinked Sync) — MuseAmpTV/
result.summary: MuseAmpTV is a deliberately thin tvOS receiver shell: its only product feature is receiving a playlist from an iPhone over LAN (Bonjour + QR pairing) and playing it back full-screen with lyrics. Entry is main.swift (MainActor.assumeIsolated + UIApplicationMain, no @main) -> TVAppDelegate -> TVSceneDelegate, which boots through TVBootProgressController (synchronous DatabaseManager init off-main, then restoreSessionStateAtLaunch) before crossfading to TVRootViewController. TVAppContext (split into TVAppContext.swift, +Bootstrap, +Session) is the TV analogue of AppEnvironment and owns all shared dependencies: APIClient, MusicLibraryDatabase, PlaylistStore, DownloadStore, PlaybackController, LyricsService, AudioFileImporter, SyncTransferSession, and TVPlaylistSessionStore, plus the in-memory currentSessionManifest and one-shot pending alerts. Almost the entire Backend/ tree (API, Downloads, Library, Logging, Lyrics, Playback, Playlist, Supplement, Sync) plus Application/AppPreferences.swift are relative symlinks into MuseAmp/ — far broader than the Sync-only mirroring AGENTS.md documents — so the only TV-native backend code is Backend/Models/ (four small AMTV-prefixed flow/content types) and Backend/Sync/TVSessionStateAdapter.swift. TVSessionStateAdapter (714 lines) is the central state machine: a private TransferPhase enum (waiting/connecting/receiving/importing/completed/disconnected/failed) is combined with the playback snapshot and session manifest into a computed AMTVLibrarySessionState, which TVRootViewController collapses to AMTVRootFlowState and renders by toggling isHidden on three pre-allocated surfaces (TVQRPairingView, TVTransferProgressView, TVNowPlayingController) with snapshot crossfades and tvOS focus updates. TVNowPlayingController hosts a split artwork/transport + read-only lyric timeline view, observing $snapshot and playbackTimeSubject, with a local TVNowPlayingLyricsCoordinator and a TV-specific LRC parser/timeline (TVLyricParser, TVLyricTimeline) feeding TVLyricTimelineView. State ownership: transfer phase lives in TVSessionStateAdapter, session manifest + alerts in TVAppContext, playback in the shared PlaybackController; views are stateless renderers driven by a single reloadContent() pass.
conventions: TV-native types are prefixed `TV`; cross-cutting flow/content models are prefixed `AMTV` (AMTVRootFlowState, AMTVLibrarySessionState, AMTVUploadWaitingContent, AMTVReceivingTracksContent) and live in Backend/Models/ even though they are UI content payloads

Responsibility-split extension files mirror the iOS AppEnvironment pattern: TVAppContext.swift + TVAppContext+Bootstrap.swift + TVAppContext+Session.swift; large logic also split via `private extension` blocks within one file (TVSessionStateAdapter)

Most of MuseAmpTV/Backend/* and Application/AppPreferences.swift are relative symlinks into MuseAmp/ (../../../MuseAmp/Backend/...); any new shared Sync/backend file needs a matching symlink or the TV target fails to compile — note actual symlink coverage (API, Downloads, Library, Logging, Lyrics, Playback, Playlist, Supplement, Sync) is much broader than the Sync-only mirroring AGENTS.md describes

Single source of truth state machine: private TransferPhase enum in TVSessionStateAdapter -> computed `sessionState` -> derived `rootFlowState`; the root VC re-renders everything through one idempotent reloadContent() that toggles isHidden on pre-allocated subviews (no view creation/destruction during state changes)

Callbacks are non-optional with empty defaults (`var onStateChanged: () -> Void = {}`, `var onBootComplete: (TVAppContext) -> Void = { _ in }`); cooldowns/throttles use `Date = .distantPast` (scrubCommitCooldownDeadline, lastUserInteractionDate) — matches repo Property Rules

Combine: observe `$snapshot` with `.receive(on: DispatchQueue.main)` + `[weak self]` + single `cancellables: Set<AnyCancellable>`; high-frequency time via `playbackTimeSubject`; transfer progress is delivered via plain closures, not Combine

tvOS uses plain UIAlertController (not the AlertController package used on iOS), and a local lightweight `Interface` enum (MuseAmpTV/Interface/Common/Interface.swift) with only `animate`/`smoothSpringAnimate` instead of MuseAmpInterfaceKit's full wrapper set; one direct UIView.transition exists in TVSceneDelegate's window-root swap (manually passing .beginFromCurrentState/.allowUserInteraction)
DEVIATION from repo View Lifecycle Rules: TVLyricTimelineView uses classic UITableViewDataSource/UITableViewDelegate, not a diffable data source

Logging via AppLog with method-name-prefixed key=value messages (e.g. `receiveTransfer manifest entries=...`), user-content wrapped in sanitizedLogText(); every silent catch logs via AppLog.error/.warning
Parsing/constants types are `nonisolated` value types (TVLyricParser, TVLyricTimeline, TVNowPlayingLayout, TVPlaylistSessionStore); UI classes are `@MainActor final class`

guard/early-return style throughout; `@available(*, unavailable) required init?(coder:) { fatalError() }` on every programmatic view/VC; tvOS focus driven by preferredFocusEnvironments + setNeedsFocusUpdate/updateFocusIfNeeded; Menu-button intercepts in pressesBegan
All user-facing strings via String(localized:) backed by MuseAmpTV/Resources/Localizable.xcstrings; views configured via static content structs (configure(content:)) rather than reaching into context
dataFlows: Library lives under Caches: TVAppContext+Bootstrap.makeLibraryBaseDirectory() = Caches/TVOfflineLibrary — the OS may purge it at any time; integrity is re-checked at every launch by TVPlaylistSessionStore.validate(), whose 'Apple TV cleaned part of the transferred playlist' paths exist precisely for this

Receiver identity: TVAppContext+Session generates a static per-process 6-digit pairing code + Bonjour token (Int.random / SyncBonjourIdentity.makeToken); SyncReceiverHandshakeInfo is JSON-encoded (sortedKeys), base64'd into a `museamp://tv?data=...` QR payload rendered by TVQRPairingView — these values never persist across launches

Transfer ingest pipeline (TVSessionStateAdapter.receiveTransfer): authenticate(endpoint:password=pairingCode) -> fetchManifest (SyncManifest with entries + SyncPlaylistSession) -> missingEntries diff against local library -> downloadEntries (audio files to disk, progress closures) -> importDownloadedFiles via AudioFileImporter into MusicLibraryDatabase (WCDB-backed, via MuseAmpDatabaseKit) -> completeTransferredPlaylistSession persists TVPlaylistSessionManifest JSON

Session manifest persistence: TVPlaylistSessionStore writes/reads/atomically-replaces tv-playlist-session.json in the library base dir; validate() cross-checks manifest counts (expectedTrackCount, expectedUniqueTrackCount, orderedTrackIDs.count) against DB allTracks() count AND on-disk audio file existence per track — any mismatch invalidates the whole session and triggers clearSessionLibrary()

Resume-after-disconnect state is in-memory only: TransferPhase.disconnected carries endpoint, auth token, manifest, completedEntryTrackIDs, downloadedURLs; completed-track IDs are recovered from file URLs via deletingPathExtension().lastPathComponent (filename == trackID assumption) — lost entirely if the app relaunches mid-recovery; disconnect heuristic = >3 failures AND >50% failure ratio

Playback identity transformation: AudioTrackRecord -> PlaybackTrack with synthetic id `tv-session|<sessionID>|<index>|<trackID>` (TVPlaylistSessionManifest.playbackItemIdentifier), allowing duplicate tracks in one queue; consumers (lyrics lookup in TVNowPlayingController/coordinator) recover the catalog trackID by splitting on '|' and taking the last component — a fragile inverse mapping crossing Interface -> Backend/Lyrics

Playback persistence: PlaybackController.persistPlaybackState() called from TVSceneDelegate on resignActive/background/disconnect (PlaybackSessionStore, symlinked from iOS); restoreSessionStateAtLaunch tries restorePersistedPlaybackIfNeeded(allowAutoPlay: false) first, falls back to replaying the session from the manifest, and forces repeatMode(.queue)

Launch reconciliation: validate()==.missing with stray DB tracks -> clearSessionLibrary() wipes everything (stop playback, stopAll sync, removeAllStoredSongs, clear session JSON); .invalid -> pending alert + wipe; pending alerts are one-shot in-memory (takePendingSessionAlert consumes and trims)

Queue-exhaustion auto-restart: TVRootViewController.bindPlayback watches snapshot; when playback was active and goes fully idle/empty while a session manifest exists, it re-calls playCurrentSession() and on failure clears the whole session library — a UI-layer trigger for destructive backend cleanup

Remote dependencies: RuntimeDependencies (TVAppContext+Bootstrap) routes DatabaseManager's download-URL resolution and lyrics through the shared APIClient (apiClient.playback(id:)/lyrics(id:)), fetches artwork bytes via URLSession, and reconstructs track metadata from file paths (albumID = first path segment, trackID = filename sans extension) via EmbeddedMetadataReader — same filename-as-ID coupling as the resume path

Lyrics caching layers: LyricsService disk/db cache (lyricsCacheStore from DatabaseManager) -> TVNowPlayingLyricsCoordinator in-memory [trackID: String] cache -> persistLyricsIfDownloaded writes fetched lyrics back; stale-application guarded by shouldApplyLoadedLyrics comparing current playing catalog ID

Bonjour advertise/browse lifecycle: startReceiverAvailability() advertises the receiver service and browses for senders only when phase is waiting/completed/failed AND no session manifest exists; advertising stops during connecting/receiving/importing/disconnected — state crossing Backend/Sync (SyncBonjourAdvertiser, SyncTransferSession) and the UI adapter

result.scope: Playlist, Search, Settings, Sync feature UI (MuseAmp/Interface/Playlist, MuseAmp/Interface/Search, MuseAmp/Interface/Settings, MuseAmp/Interface/Sync)
result.summary: This scope holds four feature-owned UIKit areas of the iOS app target, all programmatic UIKit with no storyboards. Playlist/ contains the playlist library list (PlaylistViewController + Editing/Search/Table extensions, diffable table over PlaylistStore data with sort options, multi-select editing, and local fuzzy search), the playlist detail screen (PlaylistDetailViewController + Actions/Artwork/Menu extensions, with drag-export, reorder/remove via a custom diffable data source subclass, generated/custom cover artwork, and download-all), plus PlaylistTransferCoordinator which imports/exports playlists as JSON documents through document pickers and share sheets. Search/ is a remote-catalog search screen (SearchViewController + Search/Table extensions) backed by a value-type SearchResultsState struct and a UserDefaults-backed SearchHistoryStore, querying APIClient.searchSongs/searchAlbums in parallel with 300 ms Task.sleep debounce, pagination via SearchPage offsets, and result deduplication. Settings/ is a ConfigurableKit-driven StackScrollController (SettingsViewController + API/Actions/Lyrics extensions) covering Subsonic server-profile import/validate/export/delete (ServerProfileImportCoordinator), transfers entry, downloads, lyrics/title tweaks, database and lyrics-index rebuilds, and a self-contained LogViewerController that parses Dog log files. Sync/ is the LAN song-transfer UI: SyncRoleSelectionViewController fans out to a multi-select SyncContentPickerViewController (albums/songs/playlists tabs), sender-side SyncServerStatusViewController and SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController (Bonjour + QR + temporary password), receiver-side SyncReceiverViewController (discovery, QR scan, manual endpoint+password), and SyncTransferProgressViewController which drives the manifest/compare/download/import phases. State ownership follows the project rule: PlaylistStore (Backend) owns playlist data and controllers re-read it on .playlistsDidChange; SyncTransferSession (Backend/Sync, created per-flow via environment.makeSyncTransferSession()) owns transfer state and feeds local Phase/State enums; search and log-viewer state are controller-local value types. All Sync and Settings screens render by fully tearing down and rebuilding StackScrollController arranged subviews (refreshUI pattern), while Playlist/Search use diffable snapshots with hasAppliedInitialSnapshot gating.
conventions: Responsibility-based file splits: XxxViewController.swift plus +Actions/+Menu/+Artwork/+Editing/+Search/+Table/+API/+Lyrics extension files; Sync instead uses one file per screen.

Controllers are init-injected with AppEnvironment; Playlist controllers additionally offer a store-only init (environment: AppEnvironment?) and gate every remote/library feature with `guard let environment else { return }` for testability.

Diffable data sources everywhere in Playlist/Search/SyncContentPicker: nonisolated Hashable Section/Item enums carrying IDs only (.playlist(UUID), .song(entryID:trackID:)), paired with byID dictionaries (playlistsByID, tracksByID, searchResultsByID) rebuilt on reload; hasAppliedInitialSnapshot flag gates animation; snapshot.reconfigureItems for in-place updates; custom UITableViewDiffableDataSource subclasses (PlaylistDiffableDataSource, PlaylistDetailDiffableDataSource) override canEdit/canMove/moveRow/commit and call the store directly.

Settings and Sync screens subclass StackScrollController and compose ConfigurableKit primitives (ConfigurableObject with ephemeralAnnotation .action, ConfigurableInfoView, ConfigurableSectionHeaderView, SeparatorView); UI updates remove all arrangedSubviews and re-run setupContentViews() (refreshUI), throttled in SyncTransferProgressViewController via `lastProgressRefreshDate: Date = .distantPast` (0.1 s).

Async style: Task { @MainActor [weak self] in } with guard-let-self early return; stale-result guards after awaits (`guard playlistID == playlist.id`, `guard searchState.currentQuery == query`, representedPlaylistID/representedArtworkKey checks in cells); task properties (searchTask, debounceTask, headerCoverTask, startupTask, transferTask, coverTask) cancelled before reassignment and in deinit.
Logging via AppLog.info/warning/error with key=value context strings (e.g. "moveSong from=.. to=.. playlistID=.."); swallowed errors are logged per project rule.

User feedback: AlertController for everything — AlertViewController with single .accent OK action for errors, AlertInputViewController for names/passwords (always trims whitespace and rejects empty), AlertProgressIndicatorViewController for blocking work, ConfirmationAlertPresenter for destructive confirms; DownloadSubmissionFeedbackPresenter for download submission toasts.

Search debounce uses Task.sleep (no Combine): SearchViewController separates debounceTask (300 ms) from searchTask; PlaylistViewController folds a 200 ms debounce into one searchTask with off-main filtering via withCheckedContinuation + DispatchQueue.global.

Combine only for cross-cutting observation: ConfigurableKit.publisher(forKey:) and playbackController.$snapshot with removeDuplicates + receive(on: .main) + weak-self sink stored in a single `cancellables` set; store/playlist changes use NotificationCenter (.playlistsDidChange object-scoped to the store, .libraryDidSync).

Coordinator pattern for document-picker flows: @MainActor final NSObject classes (PlaylistTransferCoordinator, ServerProfileImportCoordinator) holding weak viewController, acting as UIDocumentPickerDelegate, presenting their own alerts; callbacks are non-optional closures with empty defaults (onImportCompleted).

Platform gating via #if targetEnvironment(macCatalyst) for camera/QR features; idle timer disabled in viewWillAppear/viewDidDisappear around transfers; background interruption handled by SyncBackgroundInterruptionObserver wrapping UIApplication.didEnterBackgroundNotification.

Observed deviations from stated project rules (relevant for review): PlaylistCell.setHighlighted and prepareForReuse call highlightView.layer.removeAllAnimations() despite the global ban; SearchHistoryStore writes raw UserDefaults key "SearchHistory" instead of going through AppPreferences/ConfigurableKit; LogViewerController uses a classic (non-diffable) UITableViewDataSource.
dataFlows: Playlist mutations all funnel through PlaylistStore (createPlaylist, addSong, removeSong(at:from:), moveSong(in:from:to:), renamePlaylist, updateCover, deletePlaylists, importPlaylist, refreshSongs(in:using:)). Removal/reorder are index-based at the store API while the UI is identity-based: controllers resolve entryID -> firstIndex in playlist.songs immediately before calling the store (PlaylistDetailViewController+Actions.removeSongFromPlaylist, PlaylistDetailDiffableDataSource.commit/moveRowAt map snapshot positions to store indices) — a primary integrity surface if store contents and snapshot drift.

Playlist change propagation: store posts .playlistsDidChange (object: store); list controller reloads store + rebuilds playlistsByID + reapplies sort/search snapshot; detail controller pops itself if its playlistID disappeared, else re-reads store.reload() and reapplies. Detail also deletes snapshot items optimistically right after store mutations.

Playlist import/export crosses the file system: PlaylistTransferCoordinator encodes PlaylistTransferDocument (ISO8601 dates, prettyPrinted+sortedKeys JSON) into temp dir am-playlist-share-<UUID> for UIActivityViewController sharing (cleaned in completion handler), and on import decodes the picked file under security-scoped access, resolves each song.trackID against environment.libraryDatabase (missing tracks dropped and summarized to the user), then persists via playlistStore.importPlaylist.

Cover artwork data: custom covers are user photos resized to max 600 pt, JPEG 0.8, persisted as Data via store.updateCover; generated covers come from PlaylistCoverArtworkCache with a URL provider that prefers local paths.artworkCacheURL(for: trackID) on disk over APIClient.resolveMediaURL templates; PlaylistCell additionally memoizes rendered covers in a static NSCache keyed on playlistID + updatedAt-millis + song count + pixel size (cache invalidation depends on updatedAt being bumped by the store).

PlaylistDetailViewController+Artwork writes app-owned artwork files to disk: for each playlist entry with a local track but no cached artwork, a detached utility task extracts embedded artwork via EmbeddedMetadataReader from the audio file, falls back to a 600x600 remote download via URLSession.shared, and writes atomically to paths.artworkCacheURL (createDirectory + .atomic write), then reapplies the snapshot.

Download flow: downloadAllSongs builds SongDownloadRequest per entry (resolving unknown albumIDs via apiClient.song(id:) in a task group, defaulting to "unknown") and submits through environment.downloadManager.submitRequests; downloaded-state reads go through environment.downloadStore.isDownloaded(trackID:).

Playback flow: song taps and menus resolve PlaylistEntry -> PlaybackTrack preferring the local library record (localTrack.playbackTrack(paths:)) over remote (entry.playbackTrack(apiClient:)), then call environment.playbackController.play(tracks:startAt:source:); deleteSong fans out to musicLibraryTrackRemovalService.removeTrack plus playbackController.removeTracksFromQueue plus playlist removal.

Search data: APIClient.searchSongs/searchAlbums fetched in parallel via throwing task group, merged into SearchResultsState on MainActor; pagination appends with offset bookkeeping in SearchPage and id-based deduplication (duplicates logged); successful queries persist to SearchHistoryStore which writes directly to UserDefaults.standard key "SearchHistory" (cap 20, most-recent-first) — outside the AppPreferences path.

Server profile flow (credentials): ServerProfileImportCoordinator parses loosely-structured JSON (case-insensitive keys, nested subsonic/profile objects, multiple aliases for server/user/pass), normalizes the URL via AppPreferences.normalizeSubsonicServerURL, then persists via AppPreferences.setSubsonicConfiguration BEFORE validating with apiClient.ping(), rolling back to the previous configuration (or clearing) on failure. Export writes the plaintext password into a temp server.subsonicconfig JSON file handed to UIActivityViewController; the settings UI itself masks the password but export does not.

Diagnostics flows: rebuildDatabase clears Kingfisher memory+disk caches and a legacy APIResponseCache directory before calling environment.rebuildLibraryDatabase(forceArtwork:progressCallback:); rebuildAllLyricsIndex calls environment.rebuildAllLyricsIndex; both report scanned/updated/removed counts via alerts. LogViewerController reads Dog log file content read-only.

Sync sender: SyncContentPickerViewController resolves the multi-selection (albums via databaseManager.tracks(inAlbumID:), playlists via tracksByID lookups, deduped by trackID Set) into [AudioTrackRecord]; SyncServerStatusViewController / SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController call session.prepareSender(tracks:/playlist:includeLyrics:) (packaging files + metadata), startSender(), and publish SyncConnectionInfo — including the temporary plaintext password — both on-screen and JSON-encoded into a CIQRCodeGenerator QR image; endpoints are copyable to the pasteboard.

Sync receiver: SyncReceiverViewController authenticates (endpoint, password) -> token via SyncTransferSession (endpoints from Bonjour discovery, QR-decoded SyncConnectionInfo with SyncConstants protocol-version compatibility check, or manually parsed SyncEndpoint); SyncTransferProgressViewController then runs fetchManifest -> missingEntries (diff against local library) -> downloadEntries (temp URLs) -> importDownloadedFiles (writes into the library database, classifying succeeded/duplicates/errors/noMetadata). All interruption paths (PartialDownloadError, CancellationError triggered by the background observer, generic errors) still import whatever finished downloading before reporting, so partial imports are an intentional behavior; session.stopReceiver()/stopSender() are invoked on completion, pop, and deinit.

result.scope: Now Playing controller, sections, components, lyric sheet (MuseAmp/Interface/NowPlaying)
result.summary: This scope is the full-screen Now Playing experience, presented via LNPopupController (never a tab). Two root controllers exist for the two layout worlds: NowPlayingCompactController (iPhone; hosts NowPlayingCompactPageController, which horizontally pages queue/artwork/lyrics via NowPlayingPagingSectionView) and NowPlayingRelaxedController (iPad/Catalyst; NowPlayingRelaxedShellView with left artwork+transport panel and a right panel toggling lyrics/queue via a segmented control). Both controllers are thin: nearly all behavior is shared through a tower of @MainActor "shell controller" protocols in Support/ (NowPlayingShellController -> NowPlayingPlaybackShellController -> Queue/Artwork/Lyrics/Lifecycle/Transport variants) whose default implementations in `where Self: UIViewController` extensions do the Combine binding, queue action wiring, popup preparation, and suspension handling — effectively protocol mixins. State is owned upstream by Backend's PlaybackController (reached via AppEnvironment); this layer only mirrors it into a locally cached `currentPlaybackSnapshot` and derives presentation through NowPlayingControlIslandViewModel + the pure nonisolated NowPlayingContentMapper (PlaybackSnapshot -> AMNowPlayingContent + BackgroundSource + animation flags). Queue rows are built by AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshotBuilder (history capped at 3, upcoming at 10, occurrence-suffixed IDs for duplicate tracks, footer with remaining count/minutes) and rendered by NowPlayingQueueSectionView, a manual UITableViewDataSource with hand-rolled change detection (NowPlayingQueuePresentationUpdate) and anchored auto-scroll with a 1s programmatic-scroll block window. Lyrics live in LyricTimeline/: LyricTimelineView owns its own Combine pipeline (track-ID changes + .lyricsDidUpdate notification + playbackTimeSubject -> LyricsPhase -> item Snapshot), parsing LRC via LyricParser/LyricTimeline (offset tags, multi-timestamp lines, binary-search active line). LyricSheet/ holds LyricSelectionSheetViewController, a multi-select copy sheet that is the only diffable-data-source table in the scope. Components/ contains the transport view class hierarchy (base NowPlayingTransportView + compact/relaxed subclasses, split into +Actions/+Layout/+Navigation), self-subscribing PlayerControlButton subclasses, a scrubbing ProgressTrackView, and the ColorfulX-backed animated artwork background whose palette is computed by NowPlayingArtworkBackgroundCoordinator (DominantColors on a background queue, LRUCache of 32 keyed by artwork URL string). Notably, Support/NowPlayingLyricsCoordinator.swift appears to be unreferenced in the iOS target (LyricTimelineView+Binding now does its own loading; tvOS has its own TVNowPlayingLyricsCoordinator), making it likely dead code worth a reviewer's eye.
conventions: File splits by responsibility: `Type.swift` plus `Type+Playback.swift`, `+LyricSheet.swift`, `+Actions.swift`, `+Layout.swift`, `+Navigation.swift`, `+Binding.swift`, `+Delegate.swift`, `+DataSource.swift` — the +LyricSheet extensions are one-line protocol conformances.

Shared controller behavior via protocol mixins, not base classes: @MainActor `NowPlaying*ShellController` protocols in Support/ with default implementations constrained `where Self: UIViewController`; empty marker protocols (NowPlayingTransportShellController, NowPlayingLyricsPlaybackShellController) kept deliberately with explanatory comments.

Coordinators are final @MainActor classes injected with closures (`currentArtworkURL: () -> URL?`, `shouldApplyLoadedLyrics: (String) -> Bool`) rather than delegate protocols; instantiated as `lazy var` on controllers with `[weak self]` closure captures.
Per-view `nonisolated enum Layout` / `Palette` / `Animation` / `Appearance` namespaces holding static CGFloat/TimeInterval constants; hardcoded 100–200pt header/footer spacers per the project rule.

Callback closures are non-optional with empty defaults (`var onPlayNext: (Int) -> Void = { _ in }`) and forwarded to child views via `didSet`; NowPlayingCompactPageController is pure forwarding glue of these closures.

Date-based cooldowns initialized to `.distantPast` and compared with `Date()` (isProgramaticScrollBlocked, userInteractionDeadline, scrubCommitCooldownDeadline), paired with cancellable DispatchWorkItem or Task retries.

Logging through AppLog with structured key=value strings; dedicated free-function helpers in Support/NowPlayingLogSupport.swift redact URLs (host/lastPathComponent only) and summarize lyric text as length/lines instead of logging content.

Combine: single `var cancellables: Set<AnyCancellable> = []` per type, `[weak self]` in every sink, `.receive(on: DispatchQueue.main)` before UI work, `.removeDuplicates` using private Equatable projection structs (NowPlayingPresentationState / NowPlayingProgressState) to split one $snapshot publisher into presentation vs progress streams; high-frequency time always via playbackTimeSubject.

DEVIATION from project View Lifecycle Rules: NowPlayingQueueSectionView and LyricTimelineView use manual UITableViewDataSource with hand-rolled diff flags and reloadData/reloadSections, not diffable data sources; only LyricSelectionSheetViewController uses UITableViewDiffableDataSource (subclassed as EditableDataSource for canEditRowAt).

`@available(*, unavailable) required init?(coder:) { fatalError() }` on every view/controller; guard-first early returns throughout; animations only via Interface wrappers (smoothSpringAnimate, springAnimate, transition, animate); haptic generators stored as instance properties (gated #if os(iOS) in cells).

Presentation value types are AM-prefixed plain Equatable structs/enums under ViewModel/ (AMNowPlayingContent, AMQueueItemContent, AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshot), decoupled from MuseAmpPlayerKit via the AMNowPlayingQueueTrackPresenting protocol; mapping logic kept in nonisolated static enum funcs (NowPlayingContentMapper, AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshotBuilder) for testability.
NotificationCenter observers stored as `nonisolated(unsafe) var ... NSObjectProtocol?` and removed in deinit; .lyricsDidUpdate userInfo read through AppNotificationUserInfoKey.trackIDs.
dataFlows: Playback state is owned by Backend PlaybackController (via AppEnvironment); this scope is read-mostly. Controllers cache `currentPlaybackSnapshot` from three inputs: $snapshot (deduped twice into presentation/progress streams), playbackTimeSubject (locally patches the cached snapshot via withTime(_:duration:)), and a one-shot read in applyInitialPlaybackPresentation. While the app is backgrounded (NowPlayingApplicationLifecycleCoordinator), UI application is suppressed but the cached snapshot keeps updating; resume replays via refreshControlIslandContent/refreshPlayingContent — a window where displayed and actual state intentionally diverge.

All queue mutations flow back to environment.playbackController (skipToQueueTrack, removeFromQueue, playNext, restartCurrentTrack, shuffleUpcomingQueue, setShuffle, setRepeatMode); the UI never mutates queue arrays locally. Destructive context-menu removal is deferred through `pendingContextMenuRemoval` until the menu dismissal animation completes (NowPlayingQueueSectionView.tableView(_:willEndContextMenuInteraction:)). The onPlayNext path bounds-checks against currentPlaybackSnapshot.queue before dispatching, which can be stale relative to PlaybackController.

Queue rendering: PlaybackTrack array -> AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshotBuilder.makeSnapshot (trims history to last 3 / upcoming to first 10; identity = "track:<occurrence>:<trackID>" to disambiguate duplicates) -> NowPlayingQueueSectionView manual diff (identity vs content vs playerIndex vs header/footer change flags) -> reloadSections(.fade) or visible-cell reconfigure. Footer minutes computed from amQueueTrackDurationInSeconds of queue[playerIndex...].

Lyrics flow (active path): LyricTimelineView+Binding merges currentTrackID changes with .lyricsDidUpdate notifications (filtered to the current track), calls environment.lyricsService.loadLyrics(for:), optionally converts Chinese script per AppPreferences.isLyricsAutoConvertChineseEnabled, parses LRC into LyricTimeline, then combines with playbackTimeSubject to compute the active line. A second, independent parse path exists in LyricTimelineView+Actions.currentTimeline() which re-reads environment.lyricsService.cachedLyrics and re-parses for seek/context-menu timestamps — the two paths must stay in agreement (same conversion + parser) for 'Play from Here' seeks to match displayed lines.

Lyrics persistence (in the likely-dead Support/NowPlayingLyricsCoordinator): fetchLyrics over the network, persistLyricsIfDownloaded writes lyrics to the offline store — including persisting an empty string on HTTP 404 (shouldCacheUnavailableLyricsResult) to cache 'no lyrics' results; keeps separate raw vs Chinese-converted in-memory caches and reprocesses on preference change. If this file is truly unreferenced in the iOS target, the 404-empty-persist behavior now only exists on tvOS — relevant if auditing cache poisoning of the lyrics store.

Artwork/background: NowPlayingArtworkBackgroundCoordinator resolves the artwork image (file URL direct read, else KingfisherManager shared cache), re-encodes to PNG/JPEG, extracts up to 4 dominant colors on a global userInitiated queue, and caches palettes in LRUCache(countLimit: 32) keyed by url.absoluteString; results are applied only after re-validating lastBackgroundSource / currentArtworkURL() to drop stale async completions. The displayed-artwork fast path (updateFromDisplayedArtwork) also sets popupItem.image, guarded by URL equality against the current snapshot.

Cross-layer escape hatch: NowPlayingTransportView+Navigation walks the responder chain and reaches window?.rootViewController as MainController to close the popup and push album detail after a hardcoded 0.5s delay, touching MainController.currentLayoutMode / compactTabBarController / activeContentNavigationController — the most fragile coupling in the scope.

Settings crossings: ConfigurableKit publishers for cleanSongTitleKey (re-applies snapshot + queue presentation, title sanitized via TrackTitleSanitizer in NowPlayingContentMapper) and lyricsAutoConvertChineseKey; clipboard writes (UIPasteboard) from lyric copy actions in both LyricTimelineView+Actions and LyricSelectionSheetViewController.

result.scope: Browse flows + shared collections/common UI (MuseAmp/Interface/Browse, MuseAmp/Interface/Collections, MuseAmp/Interface/Common)
result.summary: This scope holds the local-library browse UI for the iOS app target: three list screens (SongLibraryViewController for album groups in Browse/Albums, SongsViewController for flat tracks in Browse/Songs, DownloadsViewController for active download tasks in Browse/Downloads) plus the drill-in AlbumDetailViewController in Browse/AlbumDetail and the shared AlbumNavigationHelper in Browse/Support that constructs/pushes album detail screens, including synthesizing a CatalogAlbum from local AudioTrackRecords when the album exists on disk. All lists use UITableViewDiffableDataSource keyed by lightweight ID enums (LibraryItem/SongsItem/AlbumItem/String trackID) with side dictionaries (albumsByID, tracksByID, searchTracksByID, tasksByTrackID) that cell providers resolve at dequeue time; controllers own only presentation state while all domain state lives behind AppEnvironment services (libraryDatabase/databaseManager, downloadStore/downloadManager, playlistStore, playbackController, lyricsService, musicLibraryTrackRemovalService, audioFileImporter). Interface/Collections is a library of reusable TableBaseCell subclasses (AmSongCell, AmMediaCell/AmMediaRowView, AlbumTrackCell, DownloadProgressCell, PlaylistHeaderCell, skeleton/show-more/search cells) configured exclusively through Hashable value-type *Content structs (SongRowContent, MediaRowContent, AlbumTrackCellContent, AlbumHeaderContent, AMDownloadProgressContent, ArtworkContent), with model-to-content adapter inits living in Backend/Models or in private controller extensions. Interface/Common provides the shared infrastructure: TableBaseCell, MediaDetailViewController base class, EmptyStateView, the Interface animation wrapper + InterfaceStyle/PlatformInterfacePalette style enums, MuseAmpImageView (a Kingfisher-backed image view with manual loadID ticketing, disk-cache decode queue, shimmer, and QuickLook preview), and the Presenters folder of toast/alert/progress/export presenters (SongExportPresenter, PlaybackFeedbackPresenter, DownloadSubmissionFeedbackPresenter, ConfirmationAlertPresenter, ProgressActionPresenter, LyricsReloadPresenter, TrackArtworkRepairPresenter, ImageQuickLookPreviewPresenter). AlbumDetailViewController is the most complex type: it accepts a full album, a stub album, or a bare song/album ID, lazily resolves the real album via APIClient, patches missing artwork from track attributes, and exposes download/playlist/export/playback menus per the album-menu rules. Note for reviewers: the project CLAUDE.md claims TableBaseCell/Interface/EmptyStateView/MediaDetailViewController live in a MuseAmpInterfaceKit package, but no such package exists in this working tree — the files in Interface/Common ARE the canonical implementations here, not shims.
conventions: Controllers split by responsibility: XxxViewController.swift plus +Actions.swift, +Table.swift, +Search.swift, +Menu.swift, +Playback.swift extensions; section/item identifier enums declared `nonisolated enum ... : Hashable` at file top level

Diffable-only data sources: snapshots store String/enum IDs, cell providers resolve records via [ID: Record] dictionaries; per-controller `hasAppliedInitialSnapshot`/`hasPreparedInitialData`/`hasAppliedInitialData` flags gate animation, with animate computed as `hasAppliedInitialSnapshot && view.window != nil`; in-place updates via snapshot.reconfigureItems

Cells inherit TableBaseCell (Interface/Common); reuse identifier via `class var reuseID: String { String(describing: self) }` in newer cells vs `static let reuseID = "Name"` in older ones; prepareForReuse fully resets every mutable property; subviews pre-allocated and toggled via isHidden

Cell configuration only through Hashable value-type *Content structs with defaulted memberwise inits (SongRowContent, MediaRowContent, AlbumTrackCellContent, AlbumHeaderContent, AMDownloadProgressContent); adapter inits live in Backend/Models extensions (e.g. SongRowContent+AppModels.swift) or as `private extension` init(task:) inside the consuming controller

Heavy guard/early-return style: `guard let self else { return }` at closure top, `guard ... else { return nil/[] }` in delegate methods; closures always `[weak self]`; Combine sinks `.receive(on: DispatchQueue.main)` + `.store(in: &cancellables)` with a single Set<AnyCancellable>

Logging via AppLog with subject-first signature `AppLog.error(self, "...")` or string tag (`AppLog.error("SongExportPresenter", ...)`) and key=value message suffixes (`albumID=\(...) error=\(...)`); every catch/failure path logs; MuseAmpImageView redacts URLs to host/lastPathComponent before logging

Menus: providers (PlaybackMenuProvider, AddToPlaylistMenuProvider, SongContextMenuProvider, CopyMenuProvider, DownloadMenuProvider from Backend/MenuProviders) injected as lazy properties initialized from environment; sections grouped with MenuSectionProvider.inline() / UIMenu(options: .displayInline); data fetched at action time via @escaping providers; DownloadsViewController uses UIDeferredMenuElement.uncached for the bar menu

Presenters: stateless caseless enums with static present()/run() methods for toasts and alerts (SPIndicator only inside PlaybackFeedbackPresenter/DownloadSubmissionFeedbackPresenter; AlertController for errors/confirm/progress); stateful presenters (SongExportPresenter, LyricsReloadPresenter) are final classes holding `weak var viewController`

Search: 300 ms Task.sleep debounce in a cancellable `searchTask`, DB query on Task.detached(priority: .userInitiated), result applied on MainActor; SongLibrary re-checks `currentQuery == query` before applying while Songs only checks Task.isCancelled; SongLibrary reuses `tracksByID` for search results whereas Songs keeps a separate `searchTracksByID`

Animations via the Interface wrapper enum (springAnimate/smoothSpringAnimate/animate/quickAnimate/transition/keyframeAnimate) which forces .beginFromCurrentState/.allowUserInteraction; spacing/colors via InterfaceStyle.Spacing and PlatformInterfacePalette; layout via SnapKit with stored Constraint references updated in place; Then `.then {}` for inline config

Local deviations from stated repo rules that reviewers should treat as existing convention: AlbumTrackCell.setHighlighted and MuseAmpImageView.showImageState call layer.removeAllAnimations() despite the global 'never removeAllAnimations' rule, and cleanSongTitle/refreshDownloadStateUI paths call tableView.reloadData() directly alongside the diffable source
All user-facing strings via String(localized:); init?(coder:) always `@available(*, unavailable)` + fatalError(); UIKit classes marked @MainActor; FileManager.default used directly
dataFlows: Read path: list controllers re-query MuseAmpDatabaseKit on every refresh — environment.libraryDatabase.allAlbums()/allTracks()/searchTracks(query:)/trackOrNil(byID:) and environment.databaseManager.tracks(inAlbumID:) — then rebuild parallel array + ID-dictionary state in memory; snapshots carry only IDs, so a dictionary/snapshot desync silently renders empty or stale cells (cell providers `guard let record = dictionary[id] else { return cell }` without logging)

Refresh triggers crossing layers: NotificationCenter .libraryDidSync (full reload) and .artworkDidUpdate (userInfo[AppNotificationUserInfoKey.trackIDs] -> reconfigureItems), DownloadManager.tasksPublisher (CurrentValueSubject driving DownloadsViewController.applyUpdate), PlaybackController.$snapshot currentTrack.id (reconfigures track cells), ConfigurableKit.publisher for cleanSongTitle/maxConcurrentDownloads, plus unconditional re-read in viewWillAppear

AlbumDetailViewController identity resolution: may start from a stub CatalogAlbum (id can be "") or pendingSongID; loadTracks() fetches via environment.apiClient.song(id:)/album(id:) and replaces self.album wholesale; setTracks() detects and logs duplicate track IDs and patches missing album artwork from the first track's artwork; `String.isKnownAlbumID`/`isCatalogID` gate whether network resolution and artwork-repair actions are offered

AlbumNavigationHelper.localCatalogAlbum() transforms local AudioTrackRecords into SubsonicClientKit CatalogAlbum/CatalogSong DTOs, fabricating Artwork entries whose URL is the local file URL from environment.paths.artworkCacheURL(for:) — remote DTO types double as the local-browse UI model, so ID and URL semantics differ by origin

Mutations all flow outward to Backend services: deletes via environment.musicLibraryTrackRemovalService.removeTrack(s) plus playbackController.removeTracksFromQueue(trackIDs:); downloads via track.downloadRequest(albumID:apiClient:) -> environment.downloadManager.submitRequests (feedback via DownloadSubmissionFeedbackPresenter); playlist create/add via environment.playlistStore.createPlaylist/addSong with fire-and-forget background lyrics fetch (environment.lyricsService.fetchLyrics -> playlistStore.updateLyrics, failures logged at .info only); imports via environment.audioFileImporter.importFiles(urls:) behind an AlertProgressIndicatorViewController

Album deletion in AlbumDetailViewController pops the screen when no downloaded tracks remain; SongLibrary deleteAlbums aggregates tracks per album via databaseManager queries before delegating removal — per-album query failures are logged and skipped, so a partial delete is possible

File I/O owned by this scope: SongExportPresenter writes .lrc files to temporaryDirectory/am-lyrics-export-<UUID> (dedupes case-insensitive filenames, falls back to AVURLAsset metadata extraction when LyricsCacheStore misses) and delegates audio batch prep to Backend/Sync SyncPreparedTrackBuilder, removing the temp directory in the share-sheet completion handler; MuseAmpImageView+Preview and ImageQuickLookPreviewPresenter write temp preview images (LRU-cached URL->URL / deleted on deinit); artwork cache files probed with FileManager.default.fileExists at cell-configure time

MuseAmpImageView load pipeline guards data integrity with a monotonic loadID ticket checked after every async hop (memory cache -> concurrent disk-decode queue -> Kingfisher network), re-storing decoded images to memory cache only (toDisk: false); stale completions are dropped rather than applied

DownloadsViewController defers snapshot application while a row context menu is open (isShowingRowContextMenu/hasPendingUIRefresh) — currentTasks/tasksByTrackID keep updating from the publisher, so visible cells can lag the model until flushPendingUIRefreshIfNeeded runs; identity-unchanged updates bypass snapshots and mutate visible cells directly via refreshVisibleCells() indexed by indexPath.row into currentTasks

Preference persistence from this scope: sort options via AppPreferences.storedSortOption/setStoredSortOption (libraryAlbumSortOptionKey, songsSortOptionKey); maxConcurrentDownloads written through ConfigurableKit.set; copy actions write directly to UIPasteboard.general

findings.title: Magic sentinel host "example.com" encodes hidden cross-module contract
findings.detail: perform() guards `baseURL.host != "example.com"` and throws 'No server configured.' The literal is a hidden coupling to the app target's placeholder default `AppPreferences.defaultAPIBaseURL = URL(string: "https://example.com/rest")!` (MuseAmp/Application/AppPreferences.swift:29). Nothing in the package explains why this host is special; the package's own tests dodge the guard by using subdomains (`music.example.com`, `cache.example.com`). If the app ever changes its placeholder URL, this guard silently stops working and users get raw transport errors to example.com instead of the friendly message.
findings.suggestion: Hoist the literal to a named constant (e.g. `private static let unconfiguredPlaceholderHost = "example.com"`) documented as matching AppPreferences.defaultAPIBaseURL, or better: expose an explicit `isConfigured`/placeholder API on the package that APIClient uses, so the contract is visible on both sides instead of via a magic string.

findings.title: playback(id:) reimplements the perform() cache pipeline inline
findings.detail: Every other public method funnels through the generic perform(_:cache:decode:) pipeline. playback(id:) instead hand-builds the cache key with the same `"\(baseURL.absoluteString)|\(username)|\(...cacheIdentifier)"` format duplicated from perform() line 158, manually checks `playbackCache.freshValue` (line 118), fetches via fetchSubsonicSong with `cache: nil`, and manually calls `playbackCache.setValue` (line 137). As a side effect of bypassing the canonical path, playback is the only endpoint that gets no request coalescing, no disk caching, and no stale-on-failure fallback — a behavioral divergence a reader cannot see without diffing the two code paths.
findings.suggestion: Route playback through the canonical pipeline: `try await perform(.song(id: id), cache: playbackCache) { (payload: SubsonicSongPayload) in ...build PlaybackInfo... }` (the decode closure can throw and capture self like album/song already do). At minimum, extract a `private func cacheKey(for endpoint: SubsonicEndpoint) -> String` so the key format exists once.

findings.title: perform() duplicates its decode→cache→return tail in the auth-fallback branch
findings.detail: The auth-fallback catch branch (lines 195-200: `let mapped = try await decode(fallbackPayload)`, `cache.setValue`, `storeToDisk`, `return mapped`) is a copy of the main success tail (lines 202-207) differing only in which Data it uses. Combined with the do/catch nested inside a do/catch and the redundant if/else at lines 173-178 (`if cache != nil { fetchNetworkData(..., coalescingKey: cacheKey) } else { fetchNetworkData(..., coalescingKey: nil) }`), the 80-line function mixes orchestration with two copies of the same finishing sequence, so a future change (e.g. cache write ordering) must be made in two places.
findings.suggestion: Extract a helper like `private func mapAndCache<Response, Output>(_ payload: Response, rawData: Data, cache: ResponseCache<Output>?, cacheKey: String, decode: ...) async throws -> Output` used by both the primary and fallback paths, and collapse lines 173-178 to `let coalescingKey = cache != nil ? cacheKey : nil` followed by a single fetchNetworkData call.

findings.title: loadFreshDataFromDisk and loadStaleDataFromDisk are near-identical copies
findings.detail: loadStaleDataFromDisk (lines 409-422) repeats loadFreshDataFromDisk (lines 382-399) verbatim — provider guard, envelope load, version check with remove — except for one TTL guard at line 394. And because `cacheTTL` is the constant `.infinity` (line 18), that TTL guard (`Date().timeIntervalSince(envelope.cachedAt) < cacheTTL`) can never fail, so the two 15-line functions are currently behaviorally identical; the fresh/stale naming promises a distinction that does not exist on the disk path.
findings.suggestion: Extract one `private func validCachedEnvelope(forKey:) async -> CacheEnvelope?` that handles the provider guard and version check/removal, then keep the freshness comparison as a one-line call-site difference — or delete the dead TTL plumbing entirely while cacheTTL stays .infinity.

findings.title: KeyedDecodingContainer lossy-decoding extension buried at tail of service file
findings.detail: SubsonicMusicService.swift's primary export is the service, but lines 689-740 append `private struct SubsonicPingPayload` and a 50-line internal `extension KeyedDecodingContainer` (decodeLossyString, decodeLossyStringIfPresent, decodeLossyIntIfPresent, decodeLossyInt64IfPresent). Every DTO in Models/Subsonic/ (SubsonicSong.swift, SubsonicAlbum.swift, SubsonicArtist.swift) depends on these helpers, yet a reader of those files has no way to discover the definitions live at the bottom of an unrelated 740-line service file. All other payload wrappers (SubsonicSongPayload, SubsonicAlbumPayload, ...) live in Models/Subsonic/, making SubsonicPingPayload's placement inconsistent too.
findings.suggestion: Move the extension to its own file next to its consumers, e.g. `Models/Subsonic/KeyedDecodingContainer+LossyDecoding.swift`, and move SubsonicPingPayload into Models/Subsonic/ alongside its sibling payload wrappers.

findings.title: Bare Subsonic error codes 40/41 in auth-fallback predicate
findings.detail: shouldFallbackToPlainAuth checks `code == 40 || code == 41` with no indication that these are the Subsonic protocol's 'wrong username or password' (40) and 'token authentication not supported' (41) error codes. A reader must consult the Subsonic spec to understand why exactly these two numbers trigger a downgrade to plain-password auth, while the adjacent HTTP check at line 332 (`statusCode == 401 || statusCode == 403`) at least uses universally known values.
findings.suggestion: Name the codes, e.g. `private enum SubsonicErrorCode { static let wrongCredentials = 40; static let tokenAuthUnsupported = 41 }`, and consider hoisting the sniffed keyword list (token/salt/password/authentication/credential) to a named static set.

findings.title: Resource-type literals "songs"/"albums"/"artists" repeated across mappers and into the app
findings.detail: The `type:` literal is written inline five times in this file alone (lines 592, 625, 646, 661, 676), and the same strings are re-typed in app code that constructs Catalog models (e.g. MuseAmp/Backend/Library/AudioTrackRecord+AppModels.swift:40, MuseAmp/Interface/Browse/Support/AlbumNavigationHelper.swift:47/141, AlbumDetailViewController.swift:78). Because CatalogSong/CatalogAlbum/CatalogArtist include `type` in their == and hash(into:) implementations, a single typo at any construction site silently breaks identity comparison and diffable-snapshot dedup.
findings.suggestion: Add a public canonical constant per model, e.g. `public static let resourceType = "songs"` on CatalogSong (likewise for album/artist), use it in the mapping extension, and let app-side constructors reference it instead of re-typing the literal.

findings.title: mapSong hardcodes hasLyrics: true for every song
findings.detail: mapSong unconditionally sets `hasLyrics: true, hasTimeSyncedLyrics: false` even though the Subsonic song row carries no lyrics information at all. CatalogSongAttributes models these as `Bool?` precisely so 'unknown' is representable, and no app code currently reads either field (grep finds no consumer of `attributes.hasLyrics`), so the hardcoded `true` is a dishonest value waiting to mislead the first future consumer that trusts it.
findings.suggestion: Pass `hasLyrics: nil, hasTimeSyncedLyrics: nil` to honestly model that the search/song endpoints do not report lyric availability; let the lyrics(id:) call be the source of truth.

findings.title: Per-song enrichment failures vanish with no trace
findings.detail: enrichSongSearchResponse's task group catches every error from `self.song(id:)` and silently substitutes the thinner search row (`catch { return (index, song) }`). The package deliberately contains no logger, but it also offers the caller no signal whatsoever that enrichment degraded — APIClient (which owns AppLog per the repo Logging Rules requiring every swallowed error to leave a trace) cannot log what it cannot observe. A user seeing incomplete song metadata has no diagnostic trail.
findings.suggestion: Without adding logging to the package, expose a narrow diagnostics seam — e.g. an optional `@Sendable (Error) -> Void` enrichment-failure handler injected at init — that APIClient wires to AppLog.warning, so the degradation is observable while the package stays log-free.

findings.title: Results array seeded by repeating the first song as filler
findings.detail: `var results = Array(repeating: indexed[0].element, count: indexed.count)` builds the output buffer by replicating song #0 into every slot before the task-group loop overwrites them. The reader must verify the non-empty guard at line 459, reason that every index is guaranteed to be yielded by the group, and only then conclude the filler can never leak — three steps of inference for what should be a self-evident initialization, and any future early-exit from the group would silently duplicate the first song.
findings.suggestion: Initialize from the source of truth: `var results = songs.data` — same indices, already-correct fallback values, zero placeholder reasoning needed.

findings.title: search() artwork builder uses weak-self/baseURL capture dance unlike sibling closures
findings.detail: The search decode closure builds artwork via `{ [serviceBaseURL = self.baseURL, weak self] coverArtID in self?.artworkTemplateURL(coverArtID: coverArtID, baseURL: serviceBaseURL) }`, while the album() and song() decode closures (lines 97, 110) simply call `self.artworkTemplateURL(coverArtID:)` with a strong capture. Since `baseURL` is a `let` on the class, `serviceBaseURL` is always identical to `self.baseURL`, so the `baseURL: URL? = nil` parameter on artworkTemplateURL (line 297) exists solely for this one call site and never changes behavior; the weak capture additionally means artwork silently becomes nil if the service deallocates mid-decode — a divergence from siblings with no payoff.
findings.suggestion: Capture self strongly and call `self.artworkTemplateURL(coverArtID: $0)` like album()/song() do, then delete the now-unused `baseURL: URL? = nil` parameter from artworkTemplateURL.

findings.title: Payload decoded even when Subsonic status is not ok
findings.detail: init(from:) decodes status and error, then unconditionally runs `payload = try Payload(from: nestedDecoder)`. This only works today because every payload wrapper happens to have exclusively optional/defaulted fields; the moment any payload gains a required field, error responses (status "failed") will throw a keyNotFound DecodingError from this line before decodeSubsonicResponse can inspect `status`, masking the server's actual error message behind a misleading 'Failed to decode' APIError.decodingFailed. The invariant 'all payload fields must be optional' is enforced nowhere and visible nowhere.
findings.suggestion: Decode the payload only on success: `payload = status == "ok" ? try Payload(from: nestedDecoder) : nil`, so error envelopes can never be shadowed by payload-shape mismatches.

findings.title: Stale window 7 * 24 * 3600 inlined as a default parameter
findings.detail: `staleValue(forKey:maxAge: TimeInterval = 7 * 24 * 3600)` hides the package-wide stale-fallback policy (7 days) inside a default-parameter arithmetic expression. The only production caller (SubsonicMusicService.staleFallback line 442) relies on this default, so the single most important cache policy number in the package has no name and no discoverable home.
findings.suggestion: Hoist to a named constant, e.g. `static let defaultStaleMaxAge: TimeInterval = 7 * 24 * 3600` on ResponseCache, and use it as the default parameter value.

findings.title: CatalogArtist omits Identifiable unlike its sibling catalog models
findings.detail: CatalogSong (CatalogSong.swift:10) and CatalogAlbum (CatalogAlbum.swift:10) both declare `Decodable, Hashable, Sendable, Identifiable` with identical id-based ==/hash semantics; CatalogArtist declares only `Decodable, Hashable, Sendable` despite having the same `id: String` property and the same identity-based equality. The asymmetry forces callers to special-case artists in any generic Identifiable-constrained code and reads as an oversight rather than a decision.
findings.suggestion: Add `Identifiable` to CatalogArtist's conformance list to match CatalogSong and CatalogAlbum.

findings.title: Unexplained size-query filtering inside resolvedURLString
findings.detail: After substituting {w}/{h} via string replacement, resolvedURLString re-parses the URL and drops `size` query items whose value is literally "{w}" or "{h}". This looks unreachable — the placeholders were just replaced — and only the test fixture (SubsonicClientKitTests.swift:255, `size=%7Bw%7D`) reveals the actual reason: percent-encoded placeholders survive the raw string replacement and only appear as "{w}" once URLComponents decodes the query. The function thus mixes high-level template resolution with a subtle encoding-edge-case repair, with zero in-code explanation of why the second pass exists.
findings.suggestion: Extract the filter into a named helper such as `removingUnresolvedSizePlaceholders(_:)`, or add a one-line comment stating that percent-encoded {w}/{h} placeholders bypass the string replacement and must be stripped after URL parsing.

findings.title: Transition-publish sequence duplicated across loadAndPlay, continueWithCurrentEngineItem, and restorePlayback
findings.detail: The ~20-line tail that publishes a track transition is copy-pasted three times: loadAndPlay lines 109-131, continueWithCurrentEngineItem lines 170-192, and restorePlayback (MusicPlayer+Restoration.swift lines 58-89). Each repeats the identical sequence: engine.play()/mediaCenterCoordinator.activateSessionIfPossible()/setState(.playing), nowPlayingManager.setTrack + updateRate, snapshot capture, nowPlayingManager.updateQueueInfo(index: snap.history.count, count: snap.totalCount), remoteCommandManager.updateEnabledCommands, the 4-argument updateLikeCommand call, and the didTransitionTo/didChangeQueue delegate pair. Any change to the now-playing publishing contract (e.g. a new media-center field) must be replicated in three files, and the three copies have already drifted slightly (restorePlayback orders setTrack before play, the others after).
findings.suggestion: Extract one internal helper on MusicPlayer, e.g. publishTransition(to item: PlayerItem, reason: TransitionReason, startPlaying: Bool), that owns the setTrack/updateRate/updateQueueInfo/updateEnabledCommands/updateLikeCommand/delegate sequence, and call it from all three sites. This fits the existing responsibility-based extension split (it can live in MusicPlayer+Playback.swift).

findings.title: Item-failure handling (NSError construction + advance-or-stop) duplicated in two files
findings.detail: continueWithCurrentEngineItem lines 144-165 and observeItemStatus in MusicPlayer+Observation.swift lines 91-113 contain the same block twice: build NSError(domain: "MuseAmpPlayerKit", code: -1, userInfo: [NSLocalizedDescriptionKey: String(localized: "Unknown playback error", bundle: .module)]) from avItem.error, fire delegate didFailItem, then either loadAndPlay(playbackQueue.advance(), reason: .itemFailed) or musicPlayerDidReachEndOfQueue + stop(). The error domain string, magic code -1, and localized fallback message are inlined at both sites; the recovery policy can silently drift between the preloaded-item path and the KVO path.
findings.suggestion: Add an internal helper, e.g. handleItemFailure(_ item: PlayerItem, underlying: Error?), that builds the fallback NSError (with the domain/code hoisted to named constants) and performs the didFailItem + advance-or-stop logic; call it from both continueWithCurrentEngineItem and observeItemStatus.

findings.title: Four-argument like-command refresh repeated verbatim at five call sites
findings.detail: remoteCommandManager.updateLikeCommand(isEnabled: canHandleLikeCommand, isActive: currentItemLiked, localizedTitle: likeCommandLocalizedTitle, localizedShortTitle: likeCommandLocalizedShortTitle) appears with identical arguments in MusicPlayer.swift lines 203-208 and 220-225, MusicPlayer+Playback.swift lines 122-127 and 183-188, and MusicPlayer+Restoration.swift lines 81-86. Every site re-derives the same four values from the same stored properties; adding a fifth parameter means touching five call sites in three files.
findings.suggestion: Add an internal zero-argument helper on MusicPlayer, e.g. func refreshLikeCommand(), that reads the four properties and forwards to remoteCommandManager.updateLikeCommand; replace all five call sites.

findings.title: End-of-queue sequence (didReachEndOfQueue + stop) duplicated at five sites
findings.detail: The pair delegate?.musicPlayerDidReachEndOfQueue(self) followed by stop() is repeated in MusicPlayer+Playback.swift lines 95-96 (loadAndPlay nil guard) and 161-163, MusicPlayer+Queue.swift lines 21-22 (next), and MusicPlayer+Observation.swift lines 77-80 (handleItemEnd, conditionally) and 110-112 (observeItemStatus). If the end-of-queue contract changes (e.g. state should become .idle without clearing the queue), five sites must be found and edited.
findings.suggestion: Extract an internal helper, e.g. func endQueuePlayback(), encapsulating the delegate notification plus stop(), and call it from all five sites (handleItemEnd can pass whether stop() applies, or keep its repeatMode check around the call).

findings.title: Three unused permutation helpers (dead code in tricky index domain)
findings.detail: insertIntoPermutation(_:at:) (line 40), removeFromPermutation(_:) (line 46), and adjustPermutationAfterCanonicalRemoval(at:) (line 52) have zero call sites anywhere in the package or its tests (verified by grep). PlaybackQueue.swift instead manipulates shufflePermutation inline (e.g. insert at line 252, compactMap-based removal at lines 436-439). Dead helpers in the canonical-index/permutation domain actively mislead readers into assuming they are part of the mutation flow, and they imply a second (unused) removal strategy alongside the inline one.
findings.suggestion: Either delete the three unused helpers, or refactor PlaybackQueue's inline permutation mutations (insert at line 252, removal at lines 436-443) to actually route through them so there is one canonical implementation.

findings.title: Dead private method rebuildShufflePermutationIndices with a factually false comment
findings.detail: private mutating func rebuildShufflePermutationIndices() (lines 482-488) is never called (verified by grep). Its comment states "This is called after clearUpcoming which removes items." — but clearUpcoming (lines 329-357) never calls it; clearUpcoming does its own indexMap-based rebuild inline. A reader auditing shuffle-index correctness will waste time reconciling this phantom step with the real flow.
findings.suggestion: Delete rebuildShufflePermutationIndices entirely (the inline rebuild in clearUpcoming is the real implementation), or if it is meant to be the canonical rebuild, call it from clearUpcoming and remove the inline duplicate.

findings.title: Speculative protocol requirements: rate and hasAdvancedToPreloadedItem are never used
findings.detail: var rate: Float { get } (line 12) and func hasAdvancedToPreloadedItem() -> Bool (line 34) are required by the AudioPlaybackEngine seam and implemented by both AVPlayerEngine and the test mock, but no production or test code ever reads/calls them (verified by grep: the only hits are the declarations and implementations). Every future engine implementation is forced to satisfy API nobody consumes, widening the seam beyond what behavior actually varies.
findings.suggestion: Remove rate and hasAdvancedToPreloadedItem() from the AudioPlaybackEngine protocol and from AVPlayerEngine/MockAudioPlaybackEngine, or start using them where the player currently re-derives the same information (advanceToPreloadedItem already covers the auto-advanced case internally).

findings.title: Write-only stored property: weak var player is assigned but never read
findings.detail: private weak var player: MusicPlayer? (line 12) is written once in register(player:) at line 24 and never read afterwards: every command closure captures the register parameter via [weak player], and the trailing updateLikeCommand call (lines 103-108) reads the non-optional parameter, not the property. The stored property is dead state that misleads readers into thinking the manager holds a live back-reference used outside register.
findings.suggestion: Delete the stored player property and the self.player = player assignment; the closures already capture the parameter weakly. If a stored back-reference is genuinely needed later, reintroduce it with an actual read site.

findings.title: Silent try? on audio session activate/deactivate despite available logger
findings.detail: activate() uses try? AVAudioSession.sharedInstance().setActive(true) (line 105) and deactivate() uses try? ... setActive(false, options:) (lines 111-114) with no log trace, even though the class owns a logger and a private log(_:_:) helper and the repo's logging rule requires every swallowed error to log. A failed session activation is a user-visible failure mode (playback starts silently or routes wrong) and currently leaves zero diagnostics. Additionally, the fallback try? session.setCategory(.playback, mode: .default) at line 50 swallows its own failure while the unconditional "fell back to audio session..." log at lines 51-54 prints success regardless.
findings.suggestion: Replace the try? calls with do/catch that logs via log(.warning/.error, ...) including describe(error:), e.g. "failed to activate audio session error=..."; for line 50, log when the fallback setCategory itself throws instead of unconditionally logging success.

findings.title: Optional stored callback properties that are only ever read back within the same call
findings.detail: onInterruptionBegan, onInterruptionEndedShouldResume, and onRouteOldDeviceUnavailable (lines 15-17) are optional stored properties assigned from configure's non-optional parameters (lines 28-30), then immediately copied into non-optional locals (lines 57-59) which are what the notification closures actually capture. The stored optionals are never read anywhere else, so they are write-only state; making them optional also violates the repo property rule of avoiding unnecessary optionals for callbacks.
findings.suggestion: Delete the three stored properties and capture configure's parameters directly in the notification-observer closures (they are already @Sendable and non-optional).

findings.title: teardown() is never called by anything
findings.detail: func teardown() (lines 118-127) removes the interruption/route observers, but grep finds no call site in the package, app, or tests. MusicPlayer+Playback.swift line 76 even documents the opposite policy: "Session observers stay alive for the player's lifetime". The method is speculative cleanup API that contradicts the documented lifecycle.
findings.suggestion: Delete teardown(), or if observer cleanup is genuinely needed (e.g. for tests creating multiple players), call it from MusicPlayer.stop() or a future deinit path and update the comment in stop() accordingly.

findings.title: Asymmetric side effects between shuffled and repeatMode setters
findings.detail: Both setters mutate state that is part of QueueSnapshot (snapshot carries both shuffled and repeatMode), yet they publish differently: the shuffled setter (lines 58-65) fires delegate didChangeQueue but never calls remoteCommandManager.updateEnabledCommands (whose nextTrackCommand.isEnabled depends on upcoming emptiness, which un-shuffling can change), while the repeatMode setter (lines 67-74) calls updateEnabledCommands but never fires didChangeQueue — so when Control Center's changeRepeatModeCommand sets repeatMode (RemoteCommandManager.swift lines 74-85), app-side observers of didChangeQueue never learn the snapshot changed.
findings.suggestion: Make both setters perform the same publication sequence: update queue state, preloadNextItem(), remoteCommandManager.updateEnabledCommands(queue:), and delegate didChangeQueue — ideally via one shared private helper so the two cannot drift.

findings.title: Misleading comments in rewind() contradict the actual logic
findings.detail: Line 169's comment "Move current back to upcoming by decrementing currentIndex" precedes code that does no decrement — it searches order.firstIndex(of: lastPlayed) and jumps there (lines 170-176). Line 178's "Fallback: insert at current position" describes a branch that only mutates the permutation when shuffled (lines 179-184); in the non-shuffled fallback nothing is inserted, only currentIndex is defaulted. In index-permutation code this dense, wrong comments are worse than none: a maintainer verifying history semantics will trust the comment over the code.
findings.suggestion: Rewrite or delete both comments so they describe what the code does (e.g. "jump currentIndex to lastPlayed's position in the effective order" and "shuffled fallback: lastPlayed is no longer in the permutation, reinsert it at the current position"); the repo style keeps comments only where they remove real ambiguity.

findings.title: insert(_:at:) uses two mutually exclusive if-blocks and a self-contradictory comment
findings.detail: insert(_:at:) handles shuffled with `if shuffled { ... }` (lines 250-253) and then non-shuffled with a second independent `if !shuffled { ... }` (lines 258-271) instead of an else, forcing the reader to re-derive that the branches are exclusive. The bridging comment (lines 254-257) contradicts itself: "appending to items already puts it at the right canonical position — but we need to insert at the right spot." The non-shuffled strategy (append to end, then removeLast + insert at target, then fix playedIndices and currentIndex) is an append-then-move dance that obscures the simple intent of inserting at a canonical position.
findings.suggestion: Restructure as if shuffled { ... } else { ... }, compute the clamped canonical target first and items.insert(item, at: clampedTarget) directly (adjusting playedIndices/currentIndex as today), and replace the contradictory comment with one sentence describing the index-shift bookkeeping.

findings.title: Magic preferredTimescale 600 repeated across three files
findings.detail: CMTime(seconds:, preferredTimescale: 600) is inlined at MusicPlayer+Playback.swift line 84 (seek), MusicPlayer+Observation.swift line 22 (periodic observer interval), and MusicPlayer+Restoration.swift line 64 (restore seek). The timescale is a shared engine-wide decision but exists as three uncoordinated literals.
findings.suggestion: Hoist a single internal constant, e.g. `enum PlayerTime { static let preferredTimescale: CMTimeScale = 600 }` (or a static on MusicPlayer), and use it at all three sites.

findings.title: Inline 3.0-second restart threshold in rewind()
findings.detail: rewind(currentTime:) compares `currentTime > 3.0` (line 152) to decide restart-vs-previous. The 3-second policy is documented app behavior (previous() restarts if currentTime > 3s) but lives as a bare literal inside the queue struct, where nothing names its meaning.
findings.suggestion: Hoist to a named constant on PlaybackQueue, e.g. `private static let restartThreshold: TimeInterval = 3.0`, and compare against it.

findings.title: Default Like title literal duplicated
findings.detail: String(localized: "Like", bundle: .module) is written twice: as the stored-property default at line 96 and again as the reset fallback in configureLikeCommand at line 200. If the default key or wording changes, both sites must be edited in the same file.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one private static constant, e.g. `static var defaultLikeTitle: String { String(localized: "Like", bundle: .module) }`, and use it for both the property default and the configureLikeCommand fallback.

findings.title: timeUpdateInterval default (0.1) contradicts AGENTS.md documented default (0.25s)
findings.detail: MusicPlayer.swift line 76 sets `public var timeUpdateInterval: TimeInterval = 0.1`, while AGENTS.md's MusicPlayer API reference documents `var timeUpdateInterval: TimeInterval   // default 0.25s`. The repo's Documentation Sync rule requires the doc and code to agree; consumers reading the package reference will assume a 2.5x coarser update cadence than the code delivers.
findings.suggestion: Update the AGENTS.md package-reference comment to 0.25 -> 0.1 (or restore the 0.25 default in code if 0.1 was unintentional) so the documented contract matches the implementation.

findings.title: SessionMediaCenterBackend logs under the component name MediaCenterCoordinator
findings.detail: SessionMediaCenterBackend's private log helper (line 152) and the becomeActiveIfPossible callback (lines 143-147) both hard-code component: "MediaCenterCoordinator", although the emitting type is SessionMediaCenterBackend. The package convention is that each class's log helper hard-codes its own component string (AVPlayerEngine, AudioSessionManager do); these log lines misattribute their source, which matters precisely when debugging which backend was selected.
findings.suggestion: Change the component string in both places to "SessionMediaCenterBackend" (or include the backendKind), matching the emitting type per the per-class log-helper convention.

findings.title: preloadNextItem builds a full snapshot to read one upcoming item
findings.detail: Line 202 uses `playbackQueue.snapshot().upcoming` — constructing the whole QueueSnapshot (mapping history, nowPlaying, and every upcoming item) — only to take `.first`. PlaybackQueue already exposes the derived `upcoming` property directly; everywhere else the snapshot is taken only when the full value is published. Using the snapshot here mixes the publishing-level abstraction into an internal queue peek and does redundant work on every track transition and queue edit.
findings.suggestion: Replace with `let next = playbackQueue.upcoming.first` (and drop the intermediate upcomingItems binding).

findings.title: Boolean property currentItemLiked lacks is-prefix
findings.detail: Internal stored property `var currentItemLiked = false` (line 98) is a boolean without the is/has/can prefix, inconsistent with its siblings in the same type (`isPeriodicTimeObserverSuspended` line 78, `canHandleLikeCommand` line 153) and with its own setter's parameter name (`setCurrentItemLiked(_ isLiked: Bool)` line 218). It reads as a noun phrase ("the liked item") rather than a state flag.
findings.suggestion: Rename the internal property to isCurrentItemLiked (the public method setCurrentItemLiked(_:) documented in AGENTS.md stays unchanged); update the handful of internal read sites in MusicPlayer.swift, MusicPlayer+Playback.swift, MusicPlayer+Restoration.swift, and RemoteCommandManager.swift.

findings.title: updateLikeCommand never clears localizedShortTitle when nil
