Production sites construct `.played(tracks.count)` / `.queued(resolvedItems.count)` — note the two cases are even fed from different collections (requested tracks vs resolved items), which an unlabeled Int does nothing to disambiguate.
findings.suggestion: Label the associated values: `case played(count: Int)` and `case queued(count: Int)`, updating the handful of construction/match sites.

findings.title: Repeated `.adHoc(name: "Queue")` empty-queue fallback in playNext and addToQueue
findings.detail: Lines 184-187 (playNext) and 218-221 (addToQueue) repeat the same fallback: `if player.queue.totalCount == 0 { let started = await play(tracks: tracks, source: .adHoc(name: "Queue")) ... }`, including the bare string literal "Queue" twice. The literal is internal-only (grep confirms the adHoc name is never rendered in UI, only Codable round-tripped), but it is still an uncentralized repeated literal plus a duplicated branch that must stay in sync across both queue-mutation entry points.
findings.suggestion: Hoist the literal to a named constant (e.g. `static let adHocQueueSourceName = "Queue"` next to queueItemIDPrefix) and/or extract a `startAdHocQueuePlayback(_ tracks:) async -> Bool` helper used by both methods.

findings.title: `_ = await player.seek(to: 0)` discards a Void result
findings.detail: MusicPlayer.seek is declared `func seek(to seconds: TimeInterval) async` with no return value (MuseAmpPlayerKit/Sources/MuseAmpPlayerKit/Player/MusicPlayer+Playback.swift:82). The `_ =` in restartCurrentTrack falsely signals to readers that a meaningful result (e.g. a success Bool) is being deliberately ignored. The other call site in this same file (line 370, inside seek(to:)) calls it bare, so the two sites are also inconsistent with each other.
findings.suggestion: Drop the `_ =` and call `await player.seek(to: 0)` to match line 370.

findings.title: seek(to:) uses Task + MainActor.run instead of the established Task { @MainActor } pattern
findings.detail: seek(to:) spawns `Task { [weak self] in ... await MainActor.run { self.refreshSnapshot(...) } }` (lines 368-374). PlaybackController is @MainActor, so the unstructured Task already inherits main-actor isolation, making the inner `await MainActor.run` a redundant no-op hop; the established pattern everywhere else in this class (init at lines 100 and 111, restartCurrentTrack at line 273, the snapshot timer at +Snapshot.swift:156) is `Task { @MainActor [weak self] in }` with direct calls. The divergent shape suggests a threading subtlety that does not exist.
findings.suggestion: Rewrite as `Task { @MainActor [weak self] in guard let self else { return }; await player.seek(to: targetTime); refreshSnapshot(currentTime: targetTime, duration: player.duration, persistState: true) }`, matching restartCurrentTrack.

findings.title: formattedDuration reimplements formattedPlaybackTime with divergent edge behavior
findings.detail: `formattedDuration(millis:)` and `formattedDuration(seconds:)` (lines 22-28) hand-roll minute:second formatting that formattedPlaybackTime (lines 10-20) already implements, and the copies diverge at the edges: formattedPlaybackTime clamps negatives to 0 and rolls hours (3700s → "1:01:40"), while formattedDuration(seconds: 3700) yields "61:40" and formattedDuration(seconds: -5) yields "0:-5". So the same duration can render differently in a list trailing-text versus the player transport, and negative/garbage input is only defended in one of the three functions.
findings.suggestion: Implement both formattedDuration overloads in terms of formattedPlaybackTime (e.g. `formattedDuration(seconds:) = formattedPlaybackTime(TimeInterval(seconds))` and the millis overload dividing by 1000), or extract one shared core; if list cells must never show hour-rollover, encode that as an explicit option rather than a silent divergence.

findings.title: Three hand-rolled variants of the artist–album subtitle join in one file
findings.detail: The same 'join non-empty artist and album with " · "' composition is implemented three different ways in three sibling inits: line 26 uses `[artistName, albumTitle].filter { !$0.isEmpty }.joined(separator: " · ")`, lines 37-44 use a manual if-let with string interpolation `"\(artistName) · \(albumName)"`, and lines 55-60 use a compactMap-with-guard variant. The " · " literal is repeated three times and the empty/optional handling rules differ subtly per copy, so a fourth conversion author has three competing templates to pick from.
findings.suggestion: Add one private helper in this file, e.g. `private func songSubtitle(artist: String, album: String?) -> String`, that filters empty components and joins with a single named " · " separator constant, and use it from all three inits.

findings.title: Speculative artworkWidth/artworkHeight parameters never used non-default
findings.detail: downloadRequest(albumID:apiClient:artworkWidth:artworkHeight:) exposes `artworkWidth: Int = 600, artworkHeight: Int = 600`, but the only call sites in the repo (MuseAmp/Interface/Browse/AlbumDetail/AlbumDetailViewController.swift:225 and :270) use the defaults. AGENTS.md fixes the standard artwork size at 600×600 for downloads, so the parameters invite divergence from a documented invariant while buying nothing; the sibling conversion PlaylistEntry.downloadRequest (PlaylistSong+AppModels.swift:19) hardcodes 600×600 with no such knobs, so the two adapters also disagree on shape.
findings.suggestion: Delete the artworkWidth/artworkHeight parameters and call apiClient.mediaURL(from:width: 600, height: 600) directly, matching the PlaylistEntry adapter and the AGENTS.md standard size.

findings.title: Only CatalogSong→cell conversion that skips sanitizedTrackTitle
findings.detail: This init passes `title: catalogSong.attributes.name` raw, while every sibling conversion of the same kind of title field applies `.sanitizedTrackTitle`: SongRowContent+AppModels.swift lines 15, 25, 47, 63 (including the conversion from the very same `catalogSong.attributes.name` at line 15) and AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshot+AppModels.swift line 17. If the album-detail screen intentionally shows the unsanitized full title, nothing in the file says so; as written it reads as an omission, and the same song renders with a different title in album detail versus search/song rows.
findings.suggestion: Apply `.sanitizedTrackTitle` to match the siblings, or if the raw title is deliberate in album-detail context, add the one clarifying comment AGENTS.md permits for real ambiguity.

findings.title: Static factory not make-prefixed and shadows the stored property name
findings.detail: `static func preferredFileBaseName(artistName:title:fallbackBaseName:)` has exactly the same name as the stored property it initializes (`preferredFileBaseName = Self.preferredFileBaseName(...)` at line 39), forcing the `Self.` disambiguation and making 'preferredFileBaseName' refer to two different things within one init. The Playback/Models scope's established factory convention is a make- prefix (makePlayerItem, makeQueuedPlayerItem, makeQueueItemID, makePersistedSession, makePersistedTrack), which this helper ignores.
findings.suggestion: Rename the static helper to `makePreferredFileBaseName(artistName:title:fallbackBaseName:)`, removing the property/function name collision and matching the local make- factory convention.

findings.title: Inline magic string "HDMI" in port-type mapping
findings.detail: The default branch of `kind(for:)` reads `portType.rawValue == "HDMI" ? .television : .unknown`. Every other port in the switch is matched via the typed `AVAudioSession.Port` constants, so dropping to a raw-string comparison for one case is jarring and unexplained — a reader cannot tell whether `.HDMI` was unavailable on a deployment target, deprecated, or simply forgotten, and the bare literal is invisible to symbol search.
findings.suggestion: Match `AVAudioSession.Port.HDMI` directly if it is available on all build targets; otherwise hoist the literal into a named private constant (e.g. `private static let hdmiPortRawValue = "HDMI"`) with a one-line comment stating why the typed constant cannot be used.

findings.title: "Sort by Recently Modified" actually sorts albums by title
findings.detail: SortOption.recentlyModified is titled "Sort by Recently Modified" (line 42), but its applySort branch (lines 395-398) sorts by `albumTitle.localizedCaseInsensitiveCompare` — byte-identical to the `.album` sort minus the artist tiebreak. AlbumGroup (MuseAmpDatabaseKit/Sources/MuseAmpDatabaseKit/Models/AlbumGroup.swift) carries no modification date, so the case silently degrades to an alphabetical sort while the menu still shows the option as a distinct, selectable state. SongsViewController honors the same option name with a real `fileModifiedAt` sort (SongsViewController.swift:382), so the two screens disagree on what the identically-named option means.
findings.suggestion: Either surface a real recency value on AlbumGroup (e.g. max track fileModifiedAt aggregated in the DB query) and sort by it, or remove `.recentlyModified` from SongLibraryViewController.SortOption until the data exists. At minimum add a comment-free honest implementation; do not keep a menu option whose behavior contradicts its name.

findings.title: Audio import flow duplicated verbatim across Songs and Albums controllers
findings.detail: `importTapped`/`documentPicker`/`performImport`/`showImportResult` in SongsViewController.swift (lines 463-522) are a near line-for-line copy of SongLibraryViewController+Actions.swift (lines 25-78): same UIDocumentPickerViewController setup, same AlertProgressIndicatorViewController titles, same `Importing \(current) / \(total)...` progress purpose, and the identical four-line result summary alert. Any change to the import UX or its localized strings must be made twice and can silently drift.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared presenter (e.g. `AudioImportPresenter` in Interface/Common/Presenters/ holding a weak viewController plus the AudioFileImporter) that owns the picker, the progress alert, and the result alert; both controllers keep only a one-line call plus their post-import reload.

findings.title: createPlaylistFromSelection and buildEditingMenu duplicated across Songs and Albums controllers
findings.detail: `createPlaylistFromSelection` (lines 73-94) duplicates SongLibraryViewController+Actions.swift lines 221-242 except for the prefilled text: same AlertInputViewController titles/placeholder, same trim+guard+AppLog.warning, same createPlaylist+addSong loop. `buildEditingMenu` (lines 100-162) likewise mirrors SongLibraryViewController+Actions.swift lines 252-329 (same add-to-playlist gating, New Playlist / Export Selected / Copy submenu / Delete Selected structure and section assembly).
findings.suggestion: Move the name-prompt-then-create-playlist flow into a shared presenter or into AddToPlaylistMenuProvider (which already owns playlist UI), parameterized by an entries provider and optional default name; build the shared editing-menu skeleton in one menu-builder helper that takes the per-screen copy actions.

findings.title: Song-tap playback policy reimplemented inline in two controllers
findings.detail: `playTrack(_:)` (lines 100-126) and AlbumDetailViewController+Table.swift `playTrack(at:)` (lines 84-105) both hand-roll the AGENTS "Playback Interaction Rules" policy: check `latestSnapshot.state == .playing || .buffering`, rewind via `seek(to: 0)` when the tapped track is current, otherwise `playNext` and switch on `.alreadyQueued` -> `next()` / `.queued` -> toast. The policy is duplicated logic-for-logic at two call sites (the only two that switch on `alreadyQueued` outside PlaybackFeedbackPresenter), so a future rule change must be found and edited in both.
findings.suggestion: Add one canonical entry point, e.g. `PlaybackController.handleTrackTap(track:in:source:)` (Backend/Playback owns playback state per AGENTS) that encodes the tap policy and returns/presents the feedback; both table delegates call it.

findings.title: loadTracks repeats the failure path four times and force-unwraps after a boolean check
findings.detail: Inside `loadTracks()` the block `if !hasExistingTracks { isLoadingTracks = false; applySnapshot() }` appears four times (lines 552-556, 562-565, 576-580, 591-594). Line 532 computes `hasExistingTracks` as `album.relationships?.tracks?.data.isEmpty == false` and line 536 then force-unwraps `album.relationships!.tracks!.data` — the optional chain is checked via a Bool and re-unwrapped with `!` instead of being bound once. The function also interleaves three phases (local-tracks fast path, song->album resolution, album fetch) at ~70 lines.
findings.suggestion: Bind the optional once (`if let existing = album.relationships?.tracks?.data, !existing.isEmpty { ... setTracks(existing) }`) eliminating both force unwraps, and extract a `finishLoadingWithoutTracks()` helper for the repeated failure block; optionally split the async body into `resolveAlbumFromPendingSong()` and `fetchFullAlbum()`.

findings.title: Skeleton row count is a bare magic number
findings.detail: `applySnapshot()` builds the loading state with `let count = 64` (line 440) — a magic literal with a meaningless name (`count`) that does not explain why 64 skeleton rows are appended while tracks load.
findings.suggestion: Hoist to a named constant near the type's other configuration, e.g. `private static let skeletonRowCount = 64`, so the intent (fill the viewport with placeholder rows) is named at its declaration.

findings.title: Artwork URL resolved via Artwork.imageURL instead of apiClient.mediaURL
findings.detail: `exportItem(for:)` builds the artwork URL with `track.attributes.artwork?.imageURL(width: 600, height: 600)` (line 285). This is the only place in the app target that calls `imageURL(width:height:)` directly; every other site, including line 376 of this same file, resolves template URLs through `apiClient.mediaURL(from:width:height:)` as the AGENTS Artwork URL Rules require ("Always resolve via apiClient.mediaURL(from:width:height:)").
findings.suggestion: Replace with `environment.apiClient.mediaURL(from: track.attributes.artwork?.url, width: 600, height: 600)` to keep one canonical resolution path.

findings.title: Redundant per-dequeue selectionStyle assignments fight TableBaseCell
findings.detail: The cell provider sets `cell.selectionStyle = .none` on AlbumHeaderCell (line 381), the skeleton cell (line 389) and DetailFooterCell (line 422) even though all three inherit TableBaseCell, whose init already sets `selectionStyle = .none` and hides `selectedBackgroundView`. AGENTS Cell Rules state the gray highlight is controlled only by `selectedBackgroundView` visibility, so these reassignments are dead configuration noise repeated on every dequeue.
findings.suggestion: Delete the three `cell.selectionStyle = .none` lines; keep only the `isUserInteractionEnabled` toggles that actually differ per row kind.

findings.title: Optional-environment dependency lattice is dead generality
findings.detail: The init accepts `playlistStore: PlaylistStore? = nil` AND `environment: AppEnvironment? = nil`, resolving `playlistStore ?? environment?.playlistStore` (line 76), and the optional environment cascades into five more optionals: `playlistMenuProvider`, `availablePlaylists` closure (lines 52-53), `albumNavigationHelper` (line 56), `lyricsReloadPresenter` (line 60) and `showInAlbum: environment == nil ? nil : ...` (line 419). Both real call sites pass a non-nil environment (Interface/Root/MainController.swift:373 and Interface/Settings/SettingsViewController+Actions.swift:55-59, the latter also redundantly passing playlistStore), so every nil branch is unreachable speculative wiring.
findings.suggestion: Make `environment: AppEnvironment` required, derive playlistStore from it, drop the separate parameter, and make the five derived dependencies non-optional lazy properties — matching how SongsViewController/SongLibraryViewController wire the same providers.

findings.title: Task-state predicates duplicated between menu builder and BarMenuState
findings.detail: `currentTasks.contains { $0.state != .failed }` appears at line 166 (buildMenuElements) and line 317 (currentBarMenuState); `currentTasks.contains { $0.state == .waitingForNetwork }` appears at line 187 and line 318. The two copies must stay in sync for the BarMenuState equality short-circuit (line 143) to correctly decide when the menu needs rebuilding.
findings.suggestion: Introduce computed properties (`private var hasActiveTasks: Bool`, `private var hasTasksWaitingForNetwork: Bool`) and use them in both buildMenuElements and currentBarMenuState.

findings.title: refreshVisibleCells resolves rows by index despite ID-keyed data source
findings.detail: `refreshVisibleCells()` (lines 324-335) maps `indexPath.row` into `currentTasks`, while the diffable source is keyed by trackID and the controller already maintains `tasksByTrackID` exactly for dequeue-time resolution (line 247). Correctness currently rests on the subtle invariant that `currentTasks` order always equals the applied snapshot order (guarded by `identityChanged` in renderCurrentTasks); resolving by ID would make the update self-evidently safe.
findings.suggestion: Use `diffableDataSource.itemIdentifier(for: indexPath)` and look up `tasksByTrackID[trackID]` instead of `currentTasks[indexPath.row]`, the same scheme the cell provider uses.

findings.title: ShineBarView.swift contains no ShineBarView — only a dead duplicate badge factory
findings.detail: The file declares a single global free function `makeAlbumBadgeView(text:icon:)` and no `ShineBarView` type (the real shimmer view lives in Interface/Collections/SkeletonShineBarView.swift). A repo-wide grep finds zero callers of `makeAlbumBadgeView`, and its body is a copy of the private `makeAudioTraitBadge` in Interface/Common/DetailFooterCell.swift:90-115 (same 12pt icon, 11pt semibold label, 3/8 layout margins, 0.1-alpha tint background, radius 10). Dead code under a misleading filename.
findings.suggestion: Delete ShineBarView.swift entirely (the function is unused). If album badges are needed later, reuse/expose the existing makeAudioTraitBadge from DetailFooterCell instead of resurrecting a copy.

findings.title: Silent catch swallows database error without logging
findings.detail: `localCatalogAlbum(albumID:albumName:artistName:)` wraps `environment.databaseManager.tracks(inAlbumID:)` in `do { ... } catch { return nil }` (lines 76-80) with no AppLog call. AGENTS Logging Rules require every catch that silently swallows an error to log via AppLog.error or AppLog.warning; here a DB failure silently downgrades navigation to a stub album and the diagnostic trail is lost. Every comparable query in this scope logs (e.g. SongLibraryViewController+Table.swift:203, SongLibraryViewController+Actions.swift:144).
findings.suggestion: Log before returning: `catch { AppLog.error(self, "localCatalogAlbum tracks query failed albumID=\(albumID) error=\(error)"); return nil }`.

findings.title: Direct UIView.transition call bypasses the Interface animation wrapper
findings.detail: `animateTextTransition` calls `UIView.transition(with:duration:options:animations:)` directly (lines 53-58), violating the AGENTS Animation Rule "Never call UIView.animate or UIView.transition directly" — and it shows why the rule exists: the hand-built options list includes `.beginFromCurrentState` but omits `.allowUserInteraction`, which `Interface.transition` (Interface/Common/Style/Interface.swift:91-105) would have applied automatically. Line 8 additionally calls `layer.removeAllAnimations()` in the `disablesAnimations` didSet, which the same rules forbid.
findings.suggestion: Replace with `Interface.transition(with: self, duration: textChangeAnimationDuration, options: [.transitionCrossDissolve, .allowAnimatedContent], animations: {})` and drop the removeAllAnimations call (gate new transitions on `disablesAnimations` instead).

findings.title: try? FileManager.removeItem swallows deletion errors without logging
findings.detail: Two `try? FileManager.default.removeItem(at:)` calls silently discard errors: line 22 (deinit cleanup of the previous preview file) and line 41 (replacing a stale preview file in `present`). AGENTS Logging Rules require every error-swallowing `try?` to log via AppLog, and file I/O deletes specifically must log failures via AppLog.error. The same class already logs its write failures (lines 69, 77), so the delete paths are inconsistently silent.
findings.suggestion: Convert line 41 to do/catch with `AppLog.warning(self, "failed to remove stale preview file error=...")`; for the deinit path, log via a nonisolated-safe AppLog call or schedule cleanup somewhere it can be observed.

findings.title: Preview temp-file writing duplicated with MuseAmpImageView+Preview
findings.detail: `makePreviewFileURL(image:fileName:preferredExtension:)` (lines 52-80) reimplements the logic of MuseAmpImageView+Preview.swift `makePreviewFileURL(from:sourceURL:)` (lines 39-69): identical png-vs-jpeg(0.98) encoding choice, temporaryDirectory + UUID filename, atomic write, and the same AppLog warning/error messages ("makePreviewFileURL no data generated" / "write failed"). Both files also each carry their own QLPreviewControllerDataSource with the `previewItemURL! as NSURL` force unwrap (line 89 here, line 95 there).
findings.suggestion: Extract one helper (e.g. `enum ImagePreviewFileWriter { static func writeTemporaryFile(image:baseName:preferredExtension:) -> URL? }`) in Interface/Common and have both call sites use it; consider sharing the single-item QLPreviewControllerDataSource too.

findings.title: UIButton configuration built three times with ~10 identical lines each
findings.detail: `applyButtonStyle(to:filled:)` (lines 167-210) contains two branches whose bodies differ only in `.filled()` vs `.gray()` and two color lines, while the remaining configuration (imagePadding 4, capsule corner, medium size, 13pt semibold symbol config, 15pt semibold title transformer) is repeated verbatim in both branches AND a third time in `makeActionButton(systemImage:)` (lines 212-232). Any style tweak requires three synchronized edits.
findings.suggestion: Create one `makeButtonConfiguration(filled: Bool, title: String?, image: UIImage?) -> UIButton.Configuration` that sets the shared attributes once and switches only the base style/colors; both applyButtonStyle and makeActionButton call it.

findings.title: +Table file is a grab-bag: table delegate plus playback, deletion, menus, and search
findings.detail: Despite the repo's responsibility-based split convention (AGENTS UIKit File Rules; SongLibraryViewController has dedicated +Search.swift, and SongsViewController+Actions.swift already exists), this +Table file contains the UITableViewDelegate conformance (lines 14-95), a "Playback & Navigation" extension with playTrack/openAlbumForTrack/confirmDeleteTrack/buildContextMenu/makeRepairArtworkAction (lines 99-209), and the entire UISearchResultsUpdating + performSearch debounce implementation (lines 213-255). The filename promises table wiring but hides three other responsibilities.
findings.suggestion: Move UISearchResultsUpdating/performSearch into SongsViewController+Search.swift (mirroring SongLibraryViewController+Search.swift) and the playback/menu/delete helpers into +Actions.swift or a +Playback.swift, leaving only delegate methods here.

findings.title: Debounced local search implemented twice with divergent guards
findings.detail: SongsViewController's performSearch (lines 230-254) and SongLibraryViewController+Search.swift performSearch (lines 31-63) implement the same 300ms-debounced DB search with gratuitous differences: Songs guards staleness only via `Task.isCancelled` while SongLibrary re-checks `currentQuery == query`; Songs uses `try? await Task.sleep` then a separate isCancelled check while SongLibrary uses `do/catch { return }`; Songs propagates the search error from Task.detached and logs with `self`, SongLibrary logs inside the detached closure with a string tag; results land in `searchTracksByID` vs reused `tracksByID`. Neither follows the AGENTS Search Rule of separate `debounceTask`/`searchTask` properties. The drift makes it impossible to tell which differences are intentional.
findings.suggestion: Pick one canonical shape (query-equality recheck plus the documented debounceTask/searchTask split) and apply it to both controllers, or extract a small generic `DebouncedLibrarySearch` helper in Interface/Browse/Support that both reuse.

findings.title: Search-highlight font literals silently coupled to AmSongCell's fonts
findings.detail: `configureLibrarySearchResultCell` passes `.systemFont(ofSize: 16)` / `.systemFont(ofSize: 13)` to SearchHighlightHelper (lines 360, 366) so the attributed strings match AmSongCell's private titleLabel/subtitleLabel fonts (AmSongCell.swift lines 25, 32). The same 16/13 literals are re-typed in Interface/Playlist/PlaylistSearchCell.swift (lines 37, 48) and Interface/Search/SearchViewController.swift. If the cell font changes, every highlight call site drifts and the bold-highlight rendering subtly mismatches the plain text.
findings.suggestion: Expose the fonts where they are owned, e.g. `static let titleFont` / `static let subtitleFont` on AmSongCell (or in InterfaceStyle), and reference those constants from all SearchHighlightHelper call sites.

findings.title: Stored hasAccessory flag duplicates view-hierarchy state
findings.detail: `private var hasAccessory = false` (line 16) only tracks whether `accessoryButton` has been added to contentView (lines 34-42). That fact is already available as `accessoryButton.superview != nil`, so the flag violates the AGENTS Property Rule "Do not introduce stored properties to track state that is already available from an existing source of truth" and can desynchronize if the hierarchy ever changes.
findings.suggestion: Drop the flag and guard with `if accessoryButton.superview == nil { contentView.addSubview(...); accessoryButton.snp.makeConstraints { ... } }` — or pre-add the button in init and toggle isHidden, per the pre-alloc convention.

findings.title: PreviewCoordinator models one lifecycle with three independent optionals
findings.detail: PreviewCoordinator (lines 79-97) holds `previewItemURL`, `pendingImage`, `pendingSourceURL` — three optionals that together encode a single progression (empty -> image loaded awaiting file materialization -> preview file ready). The valid combinations are implicit, and `previewController(_:previewItemAt:)` force-unwraps `previewItemURL!` (line 95) relying on the numberOfPreviewItems contract holding across mutations like `clear()`.
findings.suggestion: Model the lifecycle as one enum, e.g. `enum PreviewState { case empty; case loaded(image: UIImage, sourceURL: URL); case ready(fileURL: URL) }`, which removes the force unwrap and makes illegal combinations unrepresentable.

findings.title: Dead `_ = viewController` statements after guard
findings.detail: Both run() overloads end their dismiss completions with `guard let viewController else { return }` followed by an unused-binding suppressor `_ = viewController` (lines 30, 38, 66). The binding is never actually used; the `_ =` exists only to silence the unused-variable warning, leaving three lines of hand-fought noise that read as if they do something.
findings.suggestion: Replace each `guard let viewController else { return }` + `_ = viewController` pair with `guard viewController != nil else { return }`.

findings.title: Track numbers above 50 silently render no number glyph
findings.detail: `trackNumberImage(_:)` builds the icon from the SF Symbol name `"\(number).circle.fill"` (lines 199-202). The numbered circle.fill symbols only exist for 0-50, so `UIImage(systemName:)` returns nil for larger track numbers (long compilations, multi-disc sets) and the cell shows an empty gap where the number should be, with no fallback or log.
findings.suggestion: Fall back when the symbol is missing, e.g. return a text-drawn number image or a generic `circle.fill` for `number > 50`, making the limit explicit in code.

findings.title: Assigning onImageLoaded has a hidden replay side effect
findings.detail: The `onImageLoaded` property's didSet (lines 30-35) immediately invokes the newly assigned closure with the current URL/image. Setting a callback property normally just stores it; here assignment triggers a callback execution, which is invisible at every call site (`imageView.onImageLoaded = { ... }` looks like pure configuration but may run synchronously). The replay-on-assign semantics are encoded nowhere in the name.
findings.suggestion: Replace the property observer trick with an explicit method whose name carries the behavior, e.g. `func setOnImageLoaded(_ handler:, replayingCurrentImage: Bool = true)`, or keep the stored property plain and let callers query current state.

findings.title: Force-cast cell dequeues inconsistent with sibling controllers' guarded pattern
findings.detail: The cell provider force-casts `as! AmMediaCell` (line 284) and `as! AmSongCell` (line 302) (SongsViewController.swift:275 does the same), whereas AlbumDetailViewController (lines 371-374, 394-399) and DownloadsViewController (lines 246-254) dequeue the same way but use `guard let ... as? ... else { return UITableViewCell() }`. Two crash-on-misregistration styles coexist for the identical operation within the same browse scope.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one dequeue idiom across the browse controllers — either the guarded `as?` + fallback used by AlbumDetail/Downloads, or SwifterSwift's `dequeueReusableCell(withClass:for:)` everywhere.

findings.title: Dead background-URLSession plumbing in AppDelegate
findings.detail: AppDelegate stores a `backgroundCompletionHandler` in `application(_:handleEventsForBackgroundURLSession:completionHandler:)` (line 207) and exposes `urlSessionDidFinishEvents(forBackgroundURLSession:)` (line 215) to fire it. Repo-wide greps show: (1) no `URLSession(configuration:` / `URLSessionConfiguration.background` exists anywhere in MuseAmp, MuseAmpDatabaseKit, MuseAmpPlayerKit, or SubsonicClientKit, so the system will never call the handleEvents callback; (2) `urlSessionDidFinishEvents` has zero callers and AppDelegate does not conform to URLSessionDelegate, so the stored handler can never be invoked even if it were set. This is speculative seam/dead plumbing that misleads readers into believing background downloads exist.
findings.suggestion: Delete the `// MARK: - Background URL Session` block (lines 203-224). If background download sessions are a planned feature, add the plumbing in the same change that introduces a background URLSessionConfiguration in DownloadManager.

findings.title: hasExecutingTasks name hides that playback alone triggers the quit prompt
findings.detail: `private var hasExecutingTasks` returns `MacCatalystTerminationPolicy.shouldConfirmTermination(for:hasExecutingDownloads:)`, which is true while audio is merely playing or buffering — not an "executing task". It also shadows `environment.downloadManager.hasExecutingTasks` (used on line 125), which has the narrower downloads-only meaning. In `requestProtectedTermination` (line 130), `guard hasExecutingTasks else { action(); return }` reads as "no tasks running, exit", obscuring that playback state is part of the gate.
findings.suggestion: Rename the AppDelegate property to `shouldConfirmTermination` or `requiresExitConfirmation` so it matches the policy method it wraps and no longer collides with DownloadManager.hasExecutingTasks.

findings.title: terminateApplication uses unexplained suspend trick and magic delays
findings.detail: The non-Catalyst branch sends `#selector(NSXPCConnection.suspend)` to UIApplication (a selector-smuggling trick to suspend the app gracefully), then sleeps 1 second in a detached task before `exit(0)`, then `sleep(5)` + `fatalError()` as a watchdog (lines 231-237). None of this is explained, and the 1s/5s literals are unnamed. AGENTS.md says comments belong exactly where they remove real ambiguity — this is that case.
findings.suggestion: Name the delays (e.g. `private let exitGraceDelay: Duration = .seconds(1)`, `watchdogSeconds: UInt32 = 5`) and add a one-line comment explaining the suspend-then-exit sequence (suspend to background gracefully, exit after the OS snapshot, fatalError if exit never happens).

findings.title: configureImageRequestAuthorization does not configure authorization
findings.detail: The method body sets Kingfisher memory/disk cache limits (lines 132-135) and rewrites `KingfisherManager.shared.defaultOptions` to REMOVE any `.requestModifier` (the mechanism authorization would use) and append `.backgroundDecode` (lines 137-143). Nothing authorization-related is configured — if anything, authorization is stripped. The name actively misleads anyone searching for where image requests get auth headers.
findings.suggestion: Rename to something honest such as `configureImageCacheAndRequestOptions()` (or split cache limits and option rewriting into two named steps), keeping the call site in `AppEnvironment.init` updated.

findings.title: Bootstrap creates parallel APIClient and EmbeddedMetadataReader instances that diverge from AppEnvironment's
findings.detail: `initializeDatabaseManagerSynchronously` builds its own `apiClient` (line 20) and `metadataReader` (line 21) which are captured forever by the RuntimeDependencies closures handed to DatabaseManager. `AppEnvironment.init` then builds a second `apiClient` (AppEnvironment.swift:59) and second `metadataReader` (AppEnvironment.swift:62) used by everything else. Two live instances per behavior means any future mutable state on APIClient (auth, base-URL switching) silently diverges between the database runtime and the app. AGENTS.md sanctions the closure-based RuntimeDependencies pattern, but not duplicate dependency instances behind it.
findings.suggestion: Have the bootstrap factory accept or return the canonical instances — e.g. return `(manager, apiClient, metadataReader)` and pass them into `AppEnvironment.init(databaseManager:apiClient:metadataReader:)` so RuntimeDependencies closures capture the same objects AppEnvironment stores.

findings.title: 40-line inspectAudioFile closure mixes factory orchestration with raw path/attribute parsing
findings.detail: Inside `makeRuntimeDependencies`, the `inspectAudioFile` closure (lines 83-122) does relative-path splitting with `split(separator:maxSplits:)`, trackID derivation from path components, FileManager attribute extraction, and a 15-field `ImportedTrackMetadata` assembly — all inline in the dependency-bag factory next to one-line closures like `fetchLyrics`. The factory's abstraction level (wire dependencies) is buried under low-level parsing detail.
findings.suggestion: Extract a `private static func inspectAudioFile(at fileURL: URL, paths: LibraryPaths, metadataReader: EmbeddedMetadataReader) async throws -> AudioFileInspection` and reduce the closure to a one-line forward. The AGENTS-sanctioned closure-based RuntimeDependencies shape is preserved.

findings.title: Anonymous NSError(domain:code:) carries no failure description
findings.detail: `resolveDownloadURL` throws `NSError(domain: "AppEnvironment", code: 1)` when the playback URL string fails to parse. The error has no userInfo/localizedDescription, so logs and user-facing failure paths downstream show an opaque "AppEnvironment error 1" with no hint that the server returned an unparseable playbackURL.
findings.suggestion: Throw a descriptive error, e.g. `NSError(domain: "AppEnvironment", code: 1, userInfo: [NSLocalizedDescriptionKey: "Invalid playback URL for track \(trackID)"])` or a small dedicated error enum.

findings.title: Strong self captured in .sink violates the explicit weak-self Combine rule
findings.detail: `observeDatabaseEvents` subscribes with `.sink { event in self.handleDatabaseEvent(event) }` — a strong `self` capture stored into `self.cancellables`, creating a retain cycle (self -> cancellables -> closure -> self). AGENTS.md Combine Observation Rules state unconditionally: "Always capture `[weak self]` in `.sink` closures". AppEnvironment is app-lifetime in production, but the type takes a `baseDirectory` parameter for test instances, which would leak.
findings.suggestion: Use `.sink { [weak self] event in self?.handleDatabaseEvent(event) }`.

findings.title: librarySummary swallows the thrown error's content from the log
findings.detail: The catch block logs `"libraryDatabase.storedLibrarySummary() threw - returning empty summary"` without interpolating the caught error, so the diagnostic trail (required by AGENTS.md Logging Rules for swallowed errors) records that something failed but not why. Contrast with `refreshTrackTitleSanitizer` (line 159) in the same file, which logs `error=\(error)`.
findings.suggestion: Include the error: `AppLog.warning(self, "storedLibrarySummary failed, returning empty summary error=\(error)")`.

findings.title: Async initializeDatabaseManager wrapper relies on invisible executor hopping
findings.detail: `initializeDatabaseManager` (lines 46-54) is an async function whose entire body is a call to `initializeDatabaseManagerSynchronously`. Its only value is that a nonisolated async function leaves the caller's actor, so BootProgressController's main-actor `Task` (BootProgressController.swift:58-64) doesn't block the main thread during synchronous DB init. Nothing in the code says this; a maintainer could "simplify" by inlining the sync call into the Task and silently move heavy WCDB init onto the main thread — and under Swift 6.2's NonisolatedNonsendingByDefault the wrapper itself would stop hopping.
findings.suggestion: Make the off-main intent explicit: either wrap the body in `try await Task.detached { try initializeDatabaseManagerSynchronously(...) }.value`, or add a one-line comment stating the function exists to move blocking init off the caller's actor.

findings.title: defaultAPIBaseURL is a sentinel placeholder, not a default
findings.detail: `defaultAPIBaseURL = URL(string: "https://example.com/rest")!` is the RFC-2606 example domain — it can never serve requests. The name "default" suggests a working fallback server, and it flows into `AppEnvironment.init(apiBaseURL: AppPreferences.defaultAPIBaseURL)` and `effectiveAPIBaseURL(fallback:)`, where a reader must discover that the "default" is actually an inert placeholder used when no Subsonic server is configured.
findings.suggestion: Rename to `placeholderAPIBaseURL` (or `unconfiguredAPIBaseURL`) so call sites read honestly: `effectiveAPIBaseURL` returns the configured server or a known-inert placeholder.

findings.title: Pending-import drain logic duplicated between drainPendingImports and scheduleCoalescedImport
findings.detail: Lines 161-183 (`drainPendingImports`) and lines 237-255 (inside `scheduleCoalescedImport`) contain the same five-step sequence: copy the three pending URL arrays, `removeAll()` each, call `performFileImport`, `performPlaylistImport`, then the identical first-server-profile-only block including the duplicated `AppLog.warning(self, "Multiple server profile files received; importing the first one only")`. Any change to import dispatch (a fourth file type, different multi-profile policy) must be made twice.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single `@MainActor func dispatchPendingImports(to mainController: MainController)` that snapshots+clears the pending arrays and performs the three dispatches; call it from both paths.

findings.title: Inbound-URL classification chain duplicated between willConnectTo and openURLContexts
findings.detail: The four-way classification `if url.isFileURL, isImportableAudioFile(url) ... else if isImportablePlaylistFile ... else if isImportableServerProfileFile ... else if let receiverInfo = parseAppleTVURL(url)` appears verbatim at lines 48-59 and again at lines 94-105, differing only in which buffer/handler receives the result. Adding a new inbound URL kind requires editing both chains in lockstep.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single classifier, e.g. `enum InboundURL { case audio(URL), playlist(URL), serverProfile(URL), tvHandshake(SyncReceiverHandshakeInfo) }` with `func classify(_ url: URL) -> InboundURL?`, and switch on it in both scene callbacks.

findings.title: Importable audio extension list duplicated with AudioFileImporter
findings.detail: `Self.importableExtensions` (lines 138-140: mp3, m4a, flac, wav, aac, aiff, alac, ogg, wma, opus) is byte-for-byte duplicated as a local `audioExtensions` set inside MuseAmp/Backend/Library/AudioFileImporter.swift (lines 387-389). The two copies define the same concept — "what counts as an importable audio file" — and will drift if a format is added to only one.
findings.suggestion: Centralize as `static let importableAudioExtensions: Set<String>` on AudioFileImporter (the Backend owner of import behavior) and have SceneDelegate's `isImportableAudioFile` reference it.

findings.title: defer used for ordinary end-of-function window activation
findings.detail: Lines 63-66 use `defer { window.makeKeyAndVisible(); self.window = window }` purely to run after the `window.rootViewController = bootController` assignment at line 87. There are no early returns between the defer and the end of the function, so the defer is a non-linear ordering trick where straight-line statements at the end of the method would express the same sequence directly.
findings.suggestion: Delete the defer and place `window.makeKeyAndVisible()` and `self.window = window` after the `window.rootViewController = bootController` line.

findings.title: User-facing "Boot Failed" string is not localized
findings.detail: `statusLabel.text = "Boot Failed"` assigns a raw literal to a label shown to the user on boot failure. AGENTS.md Localization Rules require all user-facing strings in the app target to use `String(localized:)` with matching entries in MuseAmp/Resources/Localizable.xcstrings (en + zh-Hans).
findings.suggestion: Use `statusLabel.text = String(localized: "Boot Failed")` and add the key with en/zh-Hans localizations to MuseAmp/Resources/Localizable.xcstrings in the same change.

findings.title: RootDestination.library is a dead case threaded through three switches
findings.detail: No code ever constructs `RootDestination.library`: grep shows zero `.destination(.library)` or `RootDestination.library` construction sites — the only `.library` matches on this enum are its own switch arms (title line 27, imageName line 46) and `case .library, .albums:` in `contentNavigationController` (line 364), where it behaves identically to `.albums`. The dead case forces every switch over RootDestination to handle a destination that cannot occur and implies a distinct "Library" screen exists.
findings.suggestion: Remove `case library` from RootDestination and the corresponding arms in `title`, `imageName`, and `contentNavigationController(for:)`.

findings.title: selectDestination and openPlaylistDetail duplicate the popup-close + sidebar-collapse sequence
findings.detail: Lines 266-281 (`selectDestination`) and lines 287-301 (`openPlaylistDetail`) share a verbatim tail: close the open popup, install the nav controller, then the identical `if rootSplitViewController.displayMode == .oneOverSecondary { Interface.quickAnimate(duration: 0.25) { ...preferredDisplayMode = .secondaryOnly } completion: { ...preferredDisplayMode = .automatic } }` dance. The overlay-collapse animation trick is exactly the kind of subtle UIKit workaround that should exist once.
findings.suggestion: Extract a private `func presentContent(_ nav: UINavigationController)` (close popup, installContentNavigationController, collapse oneOverSecondary overlay) and call it from both methods after they set their selection state.

findings.title: Playlist tab located by comparing localized display titles
findings.detail: `revealPlaylistImportSurface` finds the compact Playlist tab via `compactTabBarController.viewControllers?.firstIndex(where: { $0.tabBarItem.title == playlistTitle })` where `playlistTitle = String(localized: "Playlist")` (lines 519-523). Tab identity is derived from presentation text: if the tab title wording or its localization changes, the import flow silently stops switching tabs. TabBarController already assigns stable identifiers (`identifier: "playlist"` for UITab, `tab.playlist` accessibility identifiers on the legacy path).
findings.suggestion: Expose a `func selectPlaylistTab()` on TabBarController that resolves the tab by its stable identifier (UITab `identifier == "playlist"` on iOS 18+, root VC `is PlaylistViewController` on legacy), and call that instead of title matching.

findings.title: Downloads badge target found by localized Settings title
findings.detail: `updateDownloadsBadge` locates the badge host with `tabBar.items?.first(where: { $0.title == String(localized: "Settings") })` (lines 227-231). Like MainController.swift:520, identity is derived from localized display text; if the Settings title changes or localizes differently the badge silently disappears (the guard just returns). The method name also hides that the downloads badge intentionally lives on the Settings tab — only reading the body reveals it.
findings.suggestion: Resolve the Settings tab by stable identity (UITab `identifier == "settings"` on iOS 18+, root `is SettingsViewController` on legacy), and log via AppLog.warning when the tab cannot be found instead of silently returning.

findings.title: Tab titles/icons re-stated and search-tab construction triplicated
findings.detail: Tab metadata pairs ("Albums"/square.stack, "Songs"/music.note, "Playlist"/music.note.list, "Settings"/gearshape, "Search"/magnifyingglass) are already encoded once in `RootDestination.title`/`imageName` (MainController.swift:25-61) yet re-stated literally in `setupWithUITab` (lines 110-172) and `setupWithViewControllers` (lines 190-198). Worse, the UISearchTab construction block (lines 163-172) is verbatim duplicated in `handleServerConfigurationDidChange` (lines 254-261), and the legacy search nav construction (lines 282-293) duplicates the `setupWithViewControllers` entry a fourth time. The 60-line `handleServerConfigurationDidChange` then re-implements insert/remove positioning for both API generations.
findings.suggestion: Extract `makeSearchTab()` (iOS 18+) and `makeSearchNavigationController()` (legacy) helpers used by both initial setup and the configuration-change handler, and drive titles/images from RootDestination instead of re-typed literals.

findings.title: Unlabeled 4-tuple of (UIViewController, String, String, String) for tab specs
findings.detail: `setupWithViewControllers` models tab specs as `[(UIViewController, String, String, String)]` — three positionally-distinguished strings (title, SF Symbol name, identifier). A reordered element compiles fine and produces a tab titled "square.stack". The destructure names at line 200 help, but the declaration itself conveys nothing.
findings.suggestion: Use a labeled tuple `(controller: UIViewController, title: String, systemImage: String, identifier: String)` or a tiny private struct TabSpec.

findings.title: Popup engine duplicated with TabBarController+Popup and already drifting
findings.detail: Roughly 200 lines are near-verbatim duplicates of TabBarController+Popup.swift: `updateNowPlayingPopupItem` (156-198 vs 128-169), `updatePopupProgress`, `popupProgress`/`progress(for:)`, `updatePopupBarButtonState`, `updatePopupArtwork` (225-262 vs 198-237), `retrievePopupArtwork`, and `buildPopupContextMenu` (351-399 vs 325-372). The copies have already drifted in subtle ways a reader cannot tell are intentional: (a) syncPopupPresentation here dismisses when state `!= .barHidden` and returns before updating the item (lines 106-111), while TabBar updates the item first and dismisses only when `== .barPresented` (TabBarController+Popup.swift:86-93); (b) TabBar resets `isNowPlayingPopupOpen = false` in willClose AND didClose (lines 290, 299) while this file does so only in didClose (line 331), so `childForStatusBarHidden` differs during the close animation; (c) TabBar's didClose also dismisses the bar when no track remains (lines 302-306) — absent here. The mapping notes call the duplication deliberate, but the drift shows the cost; the repo already proved the shared-seam pattern with PopupBarPagingHandler.
findings.suggestion: Extract the verbatim-identical parts (popup item updates, artwork loading/retrieval, progress math, bar-button state) into a shared @MainActor helper alongside PopupBarPagingHandler, parameterized with the popup container and content VC; keep only genuinely shell-specific presentation policy (dismiss conditions, lyricsActionsBeforeReload) in each controller — or document each intentional divergence at the divergent line.

findings.title: Popup userInfo keys "trackID"/"queueIndex" repeated as raw literals in three files
findings.detail: The string keys "queueIndex" and "trackID" form an implicit contract between producers (MainController+Popup.swift:183-184, TabBarController+Popup.swift:154-155, PopupBarPagingHandler.swift:82-83) and consumers (PopupBarPagingHandler.swift lines 32, 40, 50-51) — 10 raw-literal sites total. A typo in any one site silently breaks popup paging (the guards just return nil).
findings.suggestion: Define the keys once, e.g. `enum PopupItemUserInfoKey { static let trackID = "trackID"; static let queueIndex = "queueIndex" }` next to PopupBarPagingHandler, and use it at all 10 sites.

findings.title: 0.5s paging cooldown literal duplicated and enforced via perform(afterDelay:)
findings.detail: The cooldown interval appears twice and must stay in sync: `cooldownDate = Date().addingTimeInterval(0.5)` (line 55) and `perform(#selector(deferredSyncAfterPaging), with: nil, afterDelay: 0.5)` (line 63). If one changes without the other, the cooldown gate (`isCooldownActive`) and the deferred resync fire out of step. Additionally, the run-loop `NSObject.cancelPreviousPerformRequests` + `perform(afterDelay:)` pair is the only selector-timer in this scope; the repo's established cancellable-delay idiom is a stored Task with `Task.sleep` (AGENTS.md Search Rules; SceneDelegate.importCoalesceTask).
findings.suggestion: Hoist `private static let pagingCooldownInterval: TimeInterval = 0.5` and use it at both sites; optionally replace the perform(afterDelay:) machinery with a cancellable `Task` + `Task.sleep` to match the repo's debounce idiom.

findings.title: Popup-bar frame math duplicated with magic 3/5 width fraction
findings.detail: `popupBarLayoutFrameForPopupBar` (lines 8-14) and `animatePopupBarToCurrentLayout` (lines 24-28) independently re-derive sidebarWidth/availableWidth/`availableWidth * 3.0 / 5.0`/origin-x. If the two formulas drift, the animation lands the bar at a frame inconsistent with the layout override. The 3.0/5.0 fraction is unnamed in both places. (The file is also the only one in scope missing the standard `//  File.swift  MuseAmp` header comment.)
findings.suggestion: Extract `private func popupBarFrame(sidebarVisible: Bool) -> CGRect` used by both the override and the animator, with a named `private static let popupBarWidthFraction: CGFloat = 0.6`; add the standard file header.

findings.title: File named MainController+Sidebar contains no MainController extension
findings.detail: The file's 537 lines define `SidebarSection`, `SidebarItem`, the private `SidebarPlaylistCell`, the full `SidebarViewController` class (line 124), and its UICollectionViewDelegate extension — there is no `extension MainController` anywhere in it. The repo convention (and principle: filename = primary export) is that `Type+Feature.swift` holds an extension of Type; a reader looking for SidebarViewController.swift will not find it, and a reader opening this file expecting MainController code finds a different controller.
findings.suggestion: Rename to `SidebarViewController.swift` (keeping it in Interface/Root/), optionally splitting `SidebarPlaylistCell` into its own file; update the Xcode group to match per AGENTS.md.

findings.title: onImportPlaylistRequested is optional, deviating from the non-optional-callback rule
findings.detail: `var onImportPlaylistRequested: (() -> Void)?` sits beside three sibling callbacks declared per convention with empty defaults (`onDestinationSelected`, `onPlaylistSelected`, `onPlaylistsDidReload`, lines 127-129). It is always assigned before use (MainController.viewDidLoad line 179) and invoked with optional chaining `self?.onImportPlaylistRequested?()` (line 483). AGENTS.md Property Rules: callbacks always assigned before use should be non-optional with an empty default.
findings.suggestion: Declare `var onImportPlaylistRequested: () -> Void = {}` and drop the `?()` at the call site.

findings.title: Coupled magic numbers: 28pt cover size in three places, prefix(8)/prefix(7) playlist caps
findings.detail: The sidebar cover side length 28 appears at line 37 (spacerImage size), line 47 (imageProperties.maximumSize), and line 245 (`sideLength: 28` in the artwork fetch) — three sites that must agree or covers render misaligned/blurry. In `orderedSidebarPlaylists`, `prefix(8)` (line 440) and `prefix(7)` (line 446) encode the hidden relationship "8 rows max, liked-songs occupies one slot" with no name connecting them.
findings.suggestion: Hoist `private static let coverSideLength: CGFloat = 28` and `private static let maxSidebarPlaylists = 8`, deriving the second prefix as `maxSidebarPlaylists - 1`.

findings.title: Undocumented overlay hack on UIKit's internal list-cell image view
findings.detail: `SidebarPlaylistCell` renders a transparent `spacerImage` into the list content configuration (line 45), then in `layoutSubviews` recursively searches the contentView for UIKit's private internal UIImageView (`findInternalImageView`, lines 109-119) and pins two custom image views to its converted frame (lines 89-92). This depends on UIKit's private view hierarchy and on the spacer reserving layout space — genuinely ambiguous machinery with zero comments. AGENTS.md keeps comments rare but mandates them exactly where they remove real ambiguity.
findings.suggestion: Add a short comment above `findInternalImageView`/`spacerImage` explaining the technique (spacer reserves the sidebarCell image slot; custom views overlay it to support placeholder/cover crossfade) and why the configuration API alone is insufficient.

findings.title: cleanupInbox swallows per-file removal failures without logging
findings.detail: Inside `cleanupInbox`, `try? FileManager.default.removeItem(at: file)` (line 509) silently discards removal errors; the surrounding do/catch only logs `contentsOfDirectory` failures. AGENTS.md Logging Rules: every `try?` that silently swallows an error must log via AppLog.error or AppLog.warning, and file-delete failures in particular must be logged.
findings.suggestion: Replace with a do/catch per file: `do { try FileManager.default.removeItem(at: file) } catch { AppLog.warning("MainController", "cleanupInbox failed to remove \(file.lastPathComponent): \(error)") }`.

findings.title: UIImage constant homed on Bundle with a name that overpromises
findings.detail: `extension Bundle { static let appIcon: UIImage = .avatar }` puts an image constant on Bundle even though nothing about it is Bundle-related — it does not read the real app icon from the bundle's Info.plist; it just aliases the `Avatar` asset. Callers like `AlertControllerConfiguration.alertImage = Bundle.appIcon` (AppDelegate.swift:25) and the popup placeholder artwork read as "the bundle's app icon" when they actually get the avatar asset.
findings.suggestion: Move the constant to `extension UIImage { static let appIcon = UIImage.avatar }` in an Extension+UIImage.swift (or rename to reflect the asset, e.g. `placeholderArtwork`), and update the four call sites.

findings.title: shiftedHue captures alpha then silently discards it
findings.detail: `getHue(&hue, saturation:, brightness:, alpha: &alpha)` fills the local `alpha` (line 21), but the returned color hardcodes `alpha: 1` (line 33) and the failure fallback is `withAlphaComponent(1)` (line 22). The captured-but-unused `alpha` variable suggests preservation was intended; as written, the function has an invisible side effect — it strips translucency — that neither the name `shiftedHue(by:saturationMultiplier:brightnessMultiplier:)` nor any call-site hint conveys.
findings.suggestion: Either pass the captured value (`alpha: alpha`) to preserve translucency, or remove the unused capture (pass nil-equivalent and `alpha: 1`) and note the opaque-output behavior in the function name or a one-line comment.

findings.title: Asymmetric inout-threaded header/footer sizing API
findings.detail: `sizeHeaderToFit(widthConstraint: inout NSLayoutConstraint?, lastWidth: inout CGFloat)` forces every caller to own two pieces of bookkeeping state via inout threading, while the paired `sizeFooterToFit(widthConstraint:)` (line 20) threads only one and lacks the width-unchanged short-circuit — so headers skip relayout when width is stable but footers re-run `systemLayoutSizeFitting` on every call. The asymmetry is unexplained, and inout state-threading makes call sites noisy (`&headerWidthConstraint, &lastHeaderWidth`).
findings.suggestion: Unify the pair: give the footer variant the same lastWidth short-circuit, or wrap the bookkeeping into a tiny `TableAuxiliarySizer` value the controller stores once, exposing `sizeHeaderToFit(_:)`/`sizeFooterToFit(_:)` without inout parameters.

findings.title: Stack-scroll helper set copy-pasted across four Sync controllers with signature drift
findings.detail: The same private helpers are reimplemented per screen: addSectionHeader (SyncServerStatusViewController.swift:234, SyncReceiverViewController.swift:99, SyncTransferProgressViewController.swift:311, SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController.swift:382), refreshUI (217, 94, 306, 365), popToRoleSelection (SyncServerStatusViewController.swift:415-424 is byte-identical to SyncTransferProgressViewController.swift:368-377), makeDoneObject (404, 346, 548), canScanQRCode (SyncReceiverViewController.swift:86-92 ≡ SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController.swift:247-253), openQRScannerIfAvailable (SyncReceiverViewController.swift:250-276 vs SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController.swift:561-587, near-identical), and handleBackgroundInterruption (SyncServerStatusViewController.swift:208-215 ≡ SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController.swift:356-363). The copies have already drifted: SyncServerStatusViewController.addInfoView(title:value:description:menuBuilder:) at line 307 takes value as plain String, while SyncTransferProgressViewController.swift:318-344 and SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController.swift:455-477 each carry two overloads (value: String.LocalizationValue / rawValue: String). This violates 'one canonical implementation per behavior'.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared base (e.g. SyncStackScrollController: StackScrollController under Interface/Sync/) or an extension providing addSectionHeader/addInfoView(title:rawValue:description:menuBuilder:)/refreshUI/popToRoleSelection/makeDoneObject/QR-scan helpers once, and have the four controllers consume it. Keep one addInfoView signature (LocalizationValue title + raw String value + optional menuBuilder).

findings.title: loadData discards thrown database errors with no log and no user feedback
findings.detail: loadData() (lines 245-271) runs `let (albums, tracks) = try await Task.detached { try database.allAlbums()...; try database.allTracks()... }.value` inside `Task { @MainActor [weak self] in ... }` with no do/catch. The outer Task becomes Task<Void, Error> and its error is never observed: if the library database read fails, the picker silently shows an empty list with zero trace. AGENTS.md requires every swallowed error to log via AppLog.error/AppLog.warning, and error feedback to go through AlertController.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the body in do/catch; on failure call AppLog.error(self, "loadData failed error=\(error)") and present an AlertViewController with a single .accent OK action (matching the file's existing 'No Selection' alert at lines 335-344).

findings.title: resolveSelectedTracks swallows album-track query errors with unlogged try?
findings.detail: Line 392: `let albumTracks = (try? environment.libraryDatabase.databaseManager.tracks(inAlbumID: albumID)) ?? []` silently drops the error, so a selected album can contribute zero tracks to a transfer with no trace. The same query in PlaylistDetailViewController+Actions.swift:249-259 (localAlbumTracks) uses do/catch with AppLog.error, so this call site diverges from the established pattern (and also reaches through environment.libraryDatabase.databaseManager instead of environment.databaseManager used elsewhere).
findings.suggestion: Use do/catch with AppLog.error(self, "resolveSelectedTracks albumID=\(albumID) error=\(error)") before falling back to [], and access the query through environment.databaseManager like PlaylistDetailViewController+Actions does.

findings.title: Capture-session setup failure is silent (try? + guard return without logging)
findings.detail: configureCaptureSession() (lines 119-138) guards `AVCaptureDevice.default(for: .video)` and `try? AVCaptureDeviceInput(device: device)` and simply returns on failure, leaving the user on a black full-screen scanner with no AppLog trace. The canAddInput/canAddOutput false paths are also silent. AGENTS.md requires a log on every silently swallowed error/early bail of a user-visible operation.
findings.suggestion: Convert to do/catch around AVCaptureDeviceInput(device:) and AppLog.error(self, "configureCaptureSession failed ...") in each bail path; optionally surface an AlertController error and dismiss, since the screen is unusable in that state.

findings.title: makeQRCodeView/makeQRCodeImage duplicated verbatim from SyncServerStatusViewController
findings.detail: Lines 631-666 (makeQRCodeView + makeQRCodeImage, including the CIQRCodeGenerator filter setup, inputCorrectionLevel "M", 8x scale transform, and the 240x240 SnapKit container) are byte-for-byte identical to SyncServerStatusViewController.swift:347-382. Any change to QR sizing or error-correction must be made twice.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single QR helper (e.g. SyncConnectionQRCodeView maker or a `UIImage.qrCode(for: SyncConnectionInfo)` factory in Interface/Sync/ or Backend/Sync/ for the image part) and use it from both controllers.

findings.title: Single-button OK alert scaffolding reimplemented in seven-plus call sites
findings.detail: The pattern `AlertViewController(title:message:) { context in context.addAction(title: OK, attribute: .accent) { context.dispose() } }` is re-typed as presentErrorAlert here (348-355), again verbatim in SyncPlaylistAppleTVSenderViewController.swift:668-675, as presentAlert in PlaylistTransferCoordinator.swift:190-199, presentServerProfileAlert in ServerProfileImportCoordinator.swift:237-248, inline in SyncContentPickerViewController.swift:335-344, inline four times in SettingsViewController+Actions.swift (161-170, 176-183, 211-221, 226-233), inline in LogViewerController.swift:458-466, and as a pop-on-dismiss variant in SyncTransferProgressViewController.swift:357-366 / SyncServerStatusViewController.swift:426-438. The repo already centralizes confirmation alerts in ConfirmationAlertPresenter; the OK-alert sibling has no canonical implementation.
findings.suggestion: Add an InfoAlertPresenter (or extend ConfirmationAlertPresenter's home in Interface/Common/Presenters/) with present(on:title:message:onDismiss:) wrapping the single .accent OK action, then route these call sites through it.

findings.title: Disk-artwork load closure duplicated inside the cell provider without a stale-reuse guard
findings.detail: Lines 189-194 and 207-212 repeat the same block: `MuseAmpImageView.diskLoadQueue.async { guard FileManager.default.fileExists(atPath: cacheURL.path) else { return }; DispatchQueue.main.async { [weak cell] in cell?.loadArtwork(url: cacheURL) } }`. Besides the duplication, neither copy checks that the cell still represents the same item when the async hop completes — the represented-ID guard pattern used by PlaylistCell.loadCoverArtwork (PlaylistCell.swift:136-137) is the local convention for exactly this.
findings.suggestion: Extract a private `loadCachedArtworkIfAvailable(into cell:, cacheURL:, representedID:)` helper used by both branches, and have it re-check the cell's represented item ID on the main-queue hop before calling loadArtwork.

findings.title: layer.removeAllAnimations() used despite explicit AGENTS.md ban
findings.detail: setHighlighted (line 67) and prepareForReuse (line 78) both call `highlightView.layer.removeAllAnimations()`. AGENTS.md Animation Rules state: 'Do not call layer.removeAllAnimations(). Resume or transition from the current animation state.' The Interface.animate wrapper used two lines below already applies .beginFromCurrentState, which makes the removal unnecessary for the highlight fade.
findings.suggestion: Delete both removeAllAnimations() calls; setting `highlightView.alpha` directly (line 69/79) combined with Interface.animate's .beginFromCurrentState already yields the intended visual state.

findings.title: Unbounded NSCache used where the repo standard is LRUCache
findings.detail: Line 14 declares `private static let renderedArtworkCache = NSCache<NSString, UIImage>()` with no countLimit/totalCostLimit. AGENTS.md Preferred Libraries says 'Use LRUCache for bounded in-memory caches', and the codebase follows it elsewhere (e.g. MuseAmpImageView+Preview.swift:15 `LRUCache<URL, URL>(countLimit: 100)`, NowPlayingArtworkBackgroundCoordinator.swift:24).
findings.suggestion: Replace with `private static let renderedArtworkCache = LRUCache<String, UIImage>(countLimit: ...)` sized for visible-cell artwork, dropping the NSString key bridging.

findings.title: applyPlaylistsSnapshot and applySearchSnapshot are near-identical duplicates
findings.detail: Lines 432-446 and 448-462 differ only in the section constant (.playlists vs .searchResults) and the items array (sortedPlaylists.map{.playlist($0.id)} vs orderedSearchResultIDs.map{.searchResult($0)}); the previous-items capture, reconfigureItems filtering, hasAppliedInitialSnapshot gating, apply, and updateEmptyState are repeated verbatim. Changes to the animation/reconfigure policy must be made twice.
findings.suggestion: Collapse into one `applySnapshot(section: PlaylistSection, items: [PlaylistItem], animated: Bool)` private method, with the two thin call-throughs (or call it directly from reloadPlaylists/scheduleSearch).

findings.title: Random-playlist size options are inline magic numbers and the default count is dead
findings.detail: Line 113 hardcodes `[16, 25, 36, 64]` inside the add-button menu construction, and createRandomPlaylist(count: Int = 25) (line 255) declares a default that no call site uses (the only caller at line 116 always passes count). The default silently duplicates the '25' from the literal array.
findings.suggestion: Hoist `private static let randomPlaylistSizes = [16, 25, 36, 64]` next to SortOption and map over it; drop the unused `= 25` default from createRandomPlaylist.

findings.title: Five sort branches repeat the same name tiebreak comparator
findings.detail: applySort (lines 398-428) repeats `$0.name.localizedCaseInsensitiveCompare($1.name) == .orderedAscending` in all five SortOption branches as the tiebreak (lines 403, 408, 415, 420, 425), with four branches additionally following the identical 'primary key descending, then name' shape.
findings.suggestion: Extract a private `nameAscending(_:_:) -> Bool` comparator (or a generic `sort(by primary:)` helper that appends the name tiebreak) so the collation rule lives in one place.

findings.title: Empty-query reset block duplicated between updateSearchResults and scheduleSearch
findings.detail: updateSearchResults (lines 16-21) and scheduleSearch's guard (lines 34-40) both perform the same reset: cancel task, currentQuery = "", searchResultsByID = [:], orderedSearchResultIDs = [], applyPlaylistsSnapshot. The only difference is which path cancels searchTask first; the duplicated sequence can drift (e.g. one forgetting to clear orderedSearchResultIDs).
findings.suggestion: Have updateSearchResults call scheduleSearch(query: query, debounce: true) unconditionally (its guard already handles the empty/whitespace case), or extract a `clearSearchResults(animated:)` helper used by both.

findings.title: targetedPreview derives the row from sortedPlaylists instead of the diffable snapshot
findings.detail: Lines 158-164 compute `sortedPlaylists.firstIndex(where: { $0.id == playlist.id })` and build `IndexPath(row: row, section: 0)` by hand. The data source is the source of truth and already provides this mapping; during an active search the visible section is .searchResults ordered by orderedSearchResultIDs, so the sortedPlaylists-derived row points at the wrong cell (context menus are not disabled while searching — contextMenuConfigurationForRowAt only guards !isEditing at line 52).
findings.suggestion: Resolve the index path through the snapshot: `dataSource.indexPath(for: isSearchActive ? .searchResult(playlist.id) : .playlist(playlist.id))`, then fetch the cell. This removes the parallel index bookkeeping and fixes the search-mode mismatch.

findings.title: Rename action removed post-hoc by matching its localized title string
findings.detail: Lines 57-62 build the Rename UIAction unconditionally, then for liked-songs playlists strip it with `playlistActions.removeAll { ($0 as? UIAction)?.title == String(localized: "Rename") }`. Identifying an action by comparing its user-visible localized title is fragile: a copy change or locale-specific collation breaks the removal silently, and the reader must scan two places to learn when Rename appears.
findings.suggestion: Build the array conditionally instead: append the Rename action only when `playlist?.isLikedSongsPlaylist != true` (or key off `action.identifier` if post-filtering is ever genuinely needed).

findings.title: handlePlaylistsDidChange exists only to call handlePlaylistStoreDidChange
findings.detail: Lines 165-167: `@objc private func handlePlaylistsDidChange() { handlePlaylistStoreDidChange() }` adds a synonym hop — two near-identical names for one behavior, forcing the reader to chase an extra indirection from the observer registration at line 159.
findings.suggestion: Merge the two: make handlePlaylistsDidChange contain the body of handlePlaylistStoreDidChange (the @objc selector requirement is satisfied by a single method) and delete the other.

findings.title: Optional() wrapper hack to get a mutable snapshot binding inside guard
findings.detail: Line 238: `guard let dataSource, var snapshot = Optional(dataSource.snapshot()), snapshot.indexOfSection(.header) != nil else { return }` wraps a non-optional value in Optional() purely to obtain a `var` binding in the guard list. This is hand-fought syntax no other call site in the feature uses (reconfigureSongCells at 337-339 does the straightforward `guard let dataSource ...; var snapshot = dataSource.snapshot()`).
findings.suggestion: Rewrite as: `guard let dataSource else { return }; var snapshot = dataSource.snapshot(); guard snapshot.indexOfSection(.header) != nil else { return }` to match reconfigureSongCells.

findings.title: Clean-title setting change triggers tableView.reloadData() instead of snapshot reconfigure
findings.detail: Line 124's ConfigurableKit sink calls `self?.tableView.reloadData()` on a diffable-managed table. AGENTS.md View Lifecycle Rules mandate `snapshot.reconfigureItems()` for in-place cell updates; this controller even has the right primitive already (reconfigureSongCells(), line 337) used by the playback-snapshot sink directly below. SearchViewController.swift:106 repeats the same reloadData shortcut for the same preference key.
findings.suggestion: Replace the sink body with `self?.reconfigureSongCells()` here, and in SearchViewController apply a snapshot with reconfigured song items (or re-call applySnapshot) instead of reloadData.

findings.title: Redundant selectionStyle assignments contradict the TableBaseCell contract
findings.detail: Lines 301 and 327 set `cell.selectionStyle = .none` on PlaylistHeaderCell and DetailFooterCell. Both inherit TableBaseCell (Interface/Common/TableBaseCell.swift:4), which already sets selectionStyle = .none in init and manages it in setEditing. AGENTS.md Cell Rules: 'Never use selectionStyle to remove the gray tap highlight — only selectedBackgroundView visibility controls it.' The redundant assignments invite cargo-culting and can fight TableBaseCell.setEditing's toggling.
findings.suggestion: Delete both `cell.selectionStyle = .none` lines and rely on TableBaseCell.

findings.title: Magic fallback string "unknown" used as an album ID
findings.detail: Line 72: `entry.downloadRequest(albumID: albumID ?? "unknown", ...)` injects the bare literal "unknown" into persisted download records when album resolution fails. The sentinel's meaning and downstream handling are invisible at this call site, and any other producer/consumer must repeat the exact string to match it.
findings.suggestion: Hoist a named constant next to SongDownloadRequest (e.g. `static let unresolvedAlbumID = "unknown"`) or make downloadRequest accept an optional albumID and own the fallback in one place.

findings.title: reorderSections is hand-compressed with cryptic single-letter names
findings.detail: Lines 135-153 pack the section-ranking logic into semicolon-joined multi-statement lines (`let l = n.lowercased(); if l == query { return 3 }; if l.hasPrefix(query)...` at 140, `if sa != sb { return sa > sb }; return tb[a]! < tb[b]!` at 151) with names `tb`, `sa`, `sb`, `l`, `n`, `a`, `b`, plus a force-unwrapped dictionary lookup `tb[a]!`. The nested score(names:) function also silently treats .loading via dead branches. This is the densest readability hazard in the scope and diverges from the file's otherwise one-statement-per-line style.
findings.suggestion: Rename: tb → fallbackOrder, sa/sb → leftScore/rightScore, n/l → name/lowercasedName; split every semicolon-joined line; replace `tb[a]!` with a total `fallbackRank(for:)` helper; and give the match levels named constants or an enum (exactMatch=3/prefix=2/contains=1).

findings.title: Scattered semicolon-joined statements diverge from repo formatting
findings.detail: Beyond reorderSections, the same hand-compressed style appears at line 41 (`updateHistoryVisibility(); return`), line 64 (`var newSongs: [CatalogSong] = []; var newAlbums: [CatalogAlbum] = []`), and SearchViewController.swift:269 (`if case .showMore = $0 { return true }; return false`). The rest of the scope (and `make format`-governed code) is strictly one statement per line.
findings.suggestion: Break each into separate lines; for SearchViewController.swift:269 prefer `snapshot.itemIdentifiers.filter { if case .showMore = $0 { return true }\nreturn false }` split normally or a small `isShowMore` helper.

findings.title: SearchSection conflates result kinds with the loading placeholder, forcing dead .loading branches
findings.detail: SearchSection (SearchViewController.swift:16-22) mixes real result sections (.albums, .songs) with the .loading placeholder. Because `.showMore(SearchSection)` and loadMore(section:) accept the full enum, loadMore needs a guard at line 156 plus two more unreachable `case .loading: return` branches (lines 169-170 and 190-191), reorderSections must score .loading as -1 (lines 149-150), and applySnapshot needs `case .loading: break` + `default: break` (262-264). The type admits states that are meaningless for pagination — a hidden invariant every consumer re-handles.
findings.suggestion: Introduce `enum SearchResultKind { case songs, albums }` for showMore/loadMore/updatePaginatedResults/sectionOrder, keeping .loading purely as a snapshot section. The dead branches and the -1 sentinel disappear.

findings.title: Error background view rebuilt from scratch on every applySnapshot
findings.detail: Lines 276-292 construct a fresh UIView container + UILabel with full SnapKit constraints inside applySnapshot whenever searchState.searchError is set, and tear it down by replacing tableView.backgroundView. AGENTS.md View Lifecycle Rules say to pre-allocate subviews and toggle isHidden instead of creating/destroying views during data updates; the sibling PlaylistViewController follows that with its pre-allocated EmptyStateView pair. It also mixes raw view construction into a snapshot-application method (step-down violation).
findings.suggestion: Pre-allocate one error label/EmptyStateView as a stored property, install it once (e.g. as tableView.backgroundView in viewDidLoad), and in applySnapshot only set its text and isHidden.

findings.title: History table uses a classic data source with manual deleteRows/reloadData
findings.detail: historyTableView is driven by `historyTableView.dataSource = self` (SearchViewController.swift:146) with numberOfRowsInSection/cellForRowAt over the searchHistory array, mutated manually via `historyTableView.deleteRows(at: [indexPath], with: .automatic)` (line 142) and `historyTableView.reloadData()` (lines 205, 211). AGENTS.md View Lifecycle Rules mandate UITableViewDiffableDataSource for all tables with no manual data-source mutations; every other list in this scope complies.
findings.suggestion: Back historyTableView with a UITableViewDiffableDataSource<Section, String> (history entries are unique by construction in SearchHistoryStore.record), applying snapshots from loadHistory/saveQuery/clearHistory and on swipe-delete.

findings.title: SearchState.swift is a grab-bag: persistence store bundled with view state types
findings.detail: The file named SearchState.swift contains three exports: SearchPage, SearchResultsState, and SearchHistoryStore. SearchHistoryStore (line 47) is a UserDefaults-backed persistence service, not search state — it lives in the Interface layer and writes the raw key "SearchHistory" (line 54), while AGENTS.md keeps state ownership in Backend/ and routes preference keys through AppPreferences rather than scattered UserDefaults keys. Its injectable init parameters (defaults/key/limit, lines 52-56) are also speculative seams: the sole call site is `SearchHistoryStore()` at SearchViewController.swift:68 and no test uses them.
findings.suggestion: Move SearchHistoryStore to its own file (filename = export) under a Backend subdomain, register its key alongside AppPreferences' other keys, and drop the unused defaults/key/limit init parameters until a second configuration actually exists.

findings.title: LogViewerController bypasses the diffable data source mandate with raw cells
findings.detail: The controller conforms to UITableViewDataSource (line 12), wires `tableView.dataSource = self` (line 159), refreshes via `tableView.reloadData()` (lines 93, 103, 303), and dequeues a raw UITableViewCell with `cell.selectionStyle = .none` (line 135) instead of a TableBaseCell subclass. AGENTS.md requires diffable data sources for all tables, forbids using selectionStyle for highlight suppression, and requires cells to inherit TableBaseCell.
findings.suggestion: Adopt UITableViewDiffableDataSource keyed by a hashable LogLine identity (e.g. index+hash of raw line), add a small LogLineCell: TableBaseCell, and apply snapshots from applyFilters/updateSearchResults instead of reloadData.

findings.title: allLines is misnamed and parseLogLines hides filtering plus a category side effect
