findings.detail: Line 296 `allLines = parseLogLines()` suggests an unfiltered dataset, but parseLogLines() already drops lines failing selectedLevels (line 333) and selectedCategories (lines 334-336), and additionally mutates the instance property allCategories (line 340) as a side effect of 'parsing'. So `allLines` actually holds menu-filtered lines, `filteredLines` holds the search-filtered subset of those, and a 'parse' function both filters and writes state — three naming/contract lies a maintainer must untangle before touching the filters.
findings.suggestion: Split responsibilities: a pure `parseLogLines() -> (lines: [LogLine], categories: Set<String>)`, then `menuFilteredLines = apply(menuFilters:)` as a separate step; rename the stored properties to menuFilteredLines/searchFilteredLines (or compute the search subset on demand).

findings.title: Downloads explain copy duplicated between makeDownloadsObject and refreshDownloadsExplain
findings.detail: The string "Download queue, progress, and local storage.\nStored Locally: \(storedSize)" plus the identical ByteCountFormatter call is written twice: refreshDownloadsExplain (lines 102-108) and makeDownloadsObject (lines 113-120). Copy or formatting changes must be synchronized by hand across the two.
findings.suggestion: Add one private `downloadsExplainText() -> String.LocalizationValue` (computing the formatted size inside) and use it from both makeDownloadsObject and refreshDownloadsExplain.

findings.title: Closure named validateConfiguration actually persists the configuration with rollback
findings.detail: The injected dependency `validateConfiguration` (line 16) is, in its default implementation (lines 26-44), a persist-then-ping-then-rollback operation: it calls AppPreferences.setSubsonicConfiguration before pinging and restores/clears on failure. Meanwhile validateAndPersistServerProfile (lines 221-228) contains no persist step of its own — the 'AndPersist' half of its name is secretly delegated to the 'validate' closure. A caller injecting a genuinely pure validator (the seam the init advertises) would silently break persistence.
findings.suggestion: Either rename the closure to applyAndValidateConfiguration, or restructure so the coordinator owns persist/rollback in validateAndPersistServerProfile and the injectable closure is a pure `ping(configuration)` check.

findings.title: Subsonic-config UTI and export file name are inline string literals
findings.detail: Line 52 hardcodes `UTType("wiki.qaq.museamp.subsonicconfig") ?? .json` and line 154 hardcodes the export name "server.subsonicconfig"; the extension half of the UTI is repeated implicitly by the file name. The playlist transfer flow centralizes the equivalent values in PlaylistTransferFileType (Backend/Playlist/PlaylistTransferDocument.swift:4), which PlaylistTransferCoordinator.swift:30 consumes — this coordinator diverges from that established pattern.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a `ServerProfileFileType` enum (identifier, fileExtension, exportFileName) mirroring PlaylistTransferFileType and reference it from presentImportPicker and exportServerConfiguration.

findings.title: valueLabel names a button and use(menu:) hides its effect
findings.detail: Line 12: `var valueLabel: EasyHitButton { contentView as! EasyHitButton }` — the property is a UIButton, not a label, so call sites like `valueLabel.showsMenuAsPrimaryAction = true` (line 41) read as nonsense. Line 40's `func use(menu:)` is a vague verb that doesn't reveal it installs a deferred tap menu; the call site `view.use { ... }` (SettingsViewController+API.swift:19) gives the reader no hint of behavior.
findings.suggestion: Rename the accessor to `valueButton` and the method to something intent-revealing such as `setTapMenuProvider(_:)` or `showsMenuOnTap(_ provider:)`.

findings.title: popToRoleSelection duplicated verbatim from SyncServerStatusViewController
findings.detail: Lines 368-377 (find first SyncRoleSelectionViewController in the stack, popToViewController, else popToRootViewController) are identical to SyncServerStatusViewController.swift:415-424, and both feed byte-similar makeDoneObject builders (lines 346-355 vs 404-413). If the role-selection navigation anchor ever changes (e.g. presented modally), two copies must be found and fixed.
findings.suggestion: Fold into the shared Sync stack-scroll helper proposed for the broader duplication finding (one popToRoleSelection + one makeDoneObject(title:explain:) taking the pop behavior), or at minimum a UINavigationController extension in Interface/Sync/.

findings.title: PlaylistTransferDocument constructed twice in makeExportFile
findings.detail: Lines 146-152 create `PlaylistTransferDocument(playlist: playlist)` once for `.exportFileName` and a second time inside `encoder.encode(...)`. Building the document twice obscures that both uses must agree on the same snapshot of the playlist and makes the reader check the init for side effects.
findings.suggestion: Bind once: `let document = PlaylistTransferDocument(playlist: playlist)`, then use `document.exportFileName` and `encoder.encode(document)`.

findings.title: Five of six RuntimeDependencies closures are never called by the package
findings.detail: The closure-bag pattern itself is AGENTS.md-sanctioned, but only `inspectAudioFile` is ever invoked inside the package (call sites: Internal/LibraryScanner.swift:98,110 and DatabaseManager+Writes.swift:72). `resolveDownloadURL`, `requestHeaders`, `fetchLyrics`, `fetchArtworkData`, and `setScreenAwake` have zero call sites under Sources/ (grep confirms only the test fixture references them), and `DatabaseManager.dependencies` is internal so app code cannot read them back. Yet both host targets must implement them with real logic (MuseAmp/Application/AppEnvironment+Bootstrap.swift:65-80 wires APIClient playback resolution and URLSession artwork fetching into closures that are dead on arrival; MuseAmpTV/Application/TVAppContext+Bootstrap.swift:70 duplicates this). Readers of the package reasonably conclude it performs lyrics/artwork networking; it never does.
findings.suggestion: Trim RuntimeDependencies to the closures the package actually invokes (currently just `inspectAudioFile`), delete the dead implementations in both bootstrap files and the test fixture, and reintroduce members only when a package code path calls them.

findings.title: Index-reset event machinery is unreachable: indexResetReason is always nil
findings.detail: `bootstrap()` hardcodes `indexResetReason: nil` at line 64 — no code path ever produces a reason. Consequently the branch in DatabaseManager.swift:60-63 that emits `.indexResetStarted/.indexResetFinished` is dead, those two LibraryEvent cases (LibraryEvent.swift:12-13) are never sent, and none of the five `DatabaseResetReason` cases (DatabaseFormat.swift:16-22, e.g. `.corruption`, `.manualReset`) is constructed anywhere in the repo (verified by repo-wide grep). Observers subscribing to these events wait for signals that cannot occur.
findings.suggestion: Either implement reset detection in bootstrap (return a real DatabaseResetReason when the index is wiped or versions mismatch) or delete DatabaseResetReason, the two event cases, the DatabaseBootstrapResult field, and the dead branch in initializeSynchronously.

findings.title: pauseAll()/resumeAll() are no-ops whose names promise behavior that does not exist
findings.detail: `pauseAll()` and `resumeAll()` (lines 42-48) contain nothing but a DBLog.info call; they pause/resume nothing. The `.pauseAllDownloads`/`.resumeAllDownloads` commands (LibraryCommand.swift:19-20) route here via DatabaseManager+Commands.swift:39-44 and report `LibraryCommandResult.none` as if they succeeded. The real pause/resume implementation lives in the app target (MuseAmp/Backend/Downloads/DownloadManager.swift:246,263), which never sends these commands — so the package exposes a command surface that silently lies about doing work.
findings.suggestion: Remove the two commands and the no-op coordinator methods, or implement the persisted status transition they imply (e.g. flip active jobs to a paused status and emit downloadsChanged).

findings.title: Dead @DatabaseActor wrappers removeTrack(trackID:)/removeAlbum(albumID:)
findings.detail: The actor-isolated wrappers at lines 122-125 and 139-142 only forward to `removeTrackSynchronously`/`removeAlbumSynchronously` and have zero callers: the command path (DatabaseManager+Commands.swift:18-23) calls the synchronous variants directly, both wrappers are internal to the package, and grep finds no use under Sources/ or Tests/.
findings.suggestion: Delete both wrapper methods; the LibraryCommand path already covers async callers.

findings.title: clearTracks() has no callers
findings.detail: `func clearTracks()` at line 202 is never invoked anywhere in the repository (grep over all Swift sources finds only the definition). It is dead surface on the store that a reader must still account for when reasoning about who can wipe the tracks table.
findings.suggestion: Remove clearTracks() until an index-reset flow actually needs it.

findings.title: importLegacyPlaylists is the canonical playlist-replace operation, not a legacy import
findings.detail: `importLegacyPlaylists(_:)` (line 209) is the only full playlist upsert-with-entries primitive in the store. It is used for non-legacy work: `duplicatePlaylist` persists the fresh copy through it (line 259), and the app target routes ordinary playlist saves through the `.importLegacyPlaylists` command (MuseAmp/Backend/Playlist/PlaylistStore.swift:39,63). The name tells readers this is a one-time migration path when it is actually the everyday write path.
findings.suggestion: Rename the store method and LibraryCommand case to `replacePlaylists(_:)` (or `upsertPlaylists`), and if a distinct legacy-migration entry point is wanted, make it a thin wrapper with the legacy name at the call boundary.

findings.title: StateStore stores a logger it never uses; all state writes are unlogged
findings.detail: `private let logger: DatabaseLogger` (line 14) is assigned in init and never referenced again — the file contains zero DBLog calls. Meanwhile IndexStore logs its bulk writes (IndexStore.swift:169,185), and the repo logging rules require persistence stores to log write activity/failures. Every download upsert/delete and playlist mutation in this store leaves no trace, so diagnosing user-state corruption from logs is impossible.
findings.suggestion: Log mutating operations (upsertDownload, deleteDownloads, createPlaylist, deletePlaylist, savePlaylistEntries, importLegacyPlaylists) at verbose/info via DBLog, matching IndexStore; otherwise drop the unused logger parameter.

findings.title: activeDownloads() re-encodes the 'active' status set that DownloadJobStatus.isActive already defines
findings.detail: Lines 57-61 enumerate queued/waitingForNetwork/resolving/downloading/finalizing in a five-way OR chain. The canonical definition of active already exists as `DownloadJobStatus.isActive` (Models/DownloadJobStatus.swift:18-25). Adding a new status now requires updating two places, and nothing ties the WCDB predicate to the model property — they can silently drift.
findings.suggestion: Derive the predicate from the model: `let activeRaw = DownloadJobStatus.allCases.filter(\.isActive).map(\.rawValue)` and use `DownloadJobRow.Properties.status.in(activeRaw)` so the query and isActive share one source of truth.

findings.title: Meta-table accessor trio duplicated verbatim across both stores with inline key strings
findings.detail: `metaString/metaInt/setMetaValue` at StateStore.swift:324-344 are byte-for-byte the same as IndexStore.swift:206-226 except for the row type (StateMetaRow vs IndexMetaRow). The meta keys are also inline literals repeated per use: "schema_version" (IndexStore:29,37; StateStore:31,35), "format_version" (IndexStore:33,38), "last_rebuild_timestamp"/"last_rebuild_succeeded" (IndexStore:42-43,47,54). A typo in one literal silently produces a fresh meta row instead of an error.
findings.suggestion: Extract one generic meta accessor (e.g. a small `MetaTable<Row: MetaRowProtocol>` helper) shared by both stores, and hoist the keys into a private `enum MetaKey { static let schemaVersion = "schema_version" ... }`.

findings.title: Staging suffix ".tmp" is a load-bearing literal spread across three files
findings.detail: The staged-file convention is defined by string literal in three independent places: LibraryFileManager.swift:24 constructs staging names with `+ ".tmp"`, LibraryScanner.swift:68 classifies files invalid via `relativePath.hasSuffix(".tmp")`, and DatabaseManager+Support.swift:54 counts staged temp files for audit via `hasSuffix(".tmp")`. If the staging suffix ever changes in one place, scanner pruning and audit counting silently stop matching with no compile-time signal.
findings.suggestion: Define a single constant (e.g. `enum LibraryFileNaming { static let stagingSuffix = ".tmp" }` next to LibraryFileManager or LibraryPaths) and reference it at all three sites.

findings.title: Cache file extensions "jpg"/"lrc" duplicated between CacheCoordinator and LibraryPaths
findings.detail: LibraryPaths owns the cache file naming (artworkCacheURL appends ".jpg" at LibraryPaths.swift:72, lyricsCacheURL appends ".lrc" at :76), but CacheCoordinator re-states the same extensions as `expectedExtension` literals at lines 62, 70, 78, 86 for orphan detection and pruning. If the writer extension in LibraryPaths changes, orphan scans keep matching the old extension and the caches stop being pruned — the two halves of one convention live in different files.
findings.suggestion: Add `static let artworkFileExtension = "jpg"` / `lyricsFileExtension = "lrc"` on LibraryPaths, use them in both the URL builders and the CacheCoordinator prune/orphan calls.

findings.title: Canonical '<albumID>/<trackID>.<ext>' relative path is hand-built in three places
findings.detail: LibraryPaths.inferredRelativePath(for:albumID:fileExtension:) (LibraryPaths.swift:94-98) is the named API for the managed layout, yet DownloadCoordinator.enqueue line 27 inlines `"\(sanitizePathComponent(request.albumID))/\(sanitizePathComponent(request.trackID)).m4a"` and LibraryFileManager.moveToLibrary line 21 inlines the same shape again. The "m4a" default fallback is likewise repeated (DatabaseManager+Writes.swift:62, LibraryFileManager.swift:20, LibraryPaths.swift:94, DownloadCoordinator.swift:27). Three independent constructions of one layout invariant can drift (e.g. one site forgetting to sanitize the extension).
findings.suggestion: Route all relative-path construction through LibraryPaths.inferredRelativePath (give DownloadCoordinator access to LibraryPaths, and have moveToLibrary use it), and hoist the "m4a" default into one named constant referenced by the default parameters.

findings.title: Magic 0.5-second modification-date tolerance
findings.detail: Line 94 compares `abs(existing.fileModifiedAt.timeIntervalSince(modifiedAt)) < 0.5` to decide whether a file is unchanged and can skip re-inspection. The 0.5 encodes a real policy (filesystem timestamp granularity tolerance) but is an unexplained inline literal at the heart of the rescan-skip decision.
findings.suggestion: Hoist to `private static let modificationDateTolerance: TimeInterval = 0.5` on LibraryScanner so the skip policy has a name.

findings.title: rebuildIndexFromDisk mixes orchestration with per-file string parsing in one 120-line function
findings.detail: Lines 48-166 interleave high-level rebuild orchestration (snapshot diff, progress callbacks, prune, orphan cleanup, result assembly) with low-level per-file work: splitting relativePath on "/" (line 77), deriving trackID/extension via URL gymnastics (lines 85-86), reading file attributes (88-90), unchanged-file detection (92-107), record construction (112-134), and cache writes (136-141). The loop body spans four abstraction levels, and the unchanged-file forceArtwork special case (96-105) nests another do/catch inside it.
findings.suggestion: Extract per-file steps into focused helpers (e.g. `parseTrackLocation(relativePath:) -> (albumID:trackID:fileExtension:)?`, `makeRecord(for:relativePath:attributes:) async throws -> AudioTrackRecord`, `pruneInvalidFile(at:)`) so the loop reads as one level of orchestration.

findings.title: Invalid-file pruning deletes files with silent try? and the block is duplicated
findings.detail: Lines 71-72 and 146-147 both run `try? FileManager.default.removeItem(at: fileURL)` followed by `try? removeEmptyParentDirectory(for: fileURL)` with no log on failure, and line 196 in removeEmptyAlbumDirectories swallows removeItem the same way. The repo logging rules require every swallowed error to leave a log trace, and these are destructive file deletions — a failed prune leaves the invalid file on disk while the index still reports it removed via the invalidFilesRemoved event. The identical two-line prune block also appears twice in the same function.
findings.suggestion: Extract a `pruneInvalidFile(at:)` helper used by both branches that wraps the removals in do/catch and logs failures via DBLog.warning, matching the pattern already used in CacheCoordinator.clearArtworkCache.

findings.title: removeTrackCaches swallows artwork deletion failure without logging
findings.detail: Line 34 runs `try? FileManager.default.removeItem(at: artworkURL)` after an existence check, with no log trace on failure — in the same file, clearArtworkCache (lines 48-53) carefully logs every failed removal. The repo rule that swallowed errors must log is violated, and the inconsistency inside one type makes the silent path look intentional when it is not.
findings.suggestion: Use do/catch with `DBLog.warning(logger, "CacheCoordinator", ...)` on failure, mirroring clearArtworkCache (the lyrics path on line 36 is fine since LyricsCacheStore.removeLyrics logs internally).

findings.title: Empty-directory removal implemented separately in LibraryScanner and LibraryFileManager
findings.detail: LibraryScanner.removeEmptyParentDirectory (lines 168-177) and LibraryFileManager.removeEmptyDirectoryIfNeeded (LibraryFileManager.swift:73-85) implement the same behavior: guard against the audio root, list directory contents, remove if empty. LibraryScanner additionally carries a third variant, removeEmptyAlbumDirectories (179-199). Two types each own a copy of the same filesystem invariant ('no empty album folders under Audio/'), so a fix to one (e.g. logging, skipsHiddenFiles handling) will not reach the other.
findings.suggestion: Keep one canonical implementation on LibraryFileManager (or LibraryPaths) and have LibraryScanner call it — the scanner already receives its collaborators via DatabaseManager.libraryScanner(), so passing fileManager through is a one-line change.

findings.title: Lyrics-resolution expression computed twice in ingestAudioFile
findings.detail: `metadata.lyrics.nilIfEmpty ?? inspection.metadata.lyrics.nilIfEmpty` is evaluated at line 78 to decide whether to write the lyrics cache, then evaluated again verbatim at lines 101-102 to compute `hasEmbeddedLyrics`. A reader must diff the two expressions to confirm they agree, and a future edit to one fallback chain can silently desync the cache write from the recorded flag.
findings.suggestion: Hoist `let resolvedLyrics = metadata.lyrics.nilIfEmpty ?? inspection.metadata.lyrics.nilIfEmpty` once before the cache writes and use it for both the write and `hasEmbeddedLyrics: resolvedLyrics != nil`.

findings.title: artworkCacheChanged/lyricsCacheChanged events fire even when the try? cache write failed
findings.detail: Lines 74-81: `try? cacheCoordinator.writeArtwork(...)` discards failure (the error is logged inside CacheCoordinator, so logging is covered), but the very next line unconditionally sends `.artworkCacheChanged(trackIDs:)`; the lyrics branch does the same. On write failure, subscribers are told the cache changed when it did not, prompting refetches of artwork/lyrics that are not there.
findings.suggestion: Use do/catch (or `if (try? ...) != nil`) and send the cache-changed event only when the write actually succeeded.

findings.title: auditSnapshot() is declared async with no await and hides an event side effect behind a noun name
findings.detail: The function body (lines 11-88) contains no `await` — every call is synchronous (store queries, file counting, scanner path checks) — so the `async` forces callers onto a suspension point for nothing. It is also named like a pure accessor (`auditSnapshot`) yet mutates observable state by sending `.auditUpdated` on the event bus at line 50.
findings.suggestion: Drop `async` (keeping `throws`), and either rename to a verb form such as `performAudit()` or move the `.auditUpdated` emission to the caller so the snapshot accessor stays side-effect free.

findings.title: libraryScanner() factory invoked once per audited file inside the reduce closure
findings.detail: Line 38 calls `libraryScanner()` to discover files, then line 39 calls `libraryScanner()` again inside the reduce closure, constructing a brand-new LibraryScanner for every file just to call validatePath. Beyond the waste, it obscures that one scanner is conceptually doing the audit pass.
findings.suggestion: Hoist `let scanner = libraryScanner()` before the loop and use `scanner.discoverAudioFiles()` / `scanner.validatePath(...)`.

findings.title: Result case named `none` collides with Optional.none in the optional-returning command path
findings.detail: `LibraryCommandResult.none` (line 11) is returned from `sendSynchronouslyIfSupported(_:) -> LibraryCommandResult?`, where bare `.none` would mean nil — so DatabaseManager+Commands.swift must spell `LibraryCommandResult.none` fully qualified at ~17 return sites (lines 20, 23, 27, 41, 44, 48, 52, 63, 67, 71, 75, 79, 83, 87, 91, 95, 98) while `return nil` at line 103 means something entirely different ('async-only command'). The near-identical spellings of two different sentinels in one switch is a standing misread hazard.
findings.suggestion: Rename the case to `completed` (or `done`): the qualification noise disappears, and nil ('not supported synchronously') can no longer be confused with 'succeeded with nothing to report'.

findings.title: Initialization lifecycle modeled as three optionals plus a bool, producing impossible-state fallbacks
findings.detail: `indexStore?`, `stateStore?`, `downloadCoordinator?` and `initialized` (lines 26-29) all describe one lifecycle and are set together in initializeSynchronously (lines 55-58). Because `initialized` only becomes true after all three are assigned, every query that does `try requireInitialized()` followed by `guard let indexStore else { return [] }` (DatabaseManager+Queries.swift:13-15, 21-23, 34-36, 42-44, 50-52, 58-60, 91-95) is guarding a state that cannot occur — and the fallback fabricates an empty library instead of surfacing the programmer error. Same pattern in +Commands (e.g. line 33's `downloadsQueued(count: 0, skipped: requests.count)` for a nil coordinator).
findings.suggestion: Bundle the three collaborators into one `Runtime` struct stored as a single optional, and replace requireInitialized() with `requireRuntime() throws -> Runtime`; queries then use the returned stores directly and the fabricated-empty fallbacks disappear.

findings.title: DBLog static shim is a pure pass-through over DatabaseLogger's own methods
findings.detail: Lines 52-72 define `DBLog.verbose/info/warning/error/critical(_ logger:_:_:)` whose bodies are one-line forwards to the identically named instance methods on the logger passed as first argument. Every call site in the package writes `DBLog.info(logger, "Scope", msg)` instead of the equivalent `logger.info("Scope", msg)` — an extra layer that adds no behavior, no default, and no seam, just a second spelling of the same API.
findings.suggestion: Delete the DBLog enum and call the DatabaseLogger methods directly (mechanical find/replace), or give DBLog real value (e.g. a package-default logger so call sites stop threading the logger as a parameter).

findings.title: lyrics(for:) logs a warning on every routine cache miss
findings.detail: The catch at lines 22-25 treats all read failures identically and logs a warning — including the dominant, fully expected case that no .lrc file exists for the track. In the same file, removeLyrics(for:) (line 44) already demonstrates the right pattern by special-casing NSFileNoSuchFileError silently. As written, scrolling a library of tracks without cached lyrics floods the log with warnings, burying real I/O failures.
findings.suggestion: Mirror removeLyrics: catch NSCocoaErrorDomain/NSFileNoSuchFileError (or NSFileReadNoSuchFileError) and return nil silently; keep the warning for all other errors.

findings.title: NSError codes 0/1/2/4/5 are unexplained magic numbers across five throw sites
findings.detail: The DatabaseManager error domain uses bare integer codes scattered across files: code 0 (DatabaseManager+Support.swift:15, not initialized), code 1 (DatabaseManager+Audit.swift:16, audit runtime unavailable), code 2 (DatabaseManager+Writes.swift:52, write runtime unavailable), code 4 (DatabaseManager+Commands.swift:114, requires async send), code 5 (DatabaseManager+Commands.swift:149, unsupported command) — with code 3 missing entirely. Nothing names what each code means, so callers matching on codes must reverse-engineer them from message strings.
findings.suggestion: Centralize as a private enum (e.g. `enum DatabaseManagerErrorCode: Int { case notInitialized = 0, auditUnavailable = 1, writeRuntimeUnavailable = 2, requiresAsyncSend = 4, unsupportedCommand = 5 }`) plus a small NSError factory, keeping the existing domain and localized messages.

findings.title: makeRuntimeDependencies is a verbatim ~75-line copy of AppEnvironment+Bootstrap
findings.detail: TVAppContext+Bootstrap.swift lines 31-42 (logSink switch) and 65-134 (makeRuntimeDependencies, including the entire inspectAudioFile metadata-inspection closure) are character-for-character identical to MuseAmp/Application/AppEnvironment+Bootstrap.swift lines 29-40 and 60-129; the only difference is the NSError domain string ("TVAppContext" vs "AppEnvironment"). This file is TV-native (not a symlink), so any fix to import inspection, lyrics fetching, or artwork download on iOS silently diverges on tvOS. The repo's sanctioned mechanism for sharing backend code with the TV target is relative symlinks (used for ~50 other Backend files). Also note the TV sync initializer is named initializeDatabaseManager while the identical iOS sync twin is initializeDatabaseManagerSynchronously, hiding the blocking behavior the iOS name advertises.
findings.suggestion: Hoist the RuntimeDependencies factory and the DatabaseManager logSink mapping into a shared Backend file (e.g. Backend/Library/RuntimeDependenciesFactory.swift) parameterized by apiClient/metadataReader/paths, and symlink it into MuseAmpTV/Backend/ like the other shared files; keep only the thin per-target wiring in each Bootstrap extension. Align the sync initializer name with the iOS *Synchronously convention.

findings.title: Tail of receiveTransfer duplicates importAndComplete nearly verbatim
findings.detail: Lines 428-487 (set .importing phase, importDownloadedFiles with identical progress closure, downloadFailures/failedTrackCount math, stopReceiver, completeTransferredPlaylistSession, identical guard-didSaveSession failure block, set .completed) are a near-verbatim copy of importAndComplete (lines 606-669) in the same file; only the log message prefixes differ. resumeTransfer already delegates to importAndComplete (lines 597-603), so receiveTransfer is the lone divergent copy of the import/complete phase.
findings.suggestion: Replace lines 428-487 of receiveTransfer with a call to importAndComplete(session:manifest:playlistSession:missingEntries:downloadedURLs:), matching what resumeTransfer already does.

findings.title: Session-save failure fallback block triple-duplicated
findings.detail: The exact block `let message = context.takePendingSessionAlertMessage() ?? SyncTransferError.invalidPlaylistSession.errorDescription ?? String(localized: "The transferred playlist could not be saved.")` followed by AppLog.error, transferPhase = .failed(message), startReceiverAvailability(), notifyStateChanged() appears three times: lines 332-341, 465-474, and 647-656. The localized fallback string is also repeated three times.
findings.suggestion: Extract a private helper, e.g. `func failTransferAfterSessionSaveFailure(context logContext: String)`, that computes the message once and performs the phase transition; call it from all three sites. Fixing the receiveTransfer/importAndComplete duplication removes one copy automatically.

findings.title: Disconnect-detection heuristic magic numbers duplicated
findings.detail: The heuristic `failureCount > 3 && count > 0 && Double(failureCount) / Double(count) > 0.5` appears twice with inline magic numbers: lines 403-407 (receiveTransfer) and 575-579 (resumeTransfer). Neither 3 nor 0.5 is named or explained, and a future tuning change must be made in two places.
findings.suggestion: Add a single private helper such as `static func isLikelyDisconnect(failures: Int, total: Int) -> Bool` backed by named constants (e.g. minimumFailuresForDisconnect = 3, disconnectFailureRatio = 0.5) and use it at both sites.

findings.title: Pending transferReadyContinuation never resumed on cancel paths
findings.detail: runTransfer suspends on `await withCheckedContinuation { transferReadyContinuation = $0 }` (lines 280-282). Only proceedWithTransfer() (lines 217-220) ever resumes it. cancelTransfer() (lines 222-233) and retryTransfer()/connect() cancel transferTask, but Task cancellation does not resume a checked continuation, so the suspended task leaks; a subsequent connect() then overwrites transferReadyContinuation at line 281 without resuming the previous one. This is a second one-shot lifecycle state living beside transferTask with no tie-in to cancellation.
findings.suggestion: Resume-and-nil any pending continuation in cancelTransfer/retryTransfer/connect before cancelling the task (and before assigning a new continuation). Per AGENTS.md, use the shared ContinuationOnceGuard from Backend/Supplement/ConcurrencyHelpers.swift instead of a bare optional CheckedContinuation.

findings.title: Per-app identity state stored as static type-level properties
findings.detail: generatedPairingCode (line 14) and generatedBonjourToken (line 15) are `private static let` stored on the TVAppContext extension, with advertisedReceiverDeviceName/ServiceName as static computed properties (lines 17-29). This instance-lifecycle identity state is type-level only because extensions cannot hold stored instance properties. It can never be regenerated or reset (contrast SyncTransferSession, which regenerates its instance-level advertisedBonjourToken and password in prepareSender), and any second context would silently share the same pairing identity.
findings.suggestion: Move the pairing code and bonjour token into TVAppContext.swift as instance `let` properties (or a small ReceiverIdentity value), and keep only the derived computed properties in the +Session extension.

findings.title: Pairing code generation reimplements SyncPasswordGenerator inline
findings.detail: `.init(format: "%06d", Int.random(in: 0 ..< 1_000_000))` at line 14 is byte-identical to SyncPasswordGenerator.generate() (MuseAmp/Backend/Sync/SyncPasswordGenerator.swift:12), which is already compiled into the TV target via the MuseAmpTV/Backend/Sync symlink. The pairing-code format rule now lives in two places.
findings.suggestion: Use `SyncPasswordGenerator.generate()` for the pairing code so the 6-digit format has one canonical owner.

findings.title: Repeat-mode policy duplicated and twin if/!if on one boolean
findings.detail: In restoreSessionStateAtLaunch, lines 66-69 set `playbackController.setRepeatMode(.queue)` after playCurrentSession(), but playCurrentSession already calls setRepeatMode(.queue) on success (lines 163-165), so the policy executes twice on that path and ownership of the "session always repeats queue" rule is split across two functions. Lines 67-75 also use two consecutive `if didStartPlayback { ... }` / `if !didStartPlayback { ... }` statements instead of if/else.
findings.suggestion: Let playCurrentSession own the repeat-mode rule and apply it for the restored-playback branch explicitly (or hoist one `ensureQueueRepeatMode()`), then collapse the twin conditionals into a single if/else.

findings.title: Dead `return started` always returns true
findings.detail: In completeTransferredPlaylistSession, the `.valid` autoPlay branch reads: `let started = await playCurrentSession(); if !started { ...; return true }; return started` (lines 137-146). `return started` is only reachable when started == true, so every path in this branch returns true. The dead conditional value suggests the return reflects playback success when it actually reports "session saved".
findings.suggestion: Replace `return started` with `return true` (both exits of the branch), making it obvious the Bool means "session persisted", not "playback started".

findings.title: configure(content:) ignores most of AMTVUploadWaitingContent; instruction text duplicated
findings.detail: configure(content:) (lines 100-114) reads only qrPayload and (for accessibility) message. The struct's title, deviceName, connectionCodeTitle, and connectionCode fields are never rendered anywhere, yet TVSessionStateAdapter.uploadWaitingContent (lines 144-164) builds localized "Connecting to Sender" / "Selected Sender" payloads for them. Meanwhile the visible instructionLabel text is hardcoded at line 34 with the exact same localized string the adapter puts in `message` at TVSessionStateAdapter.swift:158, so the copy exists in two places and the connecting-state content is dead. Line 106 also sets isAccessibilityElement = false immediately before assigning accessibilityTraits/accessibilityLabel, which VoiceOver will ignore.
findings.suggestion: Either render the content (bind instructionLabel to content.message and show the connecting-state fields) or shrink AMTVUploadWaitingContent to the fields actually displayed and delete the adapter's dead localized strings. Resolve the isAccessibilityElement=false vs accessibilityLabel contradiction.

findings.title: configure(content:) discards content.title and content.progress
findings.detail: configure (lines 59-79) recomputes its own title `"Receiving playlist from \(content.sourceDeviceName)"` (line 60) instead of using content.title, which TVSessionStateAdapter carefully builds as "Receiving Playlist …" / "Finishing Playlist …" (TVSessionStateAdapter.swift:170,180). content.progress is never read at all, even though the adapter computes fractional download progress in two progress closures (TVSessionStateAdapter.swift:373-376 and 545-548) solely to populate it — the view shows an indeterminate spinner. Readers of the adapter reasonably assume the import phase title and progress fraction are user-visible; they are not.
findings.suggestion: Use content.title for titleLabel and render content.progress (or remove the title/progress fields from AMTVReceivingTracksContent and delete the adapter's now-dead computations) so the content struct's contract matches what is displayed.

findings.title: Direct UIView.transition for window-root swap instead of Interface wrapper
findings.detail: Line 42 calls UIView.transition directly (manually passing .beginFromCurrentState/.allowUserInteraction). AGENTS.md Animation Rules: "All UIView animations must go through Interface wrapper methods … Never call UIView.animate or UIView.transition directly." The iOS twin performs the identical window-root swap through the wrapper (MuseAmp/Application/SceneDelegate.swift:77 uses Interface.transition, defined at MuseAmp/Interface/Common/Style/Interface.swift:91). The TV-local Interface enum (MuseAmpTV/Interface/Common/Interface.swift) simply lacks a transition wrapper.
findings.suggestion: Add a `transition(with:duration:options:animations:completion:)` wrapper to MuseAmpTV/Interface/Common/Interface.swift mirroring the iOS Interface enum, and route this call through it.

findings.title: Direct UIView.transition in applySnapshot
findings.detail: Lines 203-209 call UIView.transition directly for the content-to-empty crossfade (and omit .beginFromCurrentState that the wrappers guarantee). Same Animation Rules violation as TVSceneDelegate; the iOS counterpart routes its equivalent crossfade through Interface.transition (MuseAmp/Interface/NowPlaying/LyricTimeline/LyricTimelineView.swift:104).
findings.suggestion: Once a transition wrapper exists in the TV Interface enum (see TVSceneDelegate finding), route this call through it so .beginFromCurrentState/.allowUserInteraction are applied consistently.

findings.title: Classic UITableViewDataSource instead of mandated diffable data source
findings.detail: TVLyricTimelineView assigns tableView.dataSource/delegate = self (lines 80-81) and implements UITableViewDataSource with manual reloadData() calls (lines 215, 233) plus hand-rolled structure diffing (lines 221-229). AGENTS.md View Lifecycle Rules state: "All UITableView / UICollectionView must use UITableViewDiffableDataSource … No manual data-source mutations." Note the iOS LyricTimelineView (MuseAmp/Interface/NowPlaying/LyricTimeline/LyricTimelineView+Delegate.swift:4) shares the same deviation, so this is an established but undocumented exception.
findings.suggestion: Either migrate the lyric timeline to UITableViewDiffableDataSource (the Item enum is already Hashable-ready) in both targets, or document the lyric-timeline exception in AGENTS.md so the rule and the code stop contradicting each other.

findings.title: Internal subjects and deadline exposed without external consumers
findings.detail: interactionSubject, focusSubject (lines 64-65) and `var userInteractionDeadline` (line 66) are declared at internal access, but a repo-wide grep shows no use outside TVLyricTimelineView.swift; they are purely internal plumbing bound in bindInteraction(). The non-private mutable deadline invites external mutation of state the view owns.
findings.suggestion: Mark all three `private` (and userInteractionDeadline `private(set)` if read access is ever needed).

findings.title: AGENTS.md documents Sync-only symlink mirroring; actual coverage spans nine subdomains
findings.detail: AGENTS.md line 100 says "MuseAmpTV/Backend/Sync/ mirrors selected files from MuseAmp/Backend/Sync/ via relative symlinks", but the TV target actually symlinks Application/AppPreferences.swift plus the entire Backend/API, Downloads, Library, Logging, Lyrics, Playback, Playlist, Supplement, and Sync subtrees (~49 symlinked Swift files verified with find -type l). The doc's own Documentation Sync rule ("Any structural directory change must update this AGENTS.md in the same change") is violated, and an agent adding a non-Sync shared backend file gets no instruction to create the required TV symlink — the exact failure mode the rule warns about.
findings.suggestion: Update the Placement Guide bullet to enumerate every mirrored subdomain (or state that all of MuseAmpTV/Backend/* except Models and TVSessionStateAdapter.swift is symlinked from MuseAmp), and extend the "create a matching symlink" instruction beyond Sync.

findings.title: presentStatusAlert lacks the presented-VC guard; consumed pending alert can be lost
findings.detail: presentTransferFailedAlert (line 349), presentTransferDisconnectedAlert (line 362), presentCancelTransferConfirmation (line 378), and presentLeaveSessionConfirmation (line 420) all guard `presentedViewController == nil`, but presentStatusAlert (lines 397-405) does not. Its only caller, presentPendingSessionAlertIfNeeded (line 407), first *consumes* the alert via context.takePendingSessionAlert(); if another alert is already presented (e.g. the .failed alert presented earlier in the same reloadContent pass at lines 144-150, or the connecting alert), UIKit refuses the second present and the pending session alert is silently lost — it was already taken from the context.
findings.suggestion: Guard `presentedViewController == nil` in presentPendingSessionAlertIfNeeded *before* calling takePendingSessionAlert(), so an unpresentable alert stays pending for the next viewDidAppear/reloadContent instead of being consumed and dropped.

findings.title: Stored state scattered mid-file across the root controller
findings.detail: The controller's stored state is declared in four separate clusters: properties at the top (lines 8-24), `didResumePlaybackAfterBoot` between viewDidAppear and its helper (line 59), `lastBackgroundTrackID`/`playbackWasActive` between setupLayout and bindPlayback (lines 257-258), and `connectingAlert` under the Connection mark (line 303). Auditing the full state set of this 463-line state-machine renderer requires scanning the whole file.
findings.suggestion: Group all stored properties in the single declaration block at the top of the class (the repo's dominant pattern in TVNowPlayingController and TVSessionStateAdapter), keeping the MARK-sectioned extensions purely behavioral.

findings.title: Inline 5.0-second user-interaction window
findings.detail: `Date().timeIntervalSince(lastUserInteractionDate) < 5.0` (line 36) hardcodes the interaction-window duration that gates the idle-timer hold in TVSceneDelegate.updatePlaybackActivityHold. The 5.0 has no name and its policy meaning ("keep screen awake briefly after remote input") is invisible at the call site.
findings.suggestion: Hoist to `private static let userInteractionWindow: TimeInterval = 5` (or similar) next to lastUserInteractionDate.

findings.title: Raw pixel-bucketing palette algorithm with unnamed thresholds inline in a view
findings.detail: extractColors (lines 119-184) embeds a full dominant-color algorithm — 64x64 downsample, stride-4 sampling, brightness window 60..700, saturation > 20, /32 bucket quantization, prefix(4) — directly in the background view, mixing UIKit orchestration with raw byte manipulation in one type. None of the seven tuning constants is named, so the algorithm's intent (skip near-black/near-white, skip greys, coarse 8-level buckets) must be reverse-engineered.
findings.suggestion: Extract a `TVArtworkPaletteExtractor` (nonisolated enum/struct) with named constants (sampleSize, sampleStride, minBrightness/maxBrightness, minSaturation, bucketQuantization, maxColors), leaving the view with retrieve-image / apply-colors orchestration only.

findings.title: Unbounded dictionary cache where AGENTS prescribes LRUCache
findings.detail: `private var colorCache: [String: [UIColor]] = [:]` (line 32) grows without bound per distinct artwork URL for the lifetime of the root view. AGENTS.md Preferred Libraries: "Use LRUCache for bounded in-memory caches."
findings.suggestion: Replace with `LRUCache<String, [UIColor]>(countLimit: …)` per the repo's library rules.

findings.title: Dead private helper isMacDevice
findings.detail: isMacDevice(_:) (lines 184-192) has no callers anywhere in the repo (verified by grep across MuseAmp and MuseAmpTV); the mac-detection logic actually used lives inline in symbolName(for:) (lines 211-222), which re-implements the same substring checks.
findings.suggestion: Delete isMacDevice(_:); if device-class detection is ever needed again, derive it from symbolName's mapping instead of keeping a second copy.

findings.title: Playback item ID parsing duplicated inline with no canonical inverse
findings.detail: The "tv-session|sessionID|index|trackID" identifier format is defined in TVPlaylistSessionManifest.playbackItemIdentifier (MuseAmp/Backend/Sync/TVPlaylistSessionStore.swift:58-59), but the inverse — `id.split(separator: "|").last.map(String.init) ?? id` — is hand-rolled twice in this controller (lines 25 and 176). The format knowledge is now split across two files and three sites; a change to the encoding (e.g. trackIDs containing "|") breaks the ad-hoc parsers silently.
findings.suggestion: Add `static func catalogTrackID(fromPlaybackItemID:) -> String` next to playbackItemIdentifier in TVPlaylistSessionStore.swift and use it at both call sites.

findings.title: TVLyricParser/Timeline/Line/Progress are verbatim copies of the iOS lyric engine
findings.detail: diff confirms TVLyricParser.swift, TVLyricTimeline.swift, TVLyricLine.swift, and TVLyricProgress.swift are byte-identical (modulo the TV name prefix) to MuseAmp/Interface/NowPlaying/LyricTimeline/Lyrics/{LyricParser,LyricTimeline,LyricLine,LyricProgress}.swift — ~200 duplicated lines including the LRC regex grammar, offset handling, and binary search. AGENTS.md scopes Backend/Lyrics as "lyrics fetching, parsing…", and the repo's sanctioned cross-target sharing mechanism is symlinks; instead the parser is forked under Interface in both targets, so an LRC parsing fix lands in one target only.
findings.suggestion: Move the parser/timeline/line/progress quartet to MuseAmp/Backend/Lyrics (matching its documented scope), symlink them into MuseAmpTV/Backend/Lyrics like the rest of the shared backend, and delete the renamed TV copies.

findings.title: Hardcoded 128pt spacers bypass the layout-constants enum
findings.detail: Two anonymous spacer views are pinned to `make.height.equalTo(128)` (lines 54 and 61). TVNowPlayingLayout.swift exists precisely to centralize these values and its header claims "All spacing values follow an 8/16/32/64 scale"; 128 appears nowhere in that enum and is duplicated at both ends of the stack.
findings.suggestion: Add e.g. `static let contentVerticalSpacer: CGFloat = 128` to TVNowPlayingLayout (or express it as spacing64 * 2, matching TVNowPlayingView line 42) and use it for both spacers.

findings.title: Focus/idle alpha literals repeated across four sites
findings.detail: The unfocused alpha 0.1 appears at lines 29, 56, 75, and 83, and the full expression `self.alpha = self.isFocused ? 1.0 : 0.1` is duplicated verbatim in didUpdateFocus, pressesEnded, and pressesCancelled (with 0.5 as the pressed value at line 65). Tuning the dimmed state requires touching four places.
findings.suggestion: Add private constants (idleAlpha, focusedAlpha, pressedAlpha) and a `restingAlpha` computed property (or applyRestingAlpha() helper) used by all three handlers.

findings.title: Empty layoutSubviews override and uncommented no-op traitCollectionDidChange
findings.detail: Lines 33-35 override layoutSubviews only to call super — pure dead code. Line 45 overrides traitCollectionDidChange with an empty body that deliberately(?) drops the super call (presumably to stop UIVisualEffectView from resetting the private variableBlur filter on trait changes), but carries no comment, so it reads as an accident and is exactly the kind of ambiguity AGENTS.md says comments exist to remove.
findings.suggestion: Delete the layoutSubviews override; keep the traitCollectionDidChange override but add a one-line comment stating it intentionally skips super to preserve the CAFilter-based variable blur.

findings.title: Two near-identical cached-lyrics branches; cache paths skip task cancellation
findings.detail: The in-memory cache branch (lines 47-60) and the stored-lyrics branch (lines 62-76) are structurally identical: reset lyricsLoadingTrackID, emit loading, then spawn a Task that emits the lyrics. Both also assign `lyricsTask = Task { … }` without first calling `lyricsTask?.cancel()`, unlike the fetch path which cancels at line 83 — so an in-flight fetch for the previous track keeps running (only the shouldApplyLoadedLyrics guard prevents a stale UI write).
findings.suggestion: Extract `private func applyCachedLyrics(_ lyrics: String, for trackID: String)` used by both branches, and cancel the previous lyricsTask there for consistency with the fetch path.

findings.title: Seek step and scrub cooldown literals duplicated
findings.detail: The 10-second seek step appears in both handleSeekBackward (line 191) and handleSeekForward (line 198), and the 0.5-second scrub-commit cooldown `Date().addingTimeInterval(0.5)` appears at lines 192 and 203. Neither value is named.
findings.suggestion: Add `private static let seekStep: TimeInterval = 10` and `private static let scrubCommitCooldown: TimeInterval = 0.5` and use them at all four sites.

findings.title: Unexplained placeholder port 1 in receiver advertisement
findings.detail: startReceiverAvailability publishes the Bonjour service with `port: 1` (line 688). Unlike the sender path, which advertises the real listener port (SyncTransferSession.swift:177), the receiver advertises a dummy port and nothing at the call site says so — a reader cannot tell whether 1 is a real endpoint, a sentinel, or a bug.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a named constant (e.g. `SyncConstants.receiverPlaceholderPort = 1`) with a short comment that receiver-role advertisements carry no reachable port, and use it here.

findings.title: configureImageRequestAuthorization does not configure authorization
findings.detail: The TV implementation (lines 136-144) only strips any existing .requestModifier from Kingfisher's default options and appends .backgroundDecode — no authorization is configured (the iOS namesake at AppEnvironment+Bootstrap.swift:131 additionally sets cache limits, also unrelated to authorization). The verb phrase promises a side effect that never happens, which is exactly the kind of misleading action name the naming principle targets.
findings.suggestion: Rename to describe the actual effect, e.g. `configureImageLoadingDefaults()` or `resetImageRequestModifiers()`, in both targets.

findings.title: FileManager injected and stored as a property against explicit repo rule
findings.detail: SyncTransferSession declares `private let fileManager: FileManager` (line 28), takes it as an init parameter with a .default default (line 48), and threads it into SyncPreparedTrackBuilder, which repeats the pattern (SyncPreparedTrackBuilder.swift lines 34/40). AGENTS.md Dependency Rules: "Use FileManager.default directly for standard file operations. Do not pass FileManager as a parameter or store it as a property." This file is compiled into the TV target via MuseAmpTV/Backend/Sync symlink.
findings.suggestion: Drop the stored property and init parameter in both SyncTransferSession and SyncPreparedTrackBuilder and call FileManager.default at the use sites, per the repo rule.

findings.title: Silent try? handle.close() without log trace
findings.detail: streamChunk swallows FileHandle close failures twice with bare `try? handle.close()` (lines 531 and 543) and no AppLog call. AGENTS.md Logging Rules: "Every catch block or try? that silently swallows an error must log via AppLog.error or AppLog.warning before returning/continuing." This file is part of the TV target via symlink.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the closes in do/catch with `AppLog.warning(self, "streamChunk close failed …")`, or add a small closeQuietly(handle:) helper that logs.

findings.title: receiveOutcome is a pure pass-through to underscore twin
findings.detail: `static func receiveOutcome(buffer:chunk:isComplete:)` (lines 35-41) forwards verbatim to `_receiveOutcome` (line 260) with identical signature and no added behavior. The underscore-prefixed twin is non-idiomatic Swift naming, and the indirection forces readers to chase one extra hop to find the parsing logic.
findings.suggestion: Collapse the pair: implement the logic directly in the public `receiveOutcome` and update the internal caller at line 238 (the test seam, if any, keeps working since the public name is unchanged).

findings.title: Dead 300-line NowPlayingLyricsCoordinator shadows the live lyric-loading path
findings.detail: `final class NowPlayingLyricsCoordinator` is never instantiated anywhere in the repo (grep over MuseAmp/MuseAmpTV/MuseAmpTests finds only the definition; tvOS uses its own TVNowPlayingLyricsCoordinator). The live lyric-loading pipeline now lives in LyricTimelineView+Binding.swift (bindDataSource -> lyricsService.loadLyrics -> parseLyrics). Only the file-bottom free function `shouldCacheUnavailableLyricsResult` (line 303) is still referenced — by this dead class and MuseAmpTests/NowPlaying/NowPlayingLyricsLoadingTests.swift. The class carries a full stale state machine (lyricsCache/lyricsRawCache/lyricsLoadingTrackID/lyricsTransientFailureTrackID) that actively misleads readers into thinking it is the canonical lyrics path.
findings.suggestion: Delete the NowPlayingLyricsCoordinator class. Relocate shouldCacheUnavailableLyricsResult into a small purpose-named file (e.g. under Backend/Lyrics/) so the existing tests keep passing, and update AGENTS-aligned file naming (filename = export).

findings.title: Lyric-sheet shell protocol is dead; LyricTimelineView re-implements the same presentation inline
findings.detail: `presentLyricSelectionSheet(with:activeIndex:)` (lines 5, 9) has zero call sites. The only live path is the private duplicate `presentLyricSelectionSheet(lyrics:activeIndex:)` in LyricTimelineView+Actions.swift:73-88, which repeats the identical body: empty-lyrics guard, responder-chain controller lookup, presentedViewController == nil guard, UINavigationController wrap, .formSheet, prefersGrabberVisible. Consequently the one-line conformance files NowPlayingCompactController+LyricSheet.swift and NowPlayingRelaxedController+LyricSheet.swift wire nothing.
findings.suggestion: Keep one canonical implementation: either route LyricTimelineView through the shell protocol (lookup the owning NowPlayingLyricSheetPresenting controller) and delete the private duplicate, or delete NowPlayingLyricShellController.swift plus both +LyricSheet conformance files and keep the view-local implementation.

findings.title: Tap/seek path re-parses lyrics from a different source than the rendered timeline
findings.detail: `currentTimeline()` (lines 90-102) re-derives the timeline on every row tap / context menu by reading environment.lyricsService.cachedLyrics, re-running Chinese script conversion, and re-running LyricParser — even though the rendering pipeline in LyricTimelineView+Binding (parseLyrics/buildSnapshot) already produced a ParsedLyrics containing exactly this timeline. The view discards it, keeping only `items`. The two derivations can disagree (e.g. lyrics just loaded via loadLyrics but not yet in cachedLyrics), so a tapped line can seek against a timeline that does not match what is displayed.
findings.suggestion: Store the latest ParsedLyrics (or its LyricTimeline) on LyricTimelineView when applying a snapshot, and use that single source of truth in seekToLine(at:) and makeLineContextMenu(at:), deleting currentTimeline().

findings.title: NowPlayingQueueTrackCell is dead code kept alive only by a test
findings.detail: The class is never registered or dequeued anywhere in app code — NowPlayingQueueSectionView registers AmSongCell, NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell, NowPlayingQueueEmptyCell, NowPlayingQueueFooterCell (NowPlayingQueueSectionView.swift:116-131). The only reference outside this file is MuseAmpTests/NowPlaying/NowPlayingQueueCellTests.swift:41-42, which asserts selection suppression on a cell no screen uses.
findings.suggestion: Delete NowPlayingQueueTrackCell.swift and the corresponding test case; AmSongCell is the canonical queue row cell.

findings.title: Manual UITableViewDataSource with hand-rolled diffing violates the repo diffable-data-source rule
findings.detail: AGENTS.md View Lifecycle Rules mandate: "All UITableView / UICollectionView must use UITableViewDiffableDataSource ... No manual data-source mutations." This view implements UITableViewDataSource directly (line 23), drives updates via reloadData/reloadSections(.fade) (lines 207, 225), and reimplements what diffable provides with a custom change-tracking struct NowPlayingQueuePresentationUpdate (lines 4-20: seven did*Change booleans) plus manual refreshVisibleCells/refreshQueueControlsCell/refreshQueueFooterCell. This is the largest deviation from the project's own mandated table pattern and must be re-learned by every maintainer.
findings.suggestion: Migrate to UITableViewDiffableDataSource keyed by the existing string ItemIdentifier values, using snapshot.reconfigureItems() for in-place header/footer/track content updates and the hasAppliedInitialSnapshot flag the repo already prescribes; delete NowPlayingQueuePresentationUpdate.

findings.title: isProgramaticScrollBlocked is a Date named like a Bool, and misspelled
findings.detail: `var isProgramaticScrollBlocked: Date = .distantPast` uses an is-prefixed boolean-style name for a cooldown deadline, and misspells "Programmatic" (single m) in 5 occurrences (lines 143, 608, 618, 622, 634-635) while the same file spells `pendingProgrammaticScrollRetry`/`blockProgrammaticScroll` correctly. Sibling code uses the Deadline suffix for this exact pattern (LyricTimelineView.userInteractionDeadline, ProgressTrackView.scrubCommitCooldownDeadline). The Date-as-cooldown pattern itself is AGENTS-sanctioned; the misleading name is not.
findings.suggestion: Rename to `programmaticScrollBlockDeadline: Date = .distantPast`, matching userInteractionDeadline/scrubCommitCooldownDeadline.

findings.title: Magic empty-cell height and thrice-repeated highlight color literal
findings.detail: heightForItemIdentifier returns a bare `72` for the empty-queue row (line 197) while every sibling height is a named Layout constant (sectionHeaderHeight, footerRowHeight, queueRowHeight). `UIColor.white.withAlphaComponent(0.08)` is repeated three times as the row-highlight color (lines 327, 435, 446) with no named constant, so the context-menu preview color and the current-row background can silently drift apart.
findings.suggestion: Add `Layout.emptyQueueRowHeight: CGFloat = 72` and a `Palette.rowHighlightBackground` constant (matching the per-view Palette convention used by NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell) and use them at all four sites.

findings.title: Dead Layout constants duplicating NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell's own values
findings.detail: `Layout.headerControlSize = 40` and `Layout.headerActionsWidth = 92` (lines 30-31) are referenced nowhere in the repo (verified by grep). The live copies are NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell.Layout.controlSize = 40 / actionsWidth = 92 (NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell.swift:9-10). Keeping a dead second source of truth invites someone to edit the wrong one.
findings.suggestion: Delete headerControlSize and headerActionsWidth from NowPlayingQueueSectionView.Layout; the header cell owns its dimensions.

findings.title: 741-line single-type file ignores the repo's responsibility-split convention
findings.detail: One type body contains layout constants, item-identifier scheme, snapshot application, change detection, cell configuration, UITableViewDataSource, UITableViewDelegate, context-menu construction, anchored auto-scroll math, and programmatic-scroll throttling. Sibling LyricTimelineView splits the same responsibilities into +Binding/+Actions/+Delegate files, and AGENTS.md mandates splitting by responsibility (XxxView.swift plus +Table/+Actions extensions). The top-level struct NowPlayingQueuePresentationUpdate also lives in this file rather than its own (filename = export).
findings.suggestion: Split into NowPlayingQueueSectionView.swift (state + snapshot application), +DataSource.swift, +ContextMenu.swift, and +Scrolling.swift, and move NowPlayingQueuePresentationUpdate to its own file (or fold it away during the diffable migration).

findings.title: LyricTimelineView also uses a manual data source instead of the mandated diffable pattern
findings.detail: `tableView.dataSource = self` (line 71) with the conformance in LyricTimelineView+Delegate.swift, plus hand-rolled structure-change detection in applySnapshot (lines 122-130) and reloadData branches (lines 108, 116, 135), violates the AGENTS.md rule that all UITableViews use UITableViewDiffableDataSource. Items already have stable identities (.line(index)/.staticLine(index)/.spacer/.message), so the manual zip-based structure diff re-implements exactly what a diffable snapshot would do.
findings.suggestion: Back the lyric table with UITableViewDiffableDataSource keyed by the Item cases (make Item Hashable), use reconfigureItems for active-line state changes, and keep the fade-in/fade-out branches as animation wrappers around snapshot application.

findings.title: Parameter name typo: isUserInitialed
findings.detail: `func focusCurrentLine(isUserInitialed: Bool)` (line 126, also call site LyricTimelineView+Binding.swift:113) misspells "isUserInitiated". The log line inside the same function even prints the correct spelling: `"focusCurrentLine activeRow=... userInitiated=\(isUserInitialed)"` (line 140), so the API and its diagnostics disagree.
findings.suggestion: Rename the parameter to isUserInitiated at the declaration and both call sites.

findings.title: Stale LyricTimelineAnimation constants while live animation code inlines magic numbers
findings.detail: Of the seven constants in LyricTimelineAnimation, only plainRevealTranslationY is used — and it is misused as a layout inset (`let verticalInset = LyricTimelineAnimation.plainRevealTranslationY / 2`, LyricTimelineCell.swift:34), not as an animation translation. initialRevealDuration/initialRevealStagger/outgoingFadeDuration/outgoingFadeStagger/plainRevealDuration/easeOutOptions are dead, while the actual fade-in animation hardcodes `duration: 0.5, delay: Double(order) * 0.1` inline (LyricTimelineView.swift:162-164). The file also holds two unrelated exports (LyricTimelineAnimation + LyricTimelineLineStyle) under a third name, LyricTimelineStyle.
findings.suggestion: Delete the dead animation constants, name the live 0.5/0.1 reveal duration/stagger in their place, and give the cell its own verticalInset constant. Rename the file (or split) so the filename matches its exports.

findings.title: Lyric cells bypass TableBaseCell and re-set selectionStyle inline
findings.detail: AGENTS.md Cell Rules: "All table/collection cells inherit from TableBaseCell". LyricTimelineCell (line 5), LyricTimelineMessageCell, LyricTimelineSpacerCell, and LyricSelectionCell (LyricSelectionSheetViewController.swift:196) all subclass UITableViewCell directly and hand-set `selectionStyle = .none` (line 30 here; MessageCell:25, SpacerCell:12). The queue cells in Components/ correctly inherit TableBaseCell, so the same feature contains both patterns, and NowPlayingQueueSectionView additionally re-sets `cell.selectionStyle = .none` on already-compliant cells (lines 331, 715).
findings.suggestion: Rebase the LyricTimeline cells on TableBaseCell and drop the inline selectionStyle assignments (including the redundant ones in NowPlayingQueueSectionView). Keep LyricSelectionCell separate only if multi-select editing genuinely needs the system selected background, and document that exception.

findings.title: Dead private Animation constants enum
findings.detail: The private `enum Animation { duration = 1.0, damping = 1.05, initialVelocity = 0.75 }` (lines 13-17) is never referenced anywhere in the file — the controller performs no animations of its own. It is leftover scaffolding that suggests spring behavior that does not exist.
findings.suggestion: Delete the unused Animation enum.

findings.title: Haptic generator created on demand instead of stored as a property
findings.detail: `UINotificationFeedbackGenerator().notificationOccurred(.success)` is constructed inline at fire time (line 163), and the same on-demand pattern appears in the lyric copy action (LyricTimelineView+Actions.swift:57). AGENTS.md Cell Rules require haptic generators to be stored as instance properties, not created on demand — a convention every other haptic site in this scope follows (e.g. NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell.buttonFeedbackGenerator, ProgressTrackView.feedbackGenerator).
findings.suggestion: Store a `private let copyFeedbackGenerator = UINotificationFeedbackGenerator()` on the controller (and on LyricTimelineView for the copy action), gated #if os(iOS) where the type is shared.

findings.title: Write-only Presentation.shouldAnimatePlaybackStateChange and uncalled content(for:)
findings.detail: `shouldAnimatePlaybackStateChange` is computed and stored on every apply(snapshot:) (NowPlayingControlIslandViewModel+Playback.swift:21) but read nowhere in app code or tests (repo-wide grep finds only the declaration, init parameter, and assignment). Similarly `content(for:)` (+Playback.swift:26-28) has zero call sites. Both inflate the Presentation API and make readers hunt for consumers that do not exist.
findings.suggestion: Remove shouldAnimatePlaybackStateChange from Presentation and delete content(for:); reintroduce them only when a consumer exists.

findings.title: Dead elapsedText/remainingText helpers while views hand-roll the same formatting
findings.detail: `elapsedText` (line 37) and `remainingText` (line 41) have no call sites anywhere (verified by grep across all targets); `progress` (line 45) is referenced only by a test. Meanwhile NowPlayingPlaybackTimeRowView re-implements the identical formatting inline (`"-\(formattedPlaybackTime(max(effectiveDuration - currentTime, 0)))"`, NowPlayingPlaybackTimeRowView.swift:70-71) and ProgressTrackView re-implements the clamped-ratio computation (ProgressTrackView.swift:73-77).
findings.suggestion: Delete the unused computed properties (keeping `progress` only if the test coverage is deemed valuable), or — if the helpers are meant to be canonical — extract the elapsed/remaining/ratio formatting into shared free helpers next to formattedPlaybackTime and use them from both views.

findings.title: Enum case .title is never constructed; all its handling is dead
findings.detail: `case title(String)` is pattern-matched in four accessors (lines 16, 25, 34, 43) and in NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell.configure (NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell.swift:110-116), but a repo-wide grep shows it is never built — every construction site uses `.controls(...)` (AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshotBuilder.swift:50, AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshot.swift:14, NowPlayingListSectionView.swift:94). Maintainers of the header cell must keep an unreachable branch (and its hide-actions/zero-width logic) alive.
findings.suggestion: Delete the .title case, collapse AMNowPlayingQueueHeaderContent into a struct with title/repeatMode/isShuffleFeedbackActive/isShuffleEnabled fields, and remove the dead branch from NowPlayingQueueHeaderCell.configure.

findings.title: Pure view-model builder reaches into a UIKit view for identity scheme and limits
findings.detail: The nonisolated builder constructs item IDs via `NowPlayingQueueSectionView.ItemIdentifier.track(trackID:occurrence:)` (line 26) and defaults its limits to `NowPlayingQueueSectionView.Layout.maxVisibleHistoryTracks/maxVisibleQueueTracks` (lines 10-11). A value-layer type under ViewModel/Queue thereby depends on a @MainActor UIView class in Sections/, inverting the intended direction (the AM* layer was deliberately decoupled from MuseAmpPlayerKit via AMNowPlayingQueueTrackPresenting, yet is coupled to a concrete view).
findings.suggestion: Move the ItemIdentifier namespace and the maxVisible* limits into ViewModel/Queue (e.g. onto AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshot or a dedicated AMNowPlayingQueueItemID enum) and have NowPlayingQueueSectionView consume them, so the builder has no view dependency.

findings.title: update(using:animated:) silently ignores its animated parameter
findings.detail: The signature is `func update(using backgroundSource: BackgroundSource, animated _: Bool)` — the parameter is discarded, yet callers carefully pass meaningful values (`animated: presentation.shouldAnimateTransition` in NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift:169-172, `animated: false` in applyInitialPlaybackPresentation and prepareForPopupPresentation). The background actually always animates because NowPlayingArtworkBackgroundView.apply hardcodes `setColors(colors, animated: true)`. The parameter is a behavioral lie that readers must debug to discover.
findings.suggestion: Either honor the flag (thread it into backgroundView.apply / setColors(animated:)) or remove the parameter from the signature and from all call sites.

findings.title: refreshControlIslandContent(animated:) parameter is dead across the whole hierarchy
findings.detail: The protocol requires `func refreshControlIslandContent(animated: Bool)` (line 52) but the only implementation is the default `func refreshControlIslandContent(animated _: Bool)` (line 184) which ignores it; neither NowPlayingCompactController nor NowPlayingRelaxedController overrides it. Call sites pass `animated: false` (bindContentSelector:84, NowPlayingLifecycleShellController.swift:45) implying a choice that does not exist.
findings.suggestion: Drop the parameter from the protocol requirement, default implementation, and call sites, or implement the animated behavior if it was intended.

findings.title: NowPlayingPlaybackShellController extension misfiled in NowPlayingShellController.swift
findings.detail: Lines 145-172 extend `NowPlayingPlaybackShellController` (bindCleanSongTitlePreference) inside the file named for NowPlayingShellController, which already hosts four other declarations (NowPlayingShellController, NowPlayingQueueActionPresenting plus two conformance extensions, NowPlayingQueueShellController with its default implementation). The repo's convention is filename = export with Type+Feature.swift extensions; a maintainer looking for playback-shell behavior in NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift will not find this binding.
findings.suggestion: Move the bindCleanSongTitlePreference extension into NowPlayingPlaybackShellController.swift, and consider splitting the NowPlayingQueueActionPresenting protocol + conformances into their own file.

findings.title: updateInterfaceSuspensionState takes an inout parameter shadowing the protocol property of the same name
findings.detail: `func updateInterfaceSuspensionState(_ suspended: Bool, isInterfaceSuspended: inout Bool)` exists only because NowPlayingPlaybackShellController declares `var isInterfaceSuspended: Bool { get }` (read-only), so the mixin cannot write it. Each conformer therefore defines setInterfaceSuspended that passes `&isInterfaceSuspended` back into the helper (NowPlayingCompactController.swift:154, NowPlayingRelaxedController.swift:226). The inout parameter named identically to the protocol property makes the data flow needlessly hard to follow.
findings.suggestion: Change the protocol requirement to `var isInterfaceSuspended: Bool { get set }` (conformers already store it), let the default implementation assign it directly, and delete the inout parameter plus the per-controller setInterfaceSuspended forwarding.

findings.title: Empty lifecycle overrides and empty deinit are dead code
findings.detail: viewDidAppear (lines 104-106) and viewDidLayoutSubviews (lines 108-110) only call super, and `deinit {}` (line 127) is empty. The same pattern appears in NowPlayingRelaxedController.swift (viewDidLayoutSubviews 108-110, deinit 139), LyricTimelineView.swift layoutSubviews (183-185), and EdgeFadeBlurView.swift layoutSubviews (28-30). Each one signals customization that does not exist.
findings.suggestion: Delete the no-op overrides and empty deinits in all four files.

findings.title: No-op weak-self wrapper around onToggleShuffle diverges from the relaxed controller
findings.detail: installQueueActionHandlers wraps only onToggleShuffle in `{ [weak self] in guard self != nil else { return }; onToggleShuffle() }` (lines 140-143) while forwarding the other six handlers directly — and NowPlayingRelaxedController.swift:150-159 forwards all seven directly. The wrapper adds no behavior (the closures built in bindQueueSectionActions already capture self weakly) and makes a reader hunt for a difference that does not exist.
findings.suggestion: Pass onToggleShuffle straight through like the other handlers, matching the relaxed controller.

findings.title: Trailing guards with empty bodies are leftover dead code in three methods
findings.detail: applySupplementalPlaybackProgress ends with `guard controlIslandViewModel.selectedContentSelector == .lyrics else { return }` followed by nothing (lines 28-29); refreshPlayingContent ends with `guard selector == .lyrics else { return }` followed by nothing (lines 88-90); and NowPlayingRelaxedController+Playback.swift:29-31 has an entire body that is just `guard currentRightPanel == .lyrics else { return }`. These are remnants of removed lyric-progress pushes and mislead readers into believing lyric-specific work happens behind them.
findings.suggestion: Delete the trailing guards; make the relaxed applySupplementalPlaybackProgress an explicit empty implementation (or remove the requirement for controllers that need no supplemental progress).

findings.title: makeShowLyricsAction/makeShowPlaybackQueueAction duplicated verbatim across both controllers
findings.detail: Lines 93-109 are byte-identical to NowPlayingRelaxedController+Playback.swift:75-91 (same titles, SF symbols, and controlIslandViewModel.setContentSelector bodies), and the surrounding updateTransportSongMenu construction is also largely repeated. Any wording or symbol change must now be made twice.
findings.suggestion: Hoist both factories (and the shared songContextMenuProvider menu assembly) into an extension on NowPlayingPlaybackShellController where Self: UIViewController, since both controllers already conform and own controlIslandViewModel.

findings.title: Stray empty MARK sections
findings.detail: Back-to-back `// MARK: - Right Panel` and `// MARK: - Layout Containers` (lines 65-67) precede a single property, and `// MARK: - Background Setup` (line 162) is immediately followed by `// MARK: - Content Layout` with no members between them — leftover section headers from moved code that now mislabel the file's structure in the Xcode jump bar.
findings.suggestion: Delete the empty MARK headers and keep only those that label actual sections.

findings.title: Trailing guard exists only to gate a misleading 'snapshot start' log
findings.detail: In updateQueue, `guard update.appliedSnapshot else { return }` (lines 76-78) is followed solely by an AppLog.info reading "queue snapshot start ..." (lines 80-83) — but updateQueuePresentation already applied the snapshot synchronously on line 66, so the "start" message fires after the work completed and the guard protects nothing else. Readers of the logs and of the code both get the wrong picture of ordering.
findings.suggestion: Fold the information into the existing "queue refresh apply" log (which already records identity/content/player change flags) and delete the trailing guard, or move the start log before updateQueuePresentation.

findings.title: +DataSource filename mislabels a snapshot-mapping helper
findings.detail: In this codebase a +DataSource suffix means UITableView/UICollectionView data-source conformance (the table data source for this view actually lives in superclass NowPlayingQueueSectionView). This file contains only makeQueueSnapshot — a PlaybackTrack-to-AMNowPlayingQueueSnapshot mapping wrapper — so the responsibility-based filename points readers to the wrong place.
findings.suggestion: Rename the file to NowPlayingListSectionView+Snapshot.swift (or fold the 15-line helper back into NowPlayingListSectionView.swift).

findings.title: Dead stored properties: displayedArtworkURL and an unused cancellables set
findings.detail: `private var displayedArtworkURL: URL?` (line 8) is never read or written anywhere, and the outer class's `private var cancellables = Set<AnyCancellable>()` (line 9) is never stored into — the only Combine subscription lives in the nested NowPlayingArtworkImageView, which has its own set (line 41). Both properties imply state tracking that does not exist.
findings.suggestion: Delete both properties (and the then-unneeded Combine import usage on the outer type).

findings.title: Two hand-rolled responder-chain walks duplicate each other and SwifterSwift's parentViewController
findings.detail: layoutAnimationContainerView() (lines 5-14) and owningViewController() (lines 51-60) contain the identical while-loop responder walk, differing only in returning vc.view vs vc. LyricTimelineView+Actions.swift:75-77 implements the same walk a third way via sequence(first:next:). SwifterSwift — an AGENTS-listed preferred library whose UIView.parentViewController is explicitly called out — already provides this.
findings.suggestion: Replace both helpers with SwifterSwift's `parentViewController` (layoutAnimationContainerView becomes `parentViewController?.view ?? self`), and use the same accessor in LyricTimelineView+Actions.

findings.title: Transport view performs app-root navigation by digging into MainController
findings.detail: navigateToCurrentAlbum has a UIView casting `window?.rootViewController as? MainController` (line 23), branching on mainController.currentLayoutMode, choosing between compactTabBarController.selectedViewController and activeContentNavigationController, and pushing after a magic `DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 0.5)` (line 29) to outwait the popup-close animation. A leaf transport view thus encodes the entire app shell topology — three abstraction levels above its station — while every other interaction in this scope flows out through closures (onLyricTapped, onPageChanged) or shell-controller mixins.
findings.suggestion: Expose an `onNavigateToAlbum: (PlaybackTrack) -> Void` callback on the transport view (matching the existing closure convention) and implement the close-popup-then-push sequence in the controller/root layer; name the 0.5s popup-close delay as a constant where it lands.

findings.title: currentRightPanel state duplicated between shell view and controller
findings.detail: Both NowPlayingRelaxedShellView (`private(set) var currentRightPanel: NowPlayingRelaxedPanel = .lyrics`, line 15) and NowPlayingRelaxedController (NowPlayingRelaxedController.swift:69) store the same panel state, each guarding its own copy in switchRightPanel (view line 66, controller line 202). AGENTS.md Property Rules forbid stored properties tracking state already available from an existing source of truth; two copies can drift if either switch path is called directly.
findings.suggestion: Keep the stored state in one place — drop the controller's property and read relaxedShellView.currentRightPanel (or make the controller the sole owner and pass the target panel through).

findings.title: Identical button-configuration boilerplate repeated across all four control buttons
findings.detail: PlayPausePlayerControlButton (lines 7-17), NextPlayerControlButton, PreviousPlayerControlButton, and FavoritePlayerControlButton each repeat the same 10-line block: plain configuration, white baseForegroundColor, zero contentInsets, symbol configuration differing only in pointSize, white tintColor, setImage, accessibilityLabel. Three of them also repeat the `alpha = isEnabled ? 1 : 0.1` disabled treatment in bindState — and 0.1 already has a name (NowPlayingTransportView.Layout.unavailableTransportButtonAlpha) that none of them use.
findings.suggestion: Add a `configureSymbolAppearance(systemName:pointSize:accessibilityLabel:)` helper and a `setAvailable(_:)` helper (using Layout.unavailableTransportButtonAlpha) on the PlayerControlButton base class, and call them from the subclasses.

findings.title: Tag-based button dispatch with magic 1/2 instead of identity comparison
