findings.detail: Buttons are assigned `button.tag = tag + 1` in a loop (line 80) and handleTap switches on `sender.tag` cases 1 and 2 (lines 159-166) to choose between onShuffleTap and onRepeatTap. The numeric indirection forces readers to reconstruct loop order to know which tag is which, when the two buttons are already stored properties.
findings.suggestion: Drop the tags and dispatch on identity (`if sender === shuffleButton { onShuffleTap() } else if sender === repeatButton { onRepeatTap() }`), or use per-button UIAction handlers.

findings.title: removeAnimation(forKey: "queueShuffleFade") removes a key that is never added
findings.detail: animateShuffleFeedback calls `shuffleButton.layer.removeAnimation(forKey: "queueShuffleFade")` (line 200), but no animation is ever added under that key anywhere in the repo (grep finds only this line); the subsequent Interface.keyframeAnimate uses UIView block animations with system-generated keys. The call is a no-op leftover that implies a CAAnimation pathway that does not exist.
findings.suggestion: Delete the removeAnimation line; the wrapper's .beginFromCurrentState semantics already handle re-entry per the project's animation rules.

result.reasoning: Verified at SubsonicClientKit/Sources/SubsonicClientKit/SubsonicMusicService.swift:152: perform() guards `baseURL.host != "example.com"` and throws a localized "No server configured." error, with zero explanation in the package — no comment, no named constant, and no other occurrence of the literal in package sources. The sentinel's meaning lives entirely in another module: MuseAmp/Application/AppPreferences.swift:29 defines defaultAPIBaseURL = "https://example.com/rest", which is the default threaded through AppEnvironment, AppEnvironment+Bootstrap, and MuseAmpTV/TVAppContext. Grep confirms this guard is the ONLY unconfigured-server gate in the repo (no isConfigured check in APIClient or UI), so changing the app placeholder would silently disable the friendly error, as the finding claims. Corroborating evidence of the hidden contract: package tests deliberately use subdomains (music.example.com in LiveFixtureTests.swift:8, cache.example.com in CacheTests.swift:56) to dodge the exact-host match, and dozens of app-target tests construct APIClient with bare https://example.com, implicitly relying on the guard to suppress network calls — a second undocumented dependent. AGENTS.md does not endorse sentinel hosts; the repo's conventions favor centralized named keys (AppNotificationUserInfoKey, AppPreferences/ConfigurableKit), so the suggested fix (named constant or explicit unconfigured API visible to both sides) matches local style. Severity adjusted to medium: it is a real maintenance trap that misleads readers and can breed a bug, but there is no evidence it has already caused one — both sides currently agree on the literal.

Verified against SubsonicMusicService.swift: playback(id:) (lines 116-139) hand-duplicates perform()'s cache key format (line 117 vs 158 — byte-identical interpolation), manually checks playbackCache.freshValue, fetches through fetchSubsonicSong (whose only caller is playback, and which passes cache: nil into perform), and manually calls setValue. The behavioral divergence is provable from perform()'s guards: cache == nil disables coalescing (line 177), disk read (164), disk write (203), and stale fallback (213), so playback alone loses all three behaviors while its cache property sits indistinguishably beside the four pipeline-backed caches. Refutations failed: disk-caching is raw-payload-based with decode re-run on load, so the auth-bearing playback URL would not be persisted by the suggested fix; the .stream-vs-.song key distinction is unnecessary because caches are separate typed instances; git history (single init commit) shows no deliberate design decision. AGENTS.md says nothing endorsing the pattern, and the dominant module convention is the perform() funnel used by all five other public methods — the suggestion increases conformance with local style. Medium severity is honest: real maintenance seam plus a reader-invisible behavioral inconsistency (playback fails hard offline while album/song serve stale data), but no demonstrated shipped bug.

Confirmed verbatim in SubsonicMusicService.swift: the auth-fallback catch (lines 195-200) and the main success tail (lines 202-207) are identical decode→cache.setValue→storeToDisk→return sequences differing only in which payload/Data they use, and lines 173-178 are an if/else whose branches differ only in coalescingKey vs nil. The 80-line perform() (147-226) nests do/catch inside do/catch with the inner catch returning rather than rethrowing, making the duplicated tail a second, easy-to-miss exit. The suggested fix matches the file's own dominant style — staleFallback (lines 435-456) is already an extracted generic tail helper taking the same @escaping @Sendable decode closure — so the refactor would read like the rest of the module, and collapsing 173-178 to a single ternary-derived coalescingKey is strictly simpler. AGENTS.md (byte-identical to project CLAUDE.md) contains nothing endorsing duplicated finishing sequences; its style rules favor early returns, responsibility splits, and shared helpers. Severity stays medium because the duplicate copy lives in the rarely-exercised plain-auth fallback branch (only hit when a server rejects token auth), so a change applied only to the hot-path tail (cache write ordering, logging, envelope semantics) would silently diverge without being caught in normal testing.

Verified exactly at the cited lines: loadFreshDataFromDisk (382-399) and loadStaleDataFromDisk (409-422) are verbatim copies except the TTL guard at 394-396, and cacheTTL is the constant .infinity (line 18), making the guard unfailable — the functions are currently behaviorally identical. The duplication includes a side-effecting version-mismatch removal (provider.remove), the kind of copied logic that diverges silently under maintenance. The fresh/stale naming does mirror the in-memory ResponseCache convention (freshValue/staleValue), so the naming-misleads angle is overstated and the finding's secondary suggestion to delete the TTL plumbing should be rejected; but the primary suggestion (extract a shared validCachedEnvelope(forKey:) helper, leaving the TTL comparison as the call-site difference) is a genuine, small clarity gain that matches repo style (guard/early-return) and violates nothing in AGENTS.md. Severity remains low: same-file private duplication, no bug bred yet.

findings.title: makeTrackRecord performs no readability, playability, or duration-sanity validation
findings.detail: makeTrackRecord only calls `asset.load(.duration)` (line 55) and metadata loads. There is no check that (a) the file content is actually decodable as audio (e.g. AVAudioPlayer creation or an audio-track presence check via load(.tracks)), or (b) the duration is sane. Line 56 `max(CMTimeGetSeconds(duration), 0)` accepts 0 exactly, accepts arbitrarily huge values (no <24h cap), and passes NaN through unchanged (Swift `max(NaN, 0)` returns NaN because `0 >= NaN` is false), so an indefinite CMTime would persist NaN into the WCDB index. Concrete failure: a truncated faststart M4A (moov atom intact, mdat incomplete — the layout ExportMetadataProcessor and most encoders produce) loads its full nominal duration from the header, so during rebuild (LibraryScanner.swift:110) and ingest (DatabaseManager+Writes.swift:72) the corrupt file is indexed as a fully healthy track with full duration. Playback then fails or cuts off mid-track, and nothing ever re-validates it: subsequent rebuilds skip the file because size/mtime match the stored record (LibraryScanner.swift:92-95). Zero-duration files (metadata-only containers, renamed non-audio files with parseable headers) are likewise indexed and surface in the UI as 0:00 tracks. The kit-side fallbacks `metadata.durationSeconds ?? 0` (LibraryScanner.swift:119, DatabaseManager+Writes.swift:91) bake the same acceptance of 0 into the records.
findings.suggestion: In makeTrackRecord (so iOS, tvOS, and AudioFileImporter all inherit it), after loading duration: verify the asset has at least one audio track (try await asset.load(.tracks) / asset.loadTracks(withMediaType: .audio) non-empty, or attempt AVAudioPlayer(contentsOf:) for local files), and throw EmbeddedMetadataReaderError for durationSeconds that is !isFinite, <= 1.0, or >= 86_400. Callers already have throw-handling paths (scanner catch, ingest throw), so invalid files become explicit failures instead of silently indexed records.

findings.title: Single unreadable file aborts the entire rebuild because the per-file stat is outside the per-file catch
findings.detail: `try FileManager.default.attributesOfItem(atPath:)` at line 88 sits in the scan loop but OUTSIDE the do/catch that begins at line 109, so one failing stat throws out of rebuildIndexFromDisk entirely: all accumulated upserts (line 152) are discarded, stale-row deletion (line 153-154) and orphan pruning never run, setLastRebuild is never written, and DatabaseManager+Writes.swift:21 has already sent .indexRebuildStarted with no matching finished event. This is reachable: rebuildIndexFromDisk is a nonisolated async func, so it does NOT run on @DatabaseActor and interleaves freely with actor-isolated ingestAudioFile, whose moveToLibrary briefly removes an existing finalURL during re-download finalization (LibraryFileManager.swift:36-39); removeTrack is fully synchronous on any caller thread (MusicLibraryTrackRemovalService.swift:20 -> sendSynchronously) and deletes files mid-scan; and a permission-denied/protected file in the audio directory stats with an error. The file list was captured up front by discoverAudioFiles, so any file that disappears or becomes unreadable between enumeration and its turn in the loop converts a one-file problem into 'Rebuild Failed' for the whole library (SettingsViewController+Actions.swift:172-183).
findings.suggestion: Move the attributesOfItem call (lines 88-90) inside the per-file do/catch, treating a stat failure as that single file being skipped (or invalid) rather than aborting the scan; alternatively catch around lines 88-90 separately and `continue`. Also emit a terminal rebuild event on the failure path in DatabaseManager+Writes.rebuildIndex so observers are not left with an unfinished indexRebuildStarted.

findings.title: Any inspectAudioFile error permanently deletes the audio file and its index row — transient failures are indistinguishable from corruption
findings.detail: The catch at lines 142-148 treats every thrown error identically: the relativePath is marked invalid and, because the app always rebuilds with pruneInvalidFiles: true (SongLibraryIndexer.swift:33 — both the Settings rebuild and the casual Albums-tab 'Refresh Library' at SongLibraryViewController+Actions.swift:85 go through it), the file is removed from disk (line 146) and its existing index row is deleted via line 153 (`|| invalidRelativePaths.contains($0)`). inspectAudioFile throws not only for genuinely corrupt files but for transient conditions: permission/file-protection errors, the file momentarily absent or half-written because moveToLibrary's remove-then-move window (LibraryFileManager.swift:36-39) interleaves with the off-actor scan, AVFoundation resource pressure, or a file mid-copy via Files-app sharing. In all of those cases a perfectly valid, fully-downloaded song is irreversibly deleted from disk. Only the Settings flow warns 'Unreadable files will be removed.' (SettingsViewController+Actions.swift:100); the Albums-tab refresh says only 'Scanning saved songs...' yet performs the same destructive prune. Note also the inconsistency with the forceArtwork branch (lines 96-105), where an inspection failure on an unchanged file is merely logged — the same error class is fatal-to-the-file in one branch and benign in the other.
findings.suggestion: Stop deleting files on arbitrary inspection errors. Classify errors: prune only structural path violations (already handled at lines 68-75) and files that fail a cheap deterministic corruption check (e.g. unreadable header on a direct FileHandle read) across more than one attempt; for AVFoundation errors, record the path as 'needs attention' / quarantine into a separate directory instead of removeItem, and never include transiently-unreadable existing tracks in the deletedPaths index purge.

findings.title: ingestAudioFile inspects after moveToLibrary; inspection failure strands the file at its final library path with no record and no cleanup
findings.detail: Line 63 moves the source file to its final library location, then line 72 calls dependencies.inspectAudioFile(moved.finalURL). If inspection throws (truncated/corrupt/unreadable download), ingestAudioFile rethrows with the file already at Audio/<albumID>/<trackID>.<ext>: there is no rollback, no removeItem, and upsertTracks (line 108) never runs. Concrete consequences: (1) LAN-transfer/import path — AudioFileImporter catches the error and deletes only its staging copy (AudioFileImporter.swift:289-291), but on re-import of the same track the orphan at the destination trips the fileExists check at AudioFileImporter.swift:238 and the import is reported as `.duplicate`, so the user re-transferring a song sees 'duplicate(s) skipped' while the track never appears in the library — a silent, persistent inconsistency curable only by a destructive prune rebuild (this applies to the tvOS transfer flow too, which uses the symlinked AudioFileImporter). (2) Download path — completeFinalization marks the job failed (DownloadManager+Persistence.swift:66-80) while the corrupt audio sits at the exact final path; if the inspection failure was transient, a later rebuild indexes that file as a normal track (with sourceKind .unknown instead of .downloaded, since the bootstrap closure hardcodes .unknown) while the failed DownloadJob still exists in the state store — the Downloads UI then shows a failed download for a track that is simultaneously present in the library, and tapping retry deletes the now-indexed file (cleanupLocalAudioArtifacts) leaving a dangling index row until re-ingest completes.
findings.suggestion: Inspect before committing the move: call dependencies.inspectAudioFile on the source/staging URL first and only moveToLibrary after inspection succeeds; or wrap lines 69-107 so that on any thrown error the moved file is removed (or moved back to the source URL) before rethrowing, keeping disk state consistent with the index.

findings.title: AudioFileImporter has the same validation gap: corrupt-but-parseable and zero-duration files are imported into the library
findings.detail: importSingleFile loads duration at lines 180-181 (`max(CMTimeGetSeconds(duration), 0)`) and builds the record via makeTrackRecord at line 248, with no playability check and no duration sanity bounds anywhere. A zero-duration file or a truncated file with an intact moov header passes every gate (catalog-ID check, title/artist check, duplicate checks) and is staged and ingested into the library as a healthy track (line 287). Only files whose duration load throws outright are counted as errors by the caller loop (lines 116-119). Additionally the duplicate heuristics make corrupt batches worse: with durationSeconds 0, any two distinct corrupt files sharing title/artist/album within ±2s (DuplicateKey bucketing at line 161, isDuplicate at line 345) are collapsed as 'duplicates', and a NaN duration never matches `abs(track.durationSeconds - duration) < 2.0`, so an indexed NaN-duration track can be re-imported repeatedly without ever being detected as a duplicate.
findings.suggestion: Once validation is added to EmbeddedMetadataReader.makeTrackRecord (audio-track presence/AVAudioPlayer check plus finite 1s..24h duration bounds), importSingleFile inherits it via line 248; additionally treat a thrown validation error from makeTrackRecord as a distinct 'unplayable' result so the import summary can report it separately from generic errors, and validate the locally computed durationSeconds at line 181 before using it for duplicate detection.

findings.title: inspectAudioFile closure is duplicated verbatim between iOS and tvOS bootstrap instead of shared, guaranteeing drift when validation is added
findings.detail: EmbeddedMetadataReader.swift itself is shared with the TV target via relative symlink (MuseAmpTV/Backend/Library/EmbeddedMetadataReader.swift -> ../../../MuseAmp/Backend/Library/EmbeddedMetadataReader.swift, verified with ls -la), but the ~40-line inspectAudioFile closure at TVAppContext+Bootstrap.swift:88-127 is a byte-for-byte duplicate of AppEnvironment+Bootstrap.swift:83-122 in a regular (non-symlinked) file. Both closures contain the identical unvalidated path: stat with `fileSize ?? 0` / `modifiedAt ?? now` fallbacks, makeTrackRecord with no readability/playability/duration checks, and sourceKind hardcoded to .unknown (which is how rebuild-indexed tracks lose their .downloaded/.imported provenance). Any fix for the validation gap applied to the iOS closure will silently not apply to tvOS rebuild/ingest, and vice versa.
findings.suggestion: Hoist the closure body into the already-symlinked shared layer — e.g. a static EmbeddedMetadataReader.inspect(fileURL:paths:) returning AudioFileInspection — and have both makeRuntimeDependencies implementations delegate to it, so the readability/playability/duration validation lives in exactly one compiled-into-both-targets place.

findings.title: MuseAmpInterfaceKit package does not exist on disk
findings.detail: Top Level section claims '`MuseAmpInterfaceKit/` is a separate UIKit package for cross-platform interface primitives... shared by iOS/iPadOS/tvOS'. Reality: there is no MuseAmpInterfaceKit/ directory at the repo root (root contains only MuseAmpDatabaseKit/, MuseAmpPlayerKit/, SubsonicClientKit/ as local packages); `grep -rn "import MuseAmpInterfaceKit" --include="*.swift"` returns 0 matches across the entire repo; MuseAmp.xcworkspace/contents.xcworkspacedata references only MuseAmp.xcodeproj, SubsonicClientKit, MuseAmpDatabaseKit, MuseAmpPlayerKit, and Vendor/LNPopupController; MuseAmp.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj has XCLocalSwiftPackageReference entries only for SubsonicClientKit, MuseAmpPlayerKit, MuseAmpDatabaseKit; and `git log -- MuseAmpInterfaceKit` is empty (the package never existed in history).
findings.suggestion: Remove the MuseAmpInterfaceKit bullet from the Top Level section (or create the package if the migration is still intended), and re-point all rules that reference it to the real locations under MuseAmp/Interface/Common/ and MuseAmpTV/Interface/Common/.

findings.title: Entire MuseAmpInterfaceKit Package Reference entry is fictional; key types live in app targets
findings.detail: Lines 315-322 document a `MuseAmpInterfaceKit` package at path `MuseAmpInterfaceKit/` with `import MuseAmpInterfaceKit`, key types, and a Foundation/Media/Collections/Browse/Playback/TV layout. None of this exists. Every listed key type lives in the app targets instead: `Interface` -> MuseAmp/Interface/Common/Style/Interface.swift:4 (separate tvOS copy at MuseAmpTV/Interface/Common/Interface.swift:4), `InterfaceStyle` -> MuseAmp/Interface/Common/Style/InterfaceStyle.swift:3, `TableBaseCell` -> MuseAmp/Interface/Common/TableBaseCell.swift:4, `EmptyStateView` -> MuseAmp/Interface/Common/EmptyStateView.swift:5, `MediaDetailViewController` -> MuseAmp/Interface/Common/MediaDetailViewController.swift:5, `AMPlaybackControlling` -> MuseAmp/Interface/NowPlaying/ViewModel/Playback/AMPlaybackControlling.swift:9, `AMNowPlayingContent` -> MuseAmp/Interface/NowPlaying/ViewModel/Playback/AMNowPlayingContent.swift:4, `AMTVLibrarySessionState` -> MuseAmpTV/Backend/Models/TVLibrarySessionState.swift:3. No package directory, no imports, no project/workspace reference.
findings.suggestion: Delete the `### MuseAmpInterfaceKit` section from the Package Reference, or replace it with a note documenting the real in-target locations of these shared UI types (MuseAmp/Interface/Common/ + Style/, MuseAmp/Interface/NowPlaying/ViewModel/Playback/, MuseAmpTV/Backend/Models/, MuseAmpTV/Interface/Common/).

findings.title: Cell Rules attribute TableBaseCell to nonexistent MuseAmpInterfaceKit
findings.detail: The rule says 'All table/collection cells inherit from `TableBaseCell` (MuseAmpInterfaceKit)'. TableBaseCell is actually an app-target class declared at MuseAmp/Interface/Common/TableBaseCell.swift:4 (`class TableBaseCell: UITableViewCell`); there is no MuseAmpInterfaceKit package and no file imports one. The behavioral content of the rule (selectionStyle = .none, hidden selectedBackgroundView) is accurate, but the parenthetical module attribution points readers to a module that does not exist.
findings.suggestion: Change the attribution to the real path: `TableBaseCell` (MuseAmp/Interface/Common/TableBaseCell.swift).

findings.title: Interface/Common 'compatibility shims into MuseAmpInterfaceKit' claim describes a migration that does not exist
findings.detail: Line 52 says Interface/Common/ 'may contain thin compatibility shims that forward old app-target type names into `MuseAmpInterfaceKit` while feature migration is in progress', and line 49 similarly instructs keeping tvOS focus/navigation logic in MuseAmpTV/Interface/Root/ 'rather than in `MuseAmpInterfaceKit`'. There is no MuseAmpInterfaceKit package on disk, zero imports of it, and MuseAmp/Interface/Common/ contains full concrete implementations (TableBaseCell.swift, EmptyStateView.swift, MediaDetailViewController.swift, Style/Interface.swift, Style/InterfaceStyle.swift), not forwarding shims. The doc encodes guidance around a migration target that was never committed.
findings.suggestion: Drop the shim/migration language from lines 49 and 52, describing Interface/Common/ as the home of shared UI primitives (base cells, empty state, media detail, Style/) instead.

findings.title: LyricTimelineView no longer lives in NowPlaying/Components; LyricTimeline/ subdirectory is undocumented
findings.detail: Line 66 documents `Interface/NowPlaying/Components/` as containing 'LyricTimelineView, artwork background, queue cells, route picker'. In reality LyricTimelineView is defined at MuseAmp/Interface/NowPlaying/LyricTimeline/LyricTimelineView.swift:6, inside a dedicated `Interface/NowPlaying/LyricTimeline/` subdomain (with +Actions/+Binding/+Delegate extensions, LyricTimelineStyle.swift, and Cells/ and Lyrics/ subfolders including LyricParser.swift and LyricLine.swift). The NowPlaying subdirectory list at lines 63-68 (Controller, ViewModel, Sections, Components, LyricSheet, Support) omits LyricTimeline/ entirely, violating the doc's own Documentation Sync rule for new Interface subdomains.
findings.suggestion: Add an `Interface/NowPlaying/LyricTimeline/` bullet (lyric timeline view, its cells, and lyric parsing models) and remove LyricTimelineView from the Components/ description.

findings.title: Application/ 'contains only' file list omits MacCatalystTerminationPolicy.swift
findings.detail: Line 27 states `Application/` 'contains only app lifecycle and configuration' and enumerates exactly seven files (AppDelegate, SceneDelegate, AppEnvironment, AppEnvironment+Bootstrap, AppEnvironment+Events, AppEnvironment+Transfer, AppPreferences). The directory actually contains an eighth file, MuseAmp/Application/MacCatalystTerminationPolicy.swift, which is not in the list, making the exhaustive 'contains only' claim stale.
findings.suggestion: Add `MacCatalystTerminationPolicy.swift` to the enumerated Application/ file list.

findings.title: Top Level section omits real top-level directories Website/, MuseAmpTests/, and Resources/
findings.detail: The Top Level structure list (lines 18-23) documents only MuseAmp/, MuseAmpTV/, MuseAmpDatabaseKit/, SubsonicClientKit/, MuseAmpInterfaceKit/ (nonexistent), and MuseAmpPlayerKit/. The repo root also contains: `Website/` (a Vite/TypeScript site with index.html, src/, vite.config.ts, package.json) which is mentioned nowhere in AGENTS.md; `MuseAmpTests/` (the app test target with Application/, Downloads/, Library/, Lyrics/, NowPlaying/, Playback/, Playlist/, Search/, Sync/ test folders) which is never described as a directory despite a whole Testing Rules section; and `Resources/` (AdditionalLicenses/ plus an undocumented DevKit/ folder) which is only referenced in passing at line 244. Vendor/ similarly appears only via the format-exclusion note at line 245.
findings.suggestion: Add Top Level bullets for `Website/`, `MuseAmpTests/`, `Resources/` (AdditionalLicenses + DevKit), and `Vendor/` (vendored submodules such as LNPopupController), and remove the MuseAmpInterfaceKit/ bullet.

findings.title: Symlink mirroring rule documented only for Backend/Sync but actually spans most of MuseAmpTV/Backend
findings.detail: Line 100 documents relative-symlink mirroring as specific to `MuseAmpTV/Backend/Sync/`, instructing matching symlinks only 'when adding a new Sync file'. `find MuseAmpTV -type l` shows the same mechanism covers far more: MuseAmpTV/Backend/API/ (APIClient.swift, APIClient+Transfer.swift), Backend/Downloads/ (DownloadStore.swift, DownloadArtworkProcessor.swift), Backend/Library/ (8 MusicLibraryDatabase/importer files), Backend/Playback/ (11 PlaybackController/snapshot files), Backend/Playlist/ (5 files), Backend/Supplement/ (6 files), and even MuseAmpTV/Application/AppPreferences.swift — all relative symlinks into MuseAmp/. An agent adding a new Playback, Library, or Playlist file shared by the TV target gets no guidance that a symlink is required, exactly the failure mode this rule exists to prevent. This also undercuts line 19's claim that MuseAmpTV is limited to 'thin backend adapters'.
findings.suggestion: Generalize the rule: MuseAmpTV/Backend/* (API, Downloads, Library, Playback, Playlist, Supplement, Sync) and MuseAmpTV/Application/AppPreferences.swift mirror MuseAmp sources via relative symlinks; any new shared file in those subdomains needs a matching symlink in the same change.

findings.title: Documented amusic.* notification name list is incomplete
findings.detail: Line 160 enumerates the custom notification names as `.libraryDidSync`, `.artworkDidUpdate`, `.playlistArtworkDidUpdate`. The codebase defines three more in the same namespace: `amusic.lyricsDidUpdate` and `amusic.serverConfigurationDidChange` (MuseAmp/Backend/Library/LibraryChangeNotifications.swift:14-15) and `amusic.playlistsDidChange` (MuseAmp/Backend/Playlist/PlaylistStore.swift:14). The namespacing claim itself is correct; the enumeration is stale.
findings.suggestion: Extend the list with `.lyricsDidUpdate`, `.serverConfigurationDidChange`, and `.playlistsDidChange`, or reword the rule to make clear the three named notifications are examples rather than the full set.

findings.title: Any single inspectAudioFile failure permanently deletes the user's audio file and its index row
findings.detail: In the catch block at lines 142-149, every error thrown by dependencies.inspectAudioFile (line 110) is treated as 'file is invalid' and, because pruneInvalidFiles is true on every production rebuild (MuseAmp/Backend/Library/SongLibraryIndexer.swift:33 hardcodes pruneInvalidFiles: true, and the .pruneInvalidFiles command in DatabaseManager+Commands.swift:141 also passes true), the user's audio file is irreversibly removed from disk (line 146) and its index row is deleted via the invalidRelativePaths branch of deletedPaths (line 153). The production inspectAudioFile (AppEnvironment+Bootstrap.swift:83-122) is an AVFoundation pipeline: `try await asset.load(.duration)` and collectMetadataItems can throw for reasons unrelated to file corruption — transient resource pressure, an interrupted media-services daemon, or CancellationError if the enclosing Task is ever cancelled (AVAsset.load throws on cancellation; if a caller ever cancels the rebuild Task, every remaining file in the loop would be 'inspection failed' and mass-deleted). There is no retry, no quarantine, and no distinction between a structurally invalid path (lines 68-75, which is a legitimate prune) and a read error on a file that played fine yesterday. One flaky AVAsset load deletes a downloaded/imported song with no recovery besides re-downloading.
findings.suggestion: Only prune files that are structurally invalid (bad relative path or .tmp suffix). On an inspectAudioFile error, log, leave the file on disk, and keep the existing index row (do not append to invalidRelativePaths; the path is already in seenRelativePaths so the row survives). Optionally track an 'uninspectable' count in RebuildResult and only delete a file after N consecutive failed inspections across rebuilds. Also explicitly rethrow CancellationError instead of treating it as file invalidity.

findings.title: Uncaught attributesOfItem throw aborts the entire rebuild when one file vanishes mid-scan
findings.detail: Line 88 (`let attributes = try FileManager.default.attributesOfItem(atPath: fileURL.path)`) is a bare try outside the do/catch that only wraps the inspection block (lines 109-149). The file list is materialized once in discoverAudioFiles() before the loop, and the loop suspends on `await dependencies.inspectAudioFile` for every changed file, so the scan can run for minutes. During that window, @DatabaseActor work continues to run (rebuildIndexFromDisk is a nonisolated async function executing off the actor): removeTrackSynchronously / removeAlbumSynchronously (user deletes a song/album while 'Refreshing Library' runs) and DownloadManager.cleanupLocalAudioArtifacts both delete files inside the Audio directory. If any enumerated file is deleted before the scanner reaches its stat, line 88 throws and the whole rebuild aborts: upsertTracks/deleteTracks (lines 152-154) never run, all completed inspection work is discarded, invalid/.tmp files already pruned inline (lines 70-73, 145-148) are gone from disk while the index cleanup never executes, caches written at lines 137/140 for already-inspected new files become orphans, and DatabaseManager+Writes.rebuildIndex propagates the error after having sent .indexRebuildStarted with no terminal event.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the attributesOfItem call in a per-file do/catch: on failure, log and `continue` (the path is already in seenRelativePaths, so the existing row is preserved and nothing is deleted). A vanished file will be reconciled on the next rebuild.

findings.title: Orphan-cache prune races with concurrent ingestAudioFile and deletes a just-downloaded track's artwork/lyrics caches
findings.detail: rebuildIndexFromDisk is a nonisolated async function, so under the package's Swift 6 language mode (swift-tools 6.2, no NonisolatedNonsendingByDefault) it runs on the global concurrent executor while DatabaseActor stays free — DatabaseManager+Writes.rebuildIndex merely awaits it. Download finalization concurrently calls send(.ingestAudioFile) (MuseAmp/Backend/Downloads/DownloadManager+Digger.swift:275) on @DatabaseActor. ingestAudioFile writes the artwork cache (DatabaseManager+Writes.swift:75) and lyrics cache (line 79) BEFORE upserting the track row (line 108). Interleaving: scanner executes `let validTrackIDs = try indexStore.trackIDs()` (line 155) after ingest wrote the caches but before its upsert lands; pruneOrphanArtwork/pruneOrphanLyrics (lines 156-157) then delete the new track's .jpg/.lrc as orphans (trackID not in validTrackIDs); ingest's upsert lands afterwards with hasEmbeddedArtwork=true. Because writeArtwork is never re-attempted for snapshot-matching files on later rebuilds (lines 92-106 `continue`) and CacheCoordinator.writeArtwork early-returns when nothing changed, the index permanently claims artwork/lyrics that no longer exist on disk until the user runs a forceArtwork rebuild. Trigger is realistic: user taps 'Refreshing Library' (SongLibraryViewController+Actions.swift:85) while an album download is finalizing; pruneOrphans enumerates the whole cache directory, giving a sizable window.
findings.suggestion: Serialize rebuild with all other library writes: either make rebuildIndexFromDisk's DB/cache mutation tail (upsert, delete, prune) run on @DatabaseActor, or hold a manager-level write gate for the duration of the rebuild so ingest/remove commands queue behind it. Alternatively re-fetch trackIDs immediately before deleting each orphan candidate and skip IDs that appeared.

findings.title: ingestAudioFile orphans the old audio file when relativePath changes, and rebuilds then flip-flop the row between the two files
findings.detail: ingestAudioFile fetches `existing = try indexStore.track(byID:)` (line 83) only to preserve createdAt. When the re-ingested track's relativePath differs from existing.relativePath (album reorganization on the server changes albumID — e.g. re-import via LAN sync passes AudioFileImporter's title/artist/album/duration dedupe because the album name changed, and the destURL-exists check at AudioFileImporter.swift:238 checks only the NEW path), the upsert (line 108, INSERT OR REPLACE keyed on trackID PK per TrackRow.swift:90) silently repoints the row to the new path while the old file at existing.relativePath is never deleted. Consequence chain in the rebuild pipeline: on the next rebuildIndexFromDisk the orphaned old file is not in the snapshot, gets re-inspected and upserted (LibraryScanner.swift:135/152), and INSERT OR REPLACE replaces the row again — now pointing back at the OLD file; the new path was 'seen' so deleteTracks does not touch it. Every subsequent rebuild flips the row between the two paths (whichever file is absent from the snapshot gets re-inspected and wins), so the library alternates between two different audio files for the same trackID, updatedAt churns, and one file is always invisible/orphaned on disk.
findings.suggestion: In ingestAudioFile, after the upsert, compare existing?.relativePath with moved.relativePath; if they differ, delete the old file via fileManager.removeTrackFile(relativePath: existing.relativePath) (and clean its empty parent directory) so disk and index stay one-to-one.

findings.title: inspectAudioFile dependency discards embedded lyrics and provenance, so every rebuilt row gets hasEmbeddedLyrics=false and sourceKind=.unknown
findings.detail: The production RuntimeDependencies.inspectAudioFile closure builds ImportedTrackMetadata with `lyrics: nil` (line 115) and `sourceKind: .unknown` (line 116), even though EmbeddedMetadataReader.makeTrackRecord already computed hasEmbeddedLyrics (EmbeddedMetadataReader.swift:69/89) and the reader has an extractLyrics API. LibraryScanner then derives `hasEmbeddedLyrics: metadata.lyrics.nilIfEmpty != nil` (LibraryScanner.swift:129), which is therefore ALWAYS false in production, and the embedded-lyrics cache write at LibraryScanner.swift:139-141 is unreachable dead code. Net effect: any track indexed or re-indexed through the rebuild path (file modified, row lost, file landed on disk without ingest) silently flips hasEmbeddedLyrics true→false and sourceKind .downloaded/.imported→.unknown in the index, diverging from what the file actually contains; the flag is exported via AudioTrackRecord+AppModels.swift:35 (catalogSong.hasLyrics). It also means a rebuild can never restore lost .lrc caches from lyrics that are physically embedded in the files, defeating the rebuild's purpose as a disk-truth recovery tool.
findings.suggestion: In the inspectAudioFile closure, extract embedded lyrics (metadataReader.extractLyrics over the collected metadata items) and pass them through ImportedTrackMetadata.lyrics; alternatively extend AudioFileInspection to carry record.hasEmbeddedLyrics and have LibraryScanner use it. Preserve the existing row's sourceKind in the scanner (snapshot[relativePath]?.sourceKind ?? metadata.sourceKind) instead of overwriting with .unknown.

findings.title: Enumeration failure is indistinguishable from an empty library and mass-deletes every index row plus all artwork/lyrics caches
findings.detail: discoverAudioFiles returns [] when the enumerator cannot be created (line 29-30) and passes no errorHandler to FileManager.enumerator, so per Apple's documented behavior a mid-enumeration I/O error on a subdirectory silently continues, dropping that subtree from the result. rebuildIndexFromDisk has no guard distinguishing 'zero/partial files discovered' from 'user actually has an empty library': deletedPaths = all snapshot keys not seen (line 153), deleteTracks wipes those rows (line 154), and pruneOrphanArtwork/pruneOrphanLyrics (lines 156-157) then delete the corresponding artwork and lyrics cache files. Rows can be re-created by the next successful rebuild, but network-fetched lyrics caches (written only at download time by DownloadLyricsProcessor) are permanently lost because the rebuild's embedded-lyrics re-cache path is dead (see the inspectAudioFile finding), and createdAt/sourceKind are reset. A transient unreadable album directory thus silently degrades the library.
findings.suggestion: Pass an errorHandler to the enumerator that records any enumeration error and abort (throw) the rebuild instead of treating the partial listing as truth. Additionally, refuse the deletion phase (or require an explicit flag) when scanned == 0 while the snapshot is non-empty.

findings.title: Failed rebuild never records failure: audit reports stale lastRebuildSucceeded=true and no terminal event follows .indexRebuildStarted
findings.detail: rebuildIndex sends .indexRebuildStarted (line 21) and only on success writes setLastRebuild(timestamp:succeeded: true) (line 27). There is no catch path: when rebuildIndexFromDisk throws (e.g. the uncaught stat error in LibraryScanner.swift:88), no setLastRebuild(succeeded: false) is written and no failure/finished event is emitted. IndexStore.setLastRebuild is never called anywhere else with false (verified by grep), so auditSnapshot (DatabaseManager+Audit.swift:84) reports lastRebuildSucceeded from the PREVIOUS successful run — i.e. the diagnostics screen claims the last rebuild succeeded after it actually failed, and event subscribers that saw .indexRebuildStarted never receive a terminal event for that run.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the scanner call in do/catch: on error, try? indexStore.setLastRebuild(timestamp: .init(), succeeded: false), emit a failure event (e.g. .indexRebuildFailed or .indexRebuildFinished with an error flag), then rethrow.

findings.title: deletedPaths computation is O(tracks x invalidPaths) due to Array.contains inside filter
findings.detail: `snapshot.keys.filter { !seenRelativePaths.contains($0) || invalidRelativePaths.contains($0) }` performs a linear scan of the invalidRelativePaths Array for every snapshot key. With a large library (thousands of rows) and many invalid paths (e.g. a directory full of leftover .tmp files or a failing-inspection batch from the prune path), this is quadratic work executed inside the rebuild critical path, on top of the disk I/O. seenRelativePaths is already a Set; invalidRelativePaths is not.
findings.suggestion: Build a `let invalidSet = Set(invalidRelativePaths)` once before the filter and test membership against it.

findings.title: tempFileCount audit metric can never see real staged temp files
findings.detail: tempFileCount lists only the TOP level of paths.audioDirectory (non-recursive contentsOfDirectory) and counts names with a `.tmp` suffix. Both actual temp-file schemes live one level deeper inside album subdirectories: LibraryFileManager.moveToLibrary stages `<album>/<file>.<ext>.tmp` (LibraryFileManager.swift:24), and DownloadManager finalization stages hidden `<album>/.tmp.<file>` prefix files (MuseAmp/Backend/Downloads/DownloadManager+Persistence.swift:18-21). Neither is at the audio root, so AuditSnapshot.stagedTempFiles is structurally always 0 even when crash-leftover staging files exist — note the hidden `.tmp.` prefix files are also skipped by the rebuild scanner (.skipsHiddenFiles at LibraryScanner.swift:28), so they are never cleaned by rebuild either and the audit silently under-reports them forever.
findings.suggestion: Enumerate audioDirectory recursively (without skipping hidden files) and count both `hasSuffix(".tmp")` and `lastPathComponent.hasPrefix(".tmp.")` entries; optionally have the rebuild prune `.tmp.`-prefixed leftovers whose download job no longer exists.

findings.title: forceArtwork clears the whole artwork cache but can only restore embedded artwork
findings.detail: With forceArtwork=true (Settings 'Rebuild Database' passes it, SettingsViewController+Actions.swift:146), clearArtworkCache() deletes every cached artwork file up front (line 53-55), but the rebuild loop re-writes artwork only from inspection.embeddedArtwork (lines 96-104 and 136-138). Tracks whose artwork exists only as a network-fetched cache file — written by DownloadArtworkProcessor.cachedArtworkData when embedding into the audio file failed or timed out (embed failures are swallowed as warnings in prepareDownloadedTrack) — lose their artwork permanently; the rebuild cannot refetch it and later non-force rebuilds skip unchanged files entirely (lines 92-106). Recovery requires the user to notice and run the manual per-track TrackArtworkRepairService action, which needs network. Additionally, if the rebuild aborts mid-loop (see the line 88 finding), even embedded artwork for not-yet-visited files stays deleted until a full successful re-run.
findings.suggestion: Do not bulk-clear up front. Instead, for each track during the scan, overwrite the cache from embedded artwork when present, and only delete a cache file after confirming a replacement was extracted (or record trackIDs that had cache-only artwork and refetch them via the existing artwork URL pipeline).

result.reasoning: Verified: SubsonicMusicService.swift (740 lines) ends with a private SubsonicPingPayload (line 689) and an internal KeyedDecodingContainer extension (lines 691-739) whose four lossy-decoding helpers have zero call sites in the service file itself — all ~30 usages are in Models/Subsonic/SubsonicSong.swift, SubsonicAlbum.swift, and SubsonicArtist.swift. All sibling payload wrappers live in Models/Subsonic/, and AGENTS.md neither mandates nor endorses burying Foundation-type extensions in service files (the app target's convention is dedicated Extension+ClassName.swift files, the opposite pattern). The suggested move to Models/Subsonic/KeyedDecodingContainer+LossyDecoding.swift fits package layout and violates no rule. Two downgrades: (1) the SubsonicPingPayload complaint is weak — it is private and its only consumer is ping() in the same file, so its placement is defensible; (2) "no way to discover the definitions" is overstated — jump-to-definition/grep finds them immediately and any misuse fails at compile time, so the issue cannot mislead about behavior or breed bugs. Real but cosmetic file organization, hence low rather than medium.

Confirmed at SubsonicMusicService.swift:320-336 — shouldFallbackToPlainAuth checks bare `code == 40 || code == 41`, which are Subsonic spec codes (40 = wrong username/password, 41 = token auth not supported) requiring spec lookup, unlike the adjacent self-evident 401/403 HTTP check. AGENTS.md does not endorse bare protocol numbers; its 'comments only where they remove real ambiguity' rule favors the fix, and the module already names protocol magic values (SubsonicEndpoint.apiVersion, clientName), so named constants would match local convention. Clarity harm is bounded — the function name and the token/salt/password keyword sniff make 'auth error' inferable, so this only costs a spec lookup when modifying the predicate (notably why code 40, wrong credentials, also triggers a plain-auth retry). Real but cosmetic: severity low.

Confirmed at SubsonicMusicService.swift:475 (enrichSongSearchResponse): the results buffer is seeded with `Array(repeating: indexed[0].element, count: indexed.count)`, replicating song #0 into every slot. Correctness depends on two non-local invariants — the non-empty guard at line 459 (else `indexed[0]` traps) and the guarantee that the task group yields every index (else the filler leaks as a duplicated first song). The suggested `var results = songs.data` is semantically equivalent today (each child task's error fallback is the original song at that index, i.e., exactly what songs.data holds), strictly safer under future modification, and removes both hidden invariants. Not endorsed by AGENTS.md, and not a module convention: this is the only Array(repeating:) placeholder seed in the repo; the three other withTaskGroup sites (PlaylistStore.swift:191, PlaybackController+Resolution.swift:68, PlaylistDetailViewController+Actions.swift:67) all collect into empty containers. No current bug exists (guard holds, all indices yielded), so severity stays low — cosmetic clarity with latent fragility.

Confirmed: mapSong (SubsonicMusicService.swift:604-605) hardcodes hasLyrics: true and hasTimeSyncedLyrics: false even though the decoded SubsonicSong struct contains no lyrics field of any kind — both values are fabricated. CatalogSongAttributes models both as Bool? defaulting to nil, so 'unknown' is the designed representation and the fix is just omitting the arguments. The module's own dominant convention contradicts the flagged pattern: the local-library adapter (MuseAmp/Backend/Library/AudioTrackRecord+AppModels.swift:35) maps hasLyrics honestly from hasEmbeddedLyrics and leaves hasTimeSyncedLyrics nil. AGENTS.md does not endorse the pattern; it names lyrics(id:) as the lyrics method, consistent with the suggestion. The only consumer anywhere is a fixture test (LiveFixtureTests.swift:274, '#expect(song.attributes.hasLyrics == true)') inside a test named 'song maps all fields from live response', which locks in the fabricated value under a name implying it was mapped from the response — strengthening rather than refuting the finding, though the fix requires updating that one assertion. Severity stays low: no app behavior reads either field today, so the dishonest values are latent rather than an active bug.

Verified in SubsonicMusicService.swift: line 77-79 uses the [serviceBaseURL = self.baseURL, weak self] dance while sibling album()/song()/playback() decode paths (lines 97, 110, 125) use plain strong self. baseURL is `public let` (line 11), so serviceBaseURL always equals self.baseURL; artworkTemplateURL is private and line 78 is the only caller passing baseURL:, making the `baseURL: URL? = nil` parameter (line 297) dead weight. The weak capture is also provably inert: the inner capture list evaluates self.baseURL, so the outer decode closure strongly retains self, and artworkBuilder runs synchronously inside perform() — self can never be nil at invocation. AGENTS.md only mandates [weak self] for Combine .sink closures touching UI, which does not apply here; the dominant local convention is strong capture, so the suggested fix increases consistency without violating any rule. The finding slightly overstates the nil-artwork risk (it cannot actually fire), but the core complaint — needless divergence plus a single-call-site dead parameter — is accurate and the fix is behavior-preserving. Severity is correctly low: cosmetic clarity, no behavioral impact.

The code exists as cited (SubsonicMusicService.swift:464-473 silently falls back to the thin search row on per-song enrichment failure), but the finding does not hold up. (1) The enrichment path is dead from the app's perspective: both APIClient callers (APIClient.swift:74, :95) and all five test call sites pass prefetchSongMetadata: false, so enrichSongSearchResponse is never invoked by any in-repo consumer — the claimed 'APIClient cannot observe degradation' scenario cannot occur because APIClient deliberately opts out of enrichment. (2) AGENTS.md's swallowed-error logging rule is explicitly scoped to app code via AppLog (MuseAmp/Backend/Logging/AppLog.swift), which the package cannot import; the dominant deliberate convention of SubsonicClientKit is uniform log-free best-effort degradation (silent staleFallback, silent token→plain auth fallback, try? URL/decode fallbacks) with zero diagnostics seams anywhere in the package. (3) The suggested init-injected error handler would be the package's only such seam, diverging from the SubsonicMusicService(baseURL:username:password:) init documented in AGENTS.md's Package Reference, adding API surface for a path nothing exercises — the fix would make the code read unlike the rest of the module. The fallback-to-search-row is the intended best-effort semantic of metadata prefetching, not an unhandled error condition.

Confirmed verbatim at SubsonicResponse.swift:30 — payload is decoded unconditionally after status/error. The claimed invariant is exactly right: SubsonicAlbumPayload/SubsonicSongPayload/SubsonicSearchResultPayload use optional fields and SubsonicLyricsPayload's custom init never throws, so failed envelopes only decode today by accident of payload shape. decodeSubsonicResponse (SubsonicMusicService.swift:245-271) checks wrapped.status only after decode returns and wraps any DecodingError as APIError.decodingFailed, so a required field in a future payload would mask the server's real error message — and additionally break shouldFallbackToPlainAuth (line 320), which depends on subsonicRequestFailed code 40/41 and message text to trigger the token→plain auth fallback. AGENTS.md does not endorse the pattern; its 'avoid unnecessary optionals' Property Rule actively steers maintainers toward writing the non-optional payload field that springs the trap. The suggested one-line guard matches the file's existing custom-init style and violates no repo convention. Medium severity: behavior is correct today (not high), but the invisible load-bearing invariant misleads readers and is primed to breed a hard-to-diagnose bug.

Confirmed at CatalogArtist.swift:10 — it declares only Decodable, Hashable, Sendable, while CatalogSong.swift:10 and CatalogAlbum.swift:10 (same author, same creation date, otherwise structurally identical: id+type+href+attributes with the same id+type ==/hash) both add Identifiable. The dominant module convention (2 of 3 sibling catalog resource models) is to conform, so the omission is the deviation and reads as an oversight; adding Identifiable is a zero-cost one-word change auto-satisfied by the existing `id: String`. AGENTS.md contains no rule for or against catalog-model Identifiable conformance (its only Identifiable mention, PlayerItem, conforms), so conventionEndorsed=false. The finding's claim that callers are 'forced to special-case artists' is hypothetical — no current usage (ResourceList containers, relationship fields, SubsonicMusicService mapping) is Identifiable-constrained — so impact is purely cosmetic consistency, keeping severity at low.

findings.title: updateSong rewrites playlist via non-atomic clear + per-entry add; mid-loop failure permanently loses entries
findings.detail: updateSong(in:at:with:) issues `.clearPlaylistEntries` (line 170) as one committed DB transaction, then re-inserts every song with a separate `.addPlaylistEntry` command per entry (lines 171-173). Each command is its own WCDB transaction (StateStore.savePlaylistEntries). If any add throws mid-loop (disk full, I/O error), the single catch at line 174 only logs and the function returns: the clear has already committed, so all songs from the failing index onward are permanently deleted from the database; if the first add fails, the entire playlist is emptied. refreshSongs (line 255) calls updateSong once per changed song during the user-facing 'Refresh' action, multiplying the number of full clear+re-add rewrites and the exposure window. Additionally, after such a partial failure, the remaining loop iterations in refreshSongs keep using indices captured from the pre-failure snapshot (line 226), so subsequent updateSong calls write merged metadata onto the wrong, shifted entries — silent cross-entry corruption.
findings.suggestion: Replace the clear+add loop with the already-transactional path: build the updated Playlist value and send `.importLegacyPlaylists([updatedPlaylist])` (StateStore wraps row replace + entry delete + inserts in one transaction), or add a dedicated `replacePlaylistEntries(playlistID:entries:)` command that performs delete+insert inside one transaction.

findings.title: createPlaylist is a destructive upsert: existing playlist with same UUID has all entries wiped
findings.detail: createPlaylist(id:name:coverImageData:) sends `.importLegacyPlaylists([candidate])` with candidate.entries = []. StateStore.importLegacyPlaylists (StateStore.swift:209-228) does insertOrReplace of the PlaylistRow and then DELETES every PlaylistEntryRow for that playlistID before inserting the (empty) candidate entries. So 'create' silently destroys any existing playlist with the same id, including its entries, createdAt and cover. The reachable trigger is ensureLikedSongsPlaylist (PlaylistStore+Support.swift:53-66): it decides existence purely from the in-memory `playlists` array. PlaylistStore.reload() (PlaylistStore.swift:344-350) swallows fetchPlaylists errors and leaves `playlists` stale/empty; if the fetch in init throws while writes still succeed, ensureLikedSongsPlaylist sees no Liked Songs playlist and calls createPlaylist(id: likedSongsPlaylistID) at PlaylistStore+Support.swift:60, which deletes every liked-songs entry in the database. The error path of a read is thereby converted into a destructive write keyed by the all-zero UUID.
findings.suggestion: Make createPlaylist non-destructive: check the database (databaseManager.fetchPlaylist(id:)) and return the existing playlist instead of importing over it, or extend the `.createPlaylist` LibraryCommand to accept id/cover and use StateStore.createPlaylist which uses plain insert (fails instead of replacing). At minimum, ensureLikedSongsPlaylist must verify absence against the DB, not the in-memory array.

findings.title: refreshSongs merge erases stored artworkURL when remote response omits artwork
findings.detail: In the merged PlaylistEntry built at lines 230-241, every other optional field falls back to the existing value (`albumID: refreshed.albumID ?? existing.albumID`, `albumTitle: ... ?? existing.albumTitle`, `durationMillis: ... ?? existing.durationMillis`, `trackNumber: ... ?? existing.trackNumber`), but line 237 uses `artworkURL: refreshed.artworkURL` with no fallback. refreshed.artworkURL is `catalogSong.attributes.artwork?.url` (line 204), which is nil whenever the server response lacks the artwork object. In that case merged.artworkURL becomes nil, `artworkChanged` (line 244) is true, and updateSong persists the entry with its artwork template erased — a transient missing field in one network response permanently deletes stored artwork data for the entry. Downstream, PlaylistDetailViewController+Artwork.artworkURL(for:) and PlaylistCoverArtworkCache.coverIdentity degrade for that song (cover grid identity falls back to album-id/song keys, row artwork disappears for non-downloaded tracks).
findings.suggestion: Use `artworkURL: refreshed.artworkURL ?? existing.artworkURL` to match the merge policy of the neighboring fields, or explicitly distinguish 'server returned no artwork object' from 'artwork intentionally removed'.

findings.title: mergeSongs creates duplicate trackIDs in Liked Songs, breaking toggleLiked/heart state
findings.detail: mergeSongs (lines 310-317) calls addSong for every source song with no containment check, and addSong (line 111) unconditionally appends (StateStore.addPlaylistEntry has no dedup). PlaylistContextMenuProvider.swift:95 builds merge targets as `playlists.filter { $0.id != playlist.id }`, which includes the Liked Songs playlist. Scenario: song X is already liked; user merges a playlist containing X into Liked Songs; Liked Songs now holds two entries for trackID X. Tapping unlike (heart button, lock-screen like command, album menu) runs toggleLiked (line 332): isLiked is true, removeSong(trackID:) (line 352) removes only the FIRST matching entry, returns .unliked — but the second entry remains, so isLiked(trackID:) is still true and PlaybackController.refreshSnapshot re-renders the heart as liked. The UI reports 'unliked' while the song stays in Liked Songs; the user must tap once per duplicate. This also contradicts the app's own no-duplicate intent expressed in PlaylistDetailViewController+Actions.availableTargetPlaylists (lines 127-131), which filters out playlists already containing the trackID.
findings.suggestion: Either skip songs whose trackID already exists in the target inside mergeSongs (and addSong for the Liked Songs playlist specifically), or make removeSong(trackID:from:) remove ALL entries matching the trackID so toggleLiked converges in one action.

findings.title: savePlaylistEntries inserts entry rows even when the parent PlaylistRow does not exist, creating invisible orphan rows
findings.detail: savePlaylistEntries (lines 294-313) inserts all entry rows (lines 300-303) BEFORE checking that the parent PlaylistRow exists; the guard at lines 304-309 merely `return`s from the transaction closure, which commits the already-inserted rows instead of rolling back. addPlaylistEntry (line 171) never validates the playlistID either. Reachable chain: AlbumDetailViewController.saveAlbumAsPlaylist (AlbumDetailViewController.swift:204-211) calls PlaylistStore.createPlaylist, which on a DB write failure logs and returns the unsaved candidate (PlaylistStore.swift:45 `playlist(for: id) ?? candidate`); the subsequent `entries.forEach { addSong($0, to: playlist.id) }` then inserts one orphan PlaylistEntryRow per track for a playlistID that has no PlaylistRow. fetchPlaylists only maps PlaylistRows, so these rows are invisible to the UI, are never cleaned (deletePlaylist only deletes by a known id; the audit unresolvedPlaylistEntryCount checks trackIDs, not orphaned playlistIDs), and silently inflate playlistEntryCount forever. The same hole applies to any add against a just-deleted playlist id.
findings.suggestion: In savePlaylistEntries, fetch/verify the parent PlaylistRow FIRST and throw (rolling back the transaction) when it is missing; alternatively add a periodic cleanup that deletes PlaylistEntryRows whose playlistID has no PlaylistRow.

findings.title: Playlist export/import round-trip silently drops persisted per-entry lyrics (and artworkURL)
findings.detail: SongReference (lines 20-37) serializes trackID/title/artist/album/duration/trackNumber but omits PlaylistEntry.lyrics and artworkURL, while the document does faithfully carry coverImageData. lyrics is real persisted user data: AlbumDetailViewController.fetchLyricsInBackground (AlbumDetailViewController.swift:230-243) fetches lyrics per track and persists them into playlist entries via PlaylistStore.updateLyrics after 'Save as Playlist'/add-to-playlist. On import, PlaylistTransferCoordinator (PlaylistTransferCoordinator.swift:59-65) rebuilds entries from AudioTrackRecord.playlistEntry, which sets neither lyrics nor artworkURL (AudioTrackRecord+AppModels.swift:13-23). Exporting a playlist and re-importing it — including on the same device as a backup/restore — silently loses all fetched lyrics, and entries lose their remote artwork template so artwork can only come from the local artwork cache or embedded metadata.
findings.suggestion: Add optional `lyrics` (and `artworkURL`) fields to SongReference, encode them on export, and on import prefer the document values when rebuilding entries (falling back to the local track record).

findings.title: Shuffled cover render is persisted under the canonical cache key without posting playlistArtworkDidUpdate
findings.detail: image(for:...) with shuffled: true skips cache reads (line 47) but still stores the randomized rendering into memoryCache and the disk file under the SAME canonical cacheKey (lines 88-89), while the `.playlistArtworkDidUpdate` notification is only posted for non-shuffled renders (lines 90-96). 'Regenerate Cover' (PlaylistDetailViewController+Menu.swift:167-193) invalidates all cached files for the playlist, then renders shuffled at sideLength 200 only. Result: (a) the shuffled image becomes the persisted canonical 200pt cover, but no notification fires, so already-rendered surfaces (sidebar via MainController+Sidebar playlistArtworkDidUpdate observer, playlist list cells) keep displaying the old image — persisted cache and visible UI silently disagree; (b) every other size (sidebar 28pt, 'Show Cover' 1200pt) re-renders NON-shuffled with a deterministic arrangement, so after regeneration the same playlist permanently shows two different cover arrangements at different sizes until the next mutation bumps updatedAt.
findings.suggestion: Either don't persist shuffled renders under the canonical key (use a distinct key or return without caching), or treat regeneration as a first-class state change: persist the shuffled arrangement (e.g., a stored seed/ordering), invalidate all sizes, and post .playlistArtworkDidUpdate so every surface re-renders consistently.

findings.title: Cover cache disk files keyed by updatedAt are never pruned, growing unboundedly
findings.detail: cacheKey (lines 248-251) embeds the playlist's updatedAt millisecond timestamp and song count, and savePlaylistEntries bumps updatedAt on every add/remove/move/lyrics change, so each mutation produces a brand-new key and a new PNG written at line 89 — per requested size (200pt detail, 28pt sidebar, list size). Files for superseded keys are never deleted: invalidateCache (lines 24-34) is the only pruning path and is invoked solely from the 'Regenerate Cover' action (PlaylistDetailViewController+Menu.swift:179) for one playlist. Deleted playlists never get their files removed at all. The directory under Caches therefore accumulates one orphan PNG per playlist mutation per rendered size indefinitely; the OS may purge Caches under pressure, but nothing in the app ever reclaims or bounds this storage. (invalidateCache also calls memoryCache.removeAllObjects(), discarding every other playlist's cached covers instead of only the target's.)
findings.suggestion: When writing a freshly rendered image, delete sibling files with the same `v3-<playlistID>-` prefix but a different timestamp; remove cached files in deletePlaylist flows; and in invalidateCache remove only the target playlist's memory keys instead of removeAllObjects().

result.reasoning: Confirmed at /Users/qaq/Documents/GitHub/MuseAmp/SubsonicClientKit/Sources/SubsonicClientKit/ResponseCache.swift:25 — the 7-day stale window is inlined as a default-parameter expression, and the only production caller (SubsonicMusicService.swift:442, staleFallback) relies on the default, so the policy is invisible at the call site. AGENTS.md does not endorse inline magic time values, and the module's own convention contradicts the pattern: the companion fresh-cache policy is a named property (`private let cacheTTL: TimeInterval = .infinity`, SubsonicMusicService.swift:18). Hoisting to a named constant matches local style. Severity stays low: `7 * 24 * 3600` labeled `maxAge` is readable as 7 days, so the issue is discoverability, not active confusion — though the buried constant does obscure that the parallel disk stale path (loadStaleDataFromDisk) applies no age bound at all. The finding's "single most important number in the package" framing is mildly overstated but the core claim survives adversarial review.

Verified in Artwork.swift lines 26-44: after literal {w}/{h} replacement, the function filters `size` query items whose decoded value is "{w}"/"{h}". This filter appears unreachable to a reader since the placeholders were just replaced, but URLComponents percent-decodes query values, so encoded `size=%7Bw%7D` (test fixture SubsonicClientKitTests.swift:255) bypasses the string replacement and only surfaces as "{w}" post-parse; the `.lowercased()` also silently handles case variants. No comment or named helper explains either subtlety — intent is documented only in the test file. AGENTS.md does not endorse the unexplained pattern; its comment rule ("only where they remove real ambiguity") explicitly permits the suggested one-line comment, and a small named helper matches repo style. The suggestion improves clarity without violating conventions. Severity stays low: a maintainer deleting the seemingly-dead filter would be caught by the dedicated unit test, so it is a genuine but cosmetic clarity gap rather than an active bug breeder.

Verified: the five inline literals exist at exactly the cited lines in SubsonicMusicService.swift, the app re-types them at all cited sites plus PlaylistDetailViewController+Actions.swift:242 and three test sites (~16 total across package, app, and tests), and all three Catalog models include `type` in their == and hash(into:) implementations. The models are never decoded from JSON, so `type` is a pure per-model invariant masquerading as free-form String data — every construction site, including app-side stubs (AlbumDetailViewController.swift:78/92/101, AudioTrackRecord+AppModels.swift:40), must re-type the package's magic string correctly or silently produce instances that fail identity comparison against mapper-produced ones. One consequence is overstated: current diffable item identifiers wrap id strings (AlbumItem.track(id:), SearchItem.song(String)), so today's snapshots would not break from a typo; the risk lives in the models' public Hashable contract rather than in any currently exercised dedup path. That tempers but does not refute the finding — the cross-module stringly-typed identity contract genuinely breeds bugs and misleads readers (a `type: String` property gives no hint of its closed value set), satisfying the medium rubric. AGENTS.md contains no endorsement of inline literals; repo conventions favor centralized constants (AppNotificationUserInfoKey, namespaced notification names), and the suggested per-model static constant is idiomatic, cheap, and reads consistently with the rest of the codebase.

Verified: loadAndPlay (MusicPlayer+Playback.swift:109-131) and continueWithCurrentEngineItem (170-192) share a literally identical ~22-line transition-publish tail (play/activate/setState, setTrack/updateRate, snapshot, updateQueueInfo, updateEnabledCommands, 4-arg updateLikeCommand, didTransitionTo+didChangeQueue). restorePlayback (MusicPlayer+Restoration.swift:50-90) repeats the same contract a third time with autoPlay branching; the 4-arg updateLikeCommand block additionally appears verbatim in configureLikeCommand and setCurrentItemLiked (MusicPlayer.swift:203-208, 220-225). AGENTS.md does not endorse the duplication — its conventions (shared helpers over per-file ad-hoc code, responsibility-based MusicPlayer+Xxx.swift extensions) favor the suggested internal publishTransition helper, which would read like the rest of the module. The finding overstates two points, justifying a downgrade from high to medium: (1) the three copies are not all identical — restorePlayback's setTrack-before-play ordering is plausibly deliberate (queue info must be set before the await-seek, play comes after seek), so the cited "drift" is not evidence of an existing bug; (2) no actual bug has been demonstrated, only the risk that a contract change (e.g. new media-center field) must be replicated across sites. That risk is real and breeds bugs (medium), but nothing is currently broken or blocked (not high).

findings.title: Restore trusts raw persisted index before track-ID match, shifting current track and losing playback position
findings.detail: restoredCurrentIndex() checks `resolvedItems.indices.contains(session.currentIndex)` FIRST and returns the raw persisted index, before the exact match on `session.currentTrackID` (line 28). resolvedItems is the persisted queue minus any tracks that failed local resolution (resolvePlayableItems drops tracks whose audio file no longer exists, PlaybackController+Resolution.swift:80-86). Concrete sequence: a 10-track queue is persisted with currentIndex=5/currentTrackID=T5; the user deletes the downloaded file for the track at position 2 (e.g. via Songs delete while the queue is empty, or it lingers in history per the removeTracksFromQueue gap); on next launch resolvedItems has 9 entries with originalIndex [0,1,3,4,5,...], `indices.contains(5)` is true, so restoredIndex=5 which is resolvedItems[5] = the track originally at index 6 — NOT T5, even though T5 is present at resolved position 4. Then PlaybackController.swift:519 computes `restoredCurrentTime = restoredCurrentTrack.id == session.currentTrackID ? session.currentTime : 0` → 0. Net effect: restore silently switches the current track to the wrong song and discards the saved in-track position; refreshSnapshot(persistState: true) at PlaybackController.swift:536 then re-persists the corrupted state. The exact-match branch that would have recovered correctly is unreachable whenever the stale index happens to stay in range.
findings.suggestion: Reorder the checks: try `resolvedItems.firstIndex(where: { $0.track.id == session.currentTrackID })` first; only fall back to the positional index if it also matches the persisted track ID (`resolvedItems[session.currentIndex].track.id == session.currentTrackID`), then nearest-playable. Alternatively match on originalIndex: `resolvedItems.firstIndex(where: { $0.originalIndex == session.currentIndex })`.

findings.title: Shuffled-mode removal of a pre-current permutation entry does not shift currentIndex, desyncing queue from engine and skipping a track
findings.detail: removeCanonicalIndex()'s shuffled branch (lines 435-443) rebuilds shufflePermutation via compactMap but, unlike the non-shuffled branch (line 445: `if canonicalIndex < ci { currentIndex = ci - 1 }`), never decrements currentIndex when the removed entry's permutation POSITION is before currentIndex (its bounds check `ci > shufflePermutation.count` only handles overflow, and is itself off-by-one — should be >=). Reachable scenario: shuffle is on; the user swipe-deletes the currently playing row in the NowPlaying queue list → PlaybackController.removeFromQueue(at:) (MuseAmp/Backend/Playback/PlaybackController.swift:334-336) calls player.next() (advance(): playedIndices gains old current, ci becomes k+1) then player.removeFromQueue(id: oldCurrent.id) → remove(id:) passes the not-currently-playing guard and removes the permutation entry at position k < ci. All entries after position k shift left, so the item the engine just started playing moves to position k while currentIndex stays k+1. Consequences: queue.nowPlaying points at the item AFTER the one actually audible (MusicPlayer.currentItem); QueueSnapshot.orderedItems omits the actually-playing item entirely; PlaybackController snapshot and persisted session record the wrong current track; when the audible track ends, handleItemEnd's advance() marks the never-played item at ci as played and jumps past it — that track is silently skipped.
findings.suggestion: In the shuffled branch of removeCanonicalIndex, capture the removed entry's position in shufflePermutation before compactMap (`let removedPos = shufflePermutation.firstIndex(of: canonicalIndex)`), and if `removedPos < ci` decrement currentIndex; also change the overflow guard to `ci >= shufflePermutation.count`.

findings.title: Persisted-session restore can stomp user-initiated playback started during its await window
findings.detail: restorePersistedPlaybackIfNeeded() sets didAttemptPersistedRestore, then performs long awaited work: the per-track restoredArtworkURL loop (which can call metadataReader.extractArtwork — full AVAsset metadata reads — for every restored track whose artwork cache is missing, lines 490-508) and resolvePlayableItems (line 509). After these suspensions it unconditionally overwrites queueState.currentSource/trackLookup (lines 521-522) and calls player.restorePlayback (line 524) with no re-check that the player is still idle. SceneDelegate.swift:74-83 presents the interactive MainController and only then launches this restore in a detached Task, so the user can tap a song while restore is resolving. Sequence: user taps song → play(tracks:) → player.startPlayback begins audible playback and sets queueState.trackLookup; the in-flight restore then resumes, replaces trackLookup with the stale persisted map, and MusicPlayer.restorePlayback (MuseAmpPlayerKit MusicPlayer+Restoration.swift:32-53) replaces playbackQueue and the engine's current item, then sets state .paused (allowAutoPlay is false on iOS). The song the user just started is killed mid-play and replaced by yesterday's queue, paused — and refreshSnapshot(persistState: true) re-persists the stale queue over the user's new one.
findings.suggestion: After the awaits and immediately before applying state (before line 521), guard that no playback was started concurrently: `guard player.queue.totalCount == 0, !player.state.isActive else { return false }` (or capture a generation counter incremented by play()/playNext()/addToQueue and abort if it changed).

findings.title: removeTracksFromQueue leaves deleted tracks in queue history and re-persists dead entries
findings.detail: removeTracksFromQueue is called by library deletion flows right after the audio files and DB records are destroyed (e.g. SongsViewController+Actions.swift:52-53 calls musicLibraryTrackRemovalService.removeTracks then removeTracksFromQueue). But the purge loop (lines 292-300) only iterates `player.queue.upcoming`, and the current-track branch (lines 302-313) just calls player.next() without removing the item — unlike removeFromQueue(at:) (lines 334-336) which does next() + removeFromQueue(id:). Result: (a) history entries matching the deleted trackIDs are never touched, and (b) the just-deleted current track moves into history and stays in the queue. These dead entries remain visible in the NowPlaying queue list; tapping one or pressing previous() loads a missing file, producing didFailItem and an automatic forward skip. Worse, the didChangeQueue persist writes the dead entries into PersistedPlaybackSession at positions before currentIndex, which on next launch fail resolution and trigger the restoredCurrentIndex shift (wrong restored current track, position reset to 0).
findings.suggestion: Also iterate `player.queue.history` and call player.removeFromQueue(id:) for matching entries, and in the current-track branch remove the current item after next() (mirroring removeFromQueue(at:)). Note: removing history entries in shuffle mode requires the PlaybackQueue.removeCanonicalIndex currentIndex-shift fix first.

findings.title: Stale pendingSeekSnapshotTime resurfaces an old seek target as currentTime on every later pause
findings.detail: seekState.pendingSeekSnapshotTime is set by seek(to:) (PlaybackController.swift:365), previous()-restart (line 263), and restartCurrentTrack (line 275), but is only ever cleared when the queue empties (PlaybackController+Snapshot.swift:23) or the current track changes (line 49) — never when the seek completes or playback progresses past it. resolvedSnapshotCurrentTime (lines 202-219) returns the pending value whenever state is .idle/.paused/.error. Concrete sequence: while a track plays, the user scrubs to 60s (pending=60), listens on to 200s (didUpdateTime only mutates latestSnapshot's time, pending survives), then taps pause → didChangeState(.paused) → refreshSnapshot full path with nil explicit time → resolvedSnapshotCurrentTime returns the stale 60 → the published snapshot reports currentTime=60 while the player is actually at 200. Consumers of snapshot.currentTime show the wrong position: popup mini-player progress (MainController+Popup.swift:210, TabBarController+Popup.swift:187) jumps back to 60, and opening NowPlaying while paused seeds ProgressTrackView/NowPlayingPlaybackTimeRowView via `.prepend((playbackController.snapshot.currentTime, ...))` with 60 and receives no further subject events while paused. The wrong value sticks for the entire pause. (Persisted time is unaffected since makePersistedSession reads player.currentTime directly — i.e. published state and persisted state disagree.)
findings.suggestion: Invalidate the pending value once it is no longer relevant: clear seekState.pendingSeekSnapshotTime in musicPlayer(_:didUpdateTime:) when a time update arrives (the engine is now reporting authoritative time), or clear it in the seek(to:) completion Task after the awaited player.seek returns.

findings.title: Shuffled session persistence loses the canonical order, so un-shuffling after restore cannot recover the original order
findings.detail: makePersistedSession persists only `queue.orderedItems` — the effective play order (history + nowPlaying + upcoming, which under shuffle is the shuffle permutation order) — and discards PlaybackQueue's canonical `items` array and shufflePermutation. On restore, PlaybackQueue.restore (MuseAmpPlayerKit/Sources/MuseAmpPlayerKit/Queue/PlaybackQueue.swift:103) sets `shufflePermutation = Array(0..<items.count)` (identity) over the persisted play order. Consequence: before app restart, toggling shuffle off (setShuffle(false), PlaybackQueue.swift:393-404) restores the original album/playlist order from the canonical array; after a restart of a shuffled session, the canonical array IS the shuffled order, so toggling shuffle off silently keeps the shuffled order. Same user action produces different queue order depending on whether the app was relaunched in between — persisted state does not round-trip.
findings.suggestion: Persist the canonical item order plus the current permutation (or per-entry canonical indices) in PersistedPlaybackSession, and have restore rebuild items in canonical order with the saved permutation, instead of flattening to play order with an identity permutation.

result.reasoning: Verified verbatim: MusicPlayer+Playback.swift lines 145-165 (continueWithCurrentEngineItem) and MusicPlayer+Observation.swift lines 91-113 (observeItemStatus) each inline the same fallback NSError(domain: "MuseAmpPlayerKit", code: -1, userInfo: [NSLocalizedDescriptionKey: String(localized: "Unknown playback error", bundle: .module)]) and the same recovery policy (didFailItem -> playbackQueue.advance() -> loadAndPlay(.itemFailed) or musicPlayerDidReachEndOfQueue + stop()). Grep confirms these are the only two occurrences of the domain string and localized fallback in the package, with no existing shared helper. The only divergence between the copies is logging (the preloaded path never logs the error at .error level while the KVO path does), which is incidental drift rather than deliberate policy difference — exactly the risk the finding describes. AGENTS.md (identical to project CLAUDE.md) contains no endorsement of inlined duplicated error handling; it actually favors shared helpers over per-file ad-hoc duplication. The suggested internal handleItemFailure helper matches the module's existing style (loadAndPlay, preloadNextItem, continueWithCurrentEngineItem are already internal cross-file helpers in MusicPlayer extensions), so the fix would read like the rest of the repo. Severity stays medium: the duplicated block is the playback engine's failure-recovery policy, where one-sided edits (retry logic, failure-loop fixes, end-of-queue changes) would silently diverge between the gapless/preloaded path and the KVO path.

Verified: rebuildShufflePermutationIndices (PlaybackQueue.swift lines 482-488) has zero call sites repo-wide (grep finds only the definition; it is private/file-scoped and mutating on a struct, so no dynamic dispatch is possible).
